meta.description: Combined MVP graph — two First Amendment free speech slices. Commercial speech data updated to v0.2 schema (holding_type on cases, overrule_type on OVERRULES edges, direction on MODIFIES edges, APPLIES+MODIFIES deduplication). Core political speech slice is native v0.2.
fixes_applied: FIX 1: ConstitutionalProvision labels changed to citation form (amendment_1, amendment_14_section_1) to disambiguate from Area nodes with same common-name labels
FIX 2: CHILD_OF edges added for individual_rights→constitutional_law and first_amendment→individual_rights — parent_id existed on nodes but edges were absent from edge list
meta.cleanup_pass: 2026-03-15: label disambiguation (› suffix on Area nodes), Case node short_name normalization, commercial_speech_no_protection connected to overruling chain and historical GOVERNED_BY, doctrine area_id fields, ConstitutionalProvision status fields

doctrines.label: Commercial Speech Doctrine
doctrines.description: Commercial speech occupies a subordinate position in the First Amendment hierarchy. It receives protection only if it concerns lawful activity and is not misleading or fraudulent. Government regulation of commercial speech is evaluated under the four-part Central Hudson test. Fully protected (non-commercial) speech receives stricter scrutiny than commercial speech.
elements: Speech must propose a commercial transaction or relate solely to economic interests
Speech must concern lawful activity
Speech must not be misleading or fraudulent
Government regulation must satisfy the Central Hudson four-part test
doctrines.notes: The boundary between commercial and non-commercial speech is contested. Sorrell v. IMS Health (2011) raised questions about whether content- or speaker-based restrictions on commercial speech might trigger higher scrutiny.

doctrines.label: Commercial Speech — No Protection (Pre-1976)
doctrines.description: Prior to Virginia Pharmacy Board (1976), purely commercial speech received no First Amendment protection whatsoever. The government could regulate or prohibit commercial advertising without constitutional constraint.
elements: Speech proposing a commercial transaction receives no First Amendment protection
Government may freely regulate commercial advertising
doctrines.notes: Established by Valentine v. Chrestensen (1942). Overruled by Virginia Pharmacy Board v. Virginia Consumer Council (1976).

doctrines.label: Core Political Speech Doctrine
doctrines.description: Speech on matters of public concern receives the highest First Amendment protection. Content-based restrictions on core political speech are presumptively unconstitutional and subject to strict scrutiny. The government bears an extremely heavy burden to justify any restriction. Protection extends even to speech that is offensive, provocative, or distressing to its audience, provided it does not fall within a narrow category of historically unprotected speech.
elements: Speech addresses a matter of public concern
Content-based restrictions are presumptively unconstitutional
Government bears burden of demonstrating restriction falls within a recognized unprotected category
Unprotected categories are narrow and historically grounded: incitement, true threats, fighting words, obscenity
doctrines.notes: The categorical approach — identifying historically unprotected categories rather than balancing speech value against harm — is the governing framework. United States v. Stevens (2010) reaffirmed that the Court will not recognize new unprotected categories based on ad hoc cost-benefit analysis.

doctrines.label: Incitement Doctrine
doctrines.description: Speech that incites imminent lawless action is categorically excluded from First Amendment protection. The modern standard, established in Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969), requires that the speech be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action AND be likely to produce such action. Abstract advocacy of illegal conduct, even forceful advocacy, is protected. Only speech intended to and likely to cause immediate unlawful action falls outside the First Amendment.
elements: Speech must be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action
Speech must be likely to produce such imminent lawless action
Abstract advocacy of illegal conduct is protected
Both intent and likelihood of imminent harm are required
doctrines.notes: Pre-Brandenburg standards: Schenck (1919) clear-and-present-danger test; Gitlow (1925) bad-tendency test; Dennis (1951) gravity-times-probability reformulation. All superseded by Brandenburg. The intellectual foundation was Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927), both of which required imminence — a requirement Brandenburg elevated to majority doctrine 40+ years later. SCHEMA ISSUE CANDIDATE: The doctrinal source of the Brandenburg test traces to non-majority opinions in earlier cases, not to any ESTABLISHES edge from a prior majority holding. The schema has no way to represent a dissent or concurrence as a doctrinal source.

doctrines.label: True Threats Doctrine
doctrines.description: True threats — statements where the speaker means to communicate a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence against a particular person or group — are categorically excluded from First Amendment protection. The government need not prove the speaker intended to carry out the threat. Post-Counterman (2023), the government must prove at minimum that the defendant was subjectively aware that their communications could be perceived as threatening, rejecting a purely objective reasonable-person standard.
elements: Statement communicates a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence
Directed at a particular individual or group
Speaker must at minimum consciously disregard a substantial risk that the statement will be understood as a threat (recklessness standard under Counterman v. Colorado, 2023)
Government need not prove intent to carry out the threat
doctrines.notes: The subjective awareness standard from Counterman (2023) is a significant doctrinal development. Prior to Counterman, most circuits applied an objective reasonable-person standard — whether a reasonable person would interpret the statement as a threat — without requiring proof of the speaker's mental state. Counterman requires at minimum recklessness: the speaker consciously disregarded a substantial risk that their communications would be perceived as threatening. Justice Barrett's majority left open whether intent (purposeful) or knowledge suffices in addition to recklessness. Active circuit split developing on application. SCHEMA ISSUE CANDIDATE: valid_from here reflects Counterman's refinement of the mens rea requirement, but the true threats category predates Counterman significantly (Virginia v. Black, 2003; R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul, 1992). The doctrine node has a single valid_from that cannot represent both the category's origin and the mens rea element's refinement.

doctrines.label: Public Concern Doctrine
doctrines.description: Whether speech addresses a matter of public concern is determined by its content, form, and context. In many First Amendment settings, speech on matters of public concern receives stronger constitutional protection or qualifies for constitutional scrutiny that would otherwise be unavailable. The doctrine operates differently across contexts, including public employee speech, tort liability, and related First Amendment frameworks.
elements: Content: does the speech address political, social, or community affairs
Form: how was the speech expressed and where
Context: the circumstances surrounding the speech
Public concern determination is a question of law, not fact
doctrines.notes: Valid_from reflects Connick v. Myers (1983), which first articulated the public concern test in the public employee speech context. Snyder v. Phelps (2011) extended and clarified the doctrine in the private plaintiff tort context. The doctrine operates differently in different contexts: public employee speech (Connick/Garcetti line), private plaintiff IIED claims (Snyder), and content-neutral time/place/manner restrictions. SCHEMA ISSUE CANDIDATE: This doctrine originates in a case (Connick) outside the current slice scope. The source_case_id pattern on DoctrinalTest assumes a single originating case. The public concern doctrine accumulated across multiple cases in multiple contexts, none of which is cleanly 'the' source.

doctrines.label: Prior Restraint Doctrine
doctrines.description: Any government action that suppresses speech before it occurs bears a heavy presumption of unconstitutionality. The government carries an extremely heavy burden to justify a prior restraint. Even where subsequent punishment would be constitutionally permissible, a prior restraint on the same speech may not be. The doctrine reflects the view, rooted in English common law, that freedom of the press means freedom from prior restraint above all else.
elements: Any prior restraint on speech or press comes with a heavy presumption against constitutional validity
The government bears a heavy burden to justify any prior restraint
The burden is heavier than for subsequent punishment of the same speech
Narrow exceptions exist: obscenity, incitement to violence, national security (troop movements), and content-neutral time/place/manner injunctions
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE D: The fourth element — narrow exceptions — has internal structure that the schema cannot represent. The national security exception has its own criteria (not merely speculation of harm, but direct and immediate danger to national security). The obscenity exception connects to the Miller test. These exceptions are not simple prongs; they are sub-doctrines with their own governing tests. A structured exception schema — analogous to prongs on DoctrinalTest — would be needed to model this accurately.

doctrines.label: Speech Injunction Doctrine
doctrines.description: Court injunctions restricting speech are subject to a distinct constitutional standard from classic prior restraints. Under Madsen v. Women's Health Center (1994), content-neutral injunctions — those not targeted at the message but at the conduct of specific parties — are evaluated under an intermediate standard: whether the injunction burdens no more speech than necessary to serve significant governmental interests. This is more permissive than the near-absolute rule against classic prior restraints but more demanding than the standard time/place/manner test.
elements: Injunction must be content-neutral (aimed at conduct, not message)
Must serve significant governmental interests
Must burden no more speech than necessary to serve those interests
Applies to specific parties before the court, not the public generally
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE E: The relationship between prior_restraint_doctrine and injunction_speech_doctrine is conditional on the instrument type — classic prior restraint triggers the Near/Pentagon Papers standard; content-neutral injunctions trigger the Madsen standard. The schema has no conditional governance edge. GOVERNED_BY edges are binary; they cannot express 'governs when instrument type = injunction.' This is a significant gap for any doctrine with instrument-type-conditional analysis.

doctrines.label: Actual Malice Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment prohibits a public official or public figure from recovering damages for defamation unless the plaintiff proves the defendant made the statement with actual malice — that is, with knowledge that it was false or with reckless disregard of whether it was false or not. The actual malice standard is a constitutional floor that preempts state defamation law when the plaintiff is a public official or public figure. It applies to both compensatory and punitive damages. The standard is subjective — it asks what the defendant actually knew or believed, not what a reasonable person would have known.
elements: Plaintiff must be a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure
Statement must concern the plaintiff's public role or the matter that made them a public figure
Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that defendant knew the statement was false, OR defendant acted with reckless disregard for the truth or falsity of the statement
Reckless disregard is subjective: defendant must have entertained serious doubts about the truth of the statement
Actual malice standard applies to both liability and to punitive and presumed damages
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A: The actual malice standard applies differently based on plaintiff category — public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, private figure. This four-category taxonomy is central to the doctrine's application but the schema has no way to represent it structurally. It functions like a sub-test within the doctrine but doesn't fit the DoctrinalTest model cleanly because it's a classification framework, not a sequential multi-prong test. Candidate for promotion to a first-class node type or a structured sub-schema.

doctrines.label: Private Figure Defamation Doctrine
doctrines.description: When a private figure plaintiff sues a media defendant for defamation on a matter of public concern, the First Amendment requires at minimum that the plaintiff prove the defendant was at fault — negligence or higher — and limits recovery to actual damages unless the plaintiff proves actual malice. States may set the standard above negligence but may not impose strict liability. When the matter is not of public concern, the constitutional floor is lower and states have more latitude. This doctrine creates a federal constitutional minimum above which state defamation law operates.
elements: For matters of public concern: plaintiff must prove at minimum that defendant was at fault (negligence standard)
States may impose a higher standard (actual malice) but may not impose strict liability
Recovery limited to actual damages unless plaintiff proves actual malice
For matters not of public concern: states have greater latitude; constitutional floor is lower
Presumed and punitive damages require proof of actual malice regardless of plaintiff's status
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE E: This doctrine creates a federal constitutional floor with state law operating above it. The schema is scoped to federal constitutional law, but this doctrine is meaningless without reference to the state law it constrains. Gertz explicitly declined to impose the actual malice standard on private figure plaintiffs and left the standard to the states above the constitutional minimum. The graph needs a way to represent a doctrine that sets a floor for state law without fully occupying the field. This is a Phase 2 boundary condition — it requires the statute/state law layer to model completely.

doctrines.label: Opinion-Fact Distinction Doctrine
doctrines.description: Statements of pure opinion on matters of public concern that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts are protected by the First Amendment. The protection is not grounded in a categorical 'opinion privilege' — Milkovich (1990) explicitly rejected such a privilege — but in the falsity requirement: a statement of opinion that cannot be proven true or false cannot satisfy the falsity element of defamation. The First Amendment also protects rhetorical hyperbole and statements that no reasonable person would interpret as stating facts. Context is critical: the same statement may be actionable in a news article but protected as opinion in an editorial or letter to the editor.
elements: Pure opinion on matters of public concern that cannot be proven false is protected
No categorical 'opinion privilege' exists — Milkovich rejected such a privilege
Statements that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts are protected
Rhetorical hyperbole and satire are protected when context makes clear they are not statements of fact
Context determines whether a reasonable reader would interpret a statement as fact or opinion
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE C: The opinion-fact distinction is a multi-factor standard without clean numbered prongs. Milkovich identified relevant factors (whether statement is objectively verifiable, whether the context signals opinion, whether the statement is capable of being proven true or false) but did not enumerate a formal test. The Doctrine vs. DoctrinalTest boundary question: is this a doctrine with contextual factors, or a test without clean prong structure? Currently modeled as Doctrine. If the research tool needs to display the factors as a structured checklist, it should be promoted to DoctrinalTest with a contested classification note.

doctrines.label: Reporter's Privilege Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment does not protect journalists from being compelled to testify before grand juries about their sources or newsgathering activities. Branzburg v. Hayes (1972) rejected a constitutional reporter's privilege, with Powell's concurrence leaving open the possibility of a more limited privilege in civil cases or before legislative committees. Most reporter's privilege protection in the United States comes from state shield laws and the federal common law, not from constitutional doctrine. This doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Phase 2 statute layer development.
elements: No absolute First Amendment privilege to refuse grand jury testimony about sources
Powell concurrence suggests possible balancing test in civil cases or legislative subpoenas
Constitutional protection, if any, is limited and context-dependent
Primary protection comes from state shield laws and federal common law, not the Constitution

pending_establishment.note: Branzburg is the foundational case for reporter's privilege doctrine. Modeled as stub here pending full reporters_privilege sub-slice which requires statute layer (Phase 2) to model completely.

doctrines.notes: TRANSITIONAL: This doctrine is modeled as a stub. Full modeling requires the Phase 2 statute layer because most reporter's privilege protection is statutory (state shield laws, Privacy Protection Act). The constitutional dimension (Branzburg line) can be modeled in isolation but is misleading without the statutory context. Defer full modeling until Phase 2.

doctrines.label: Free Exercise Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment protects religious belief absolutely and protects religiously motivated conduct subject to constitutional limits. Before Smith, the Court sometimes applied a compelling-interest framework, especially in unemployment-benefit and related exemption contexts. Smith rejected any general constitutional requirement of religious exemptions from neutral, generally applicable laws, while Lukumi and Fulton preserved and elaborated the exceptions for laws that are not neutral or not generally applicable.
elements: Religious belief is absolutely protected — government may never punish or compel belief
Religious conduct receives substantial but not absolute protection
Neutral, generally applicable laws that incidentally burden religious conduct do not require compelling interest justification (Smith rule)
Laws that are not neutral or not generally applicable trigger strict scrutiny regardless of Smith
Government may not selectively impose burdens on religious conduct that it does not impose on comparable secular conduct
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A: The belief/conduct distinction functions as a threshold classification framework within Free Exercise doctrine — identical structurally to the public figure taxonomy in press freedom doctrine (Issue #19). The schema has no way to represent this as a structured pre-test classification. The distinction is not a prong of the Free Exercise test but a gateway that determines whether the test applies at all. Proposed fix: same as Issue #19 — PRECONDITION_TO edge type or structured sub-schema for threshold classifications. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The Smith rule is formally good law but the current Court has signaled in Fulton (2021) and in individual writings by at least four justices that Smith may be reconsidered. The status: disputed flag captures this but does not distinguish between (a) circuit split on application, (b) academic dispute, and (c) intra-Court challenge to a precedent by sitting justices. These are meaningfully different kinds of dispute for a research tool. Proposed fix: add a dispute_source enum to the status field: circuit_split | academic_consensus | intra_court | legislative_response.

doctrines.label: Belief-Conduct Distinction
doctrines.description: The foundational Free Exercise distinction between religious belief and religiously motivated conduct. Belief is absolutely protected: the government may never punish a person for holding a religious belief, compel a person to profess a belief, or penalize a person for changing their religion. Conduct, even when religiously motivated, is subject to government regulation — the degree of protection depends on whether the law is neutral and generally applicable (Smith) or not (Lukumi). The distinction was first articulated in Reynolds v. United States (1879) and incorporated into the modern constitutional framework by Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940).
elements: Government may never punish religious belief as such
Government may never compel profession or abandonment of religious belief
Government may never penalize a person for changing their religion
Religiously motivated conduct is regulable subject to neutrality and general applicability requirements
The belief/conduct line is determined by whether the government is targeting the belief itself or regulating conduct that happens to be religiously motivated

pending_establishment.note: Reynolds first articulated the belief/conduct distinction but predates incorporation and the modern Free Exercise framework. Treated as historical background. Full case node deferred.

doctrines.notes: The belief/conduct distinction is not contested — all justices across all eras agree on it. The dispute is entirely about how much protection conduct receives once the belief/conduct threshold is crossed.

doctrines.label: Neutrality and General Applicability Doctrine
doctrines.description: Under Employment Division v. Smith (1990) and Church of Lukumi (1993), the threshold question in any Free Exercise challenge to a law is whether the law is both neutral (not targeted at religious practice) and generally applicable (not riddled with exceptions for comparable secular conduct). If a law fails either requirement, strict scrutiny applies regardless of Smith's general rule. A law is not neutral if its object is to infringe upon or restrict religious practice. A law is not generally applicable if it exempts comparable secular conduct from its prohibitions while refusing religious exemptions, or if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allow government discretion to grant or deny exemptions based on the nature of the reason for the exemption request.
elements: A law must be neutral: its object must not be to target or restrict religious practice
A law must be generally applicable: it must not selectively burden religious conduct while exempting comparable secular conduct
A law is not generally applicable if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allow for discretionary secular exemptions but not religious ones (Fulton rule)
Lack of neutrality or general applicability triggers strict scrutiny regardless of Smith's general rule
The neutrality and general applicability inquiries are distinct: a law may fail either or both
doctrines.notes: valid_from reflects Lukumi (1993), which gave the neutrality/general applicability framework its definitive statement. The framework is implicit in Smith (1990) but not fully articulated there. Fulton (2021) significantly expanded the general applicability inquiry — a categorical exception in a law's own terms that allows for discretionary secular exemptions makes the law not generally applicable. SCHEMA ISSUE D: The determination of whether a law is neutral and generally applicable is a totality-of-circumstances analysis drawing on multiple factors without clean numbered prongs. Same structural problem as the opinion-fact distinction (Issue #23). Currently modeled as Doctrine. test_form: totality_of_circumstances would be the appropriate classification if promoted to DoctrinalTest.

doctrines.label: Establishment Clause Doctrine
doctrines.description: The Establishment Clause prohibits government from establishing an official religion, preferring one religion over others, or preferring religion over non-religion. These core prohibitions are not contested. What is contested is how to identify violations in practice — what test or framework a court applies to determine whether a government action crosses the constitutional line. The Lemon test (1971-2022) provided a structured three-part framework. Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) replaced it with a historical practices and understandings approach. The coercion test (Lee v. Weisman, 1992) remains as an alternative analytical framework, particularly in the school prayer context.
elements: Government may not establish an official religion
Government may not prefer one religion over others
Government may not prefer religion over non-religion (or non-religion over religion)
Government may not coerce religious participation or observance
Post-Kennedy, courts are directed to consult historical practices and understandings in evaluating many Establishment Clause questions, but the operational content of that method remains unsettled.
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE C: The post-Kennedy status of this doctrine is a distinct state from standard 'disputed' doctrine. The Lemon test is gone; the historical practices standard is stated but not operationalized; lower courts are producing inconsistent results. This is 'post-overruling transition' — a state where the old framework is abolished and the new framework is stated but has no settled application. Proposed fix: dispute_source: post_overruling_transition (from Issue #24) captures this, but the doctrine node itself needs a transition_status field to document that the governing framework changed in a specific year and the replacement is still being operationalized.

doctrines.label: No-Establishment Core Doctrine
doctrines.description: The bedrock prohibitions of the Establishment Clause that are not contested regardless of which analytical framework governs: government may not mandate religious observance, sponsor official prayer, require profession of religious belief, or coerce religious practice through legal sanctions. These prohibitions were settled before Lemon and survive Kennedy — they represent the uncontested core of Establishment Clause doctrine around which the contested framework question revolves.
elements: No mandatory state religion or official church
No government-mandated religious observance or prayer
No religious test for public office or civic participation
No legal sanctions for religious non-conformity
doctrines.notes: These prohibitions represent the historical core of Establishment Clause doctrine — the practices the Clause was enacted to prevent. They are not dependent on the Lemon test or the historical practices standard and survive the Kennedy transition intact.

doctrines.label: Historical Practices and Understandings Standard
doctrines.description: The post-Kennedy interpretive standard for Establishment Clause analysis. Courts must interpret the Establishment Clause by reference to historical practices and understandings — what was understood to be an establishment of religion at the founding and, where relevant, at Reconstruction. If a challenged practice is consistent with those historical understandings, it does not violate the Establishment Clause; if it represents a departure from historical practice in the direction of greater government entanglement with religion, it may. The standard deliberately resists formalization into prongs and treats history as the primary guide rather than a structured analytical test.
elements: Identify the challenged government practice
Examine historical practices and understandings at the founding (1791) and Reconstruction (1868)
Determine whether the challenged practice is consistent with or represents a departure from those historical understandings
Government practices with long historical pedigree are presumptively constitutional
Novel government-religion entanglements without historical precedent are suspect
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE B: The historical practices and understandings standard deliberately resists formalization as a structured test. Gorsuch's Kennedy majority explicitly declined to articulate prongs or a sequential analytical framework. The 'elements' listed above are a reconstruction of the analytical steps implied by the standard — they are not enumerated in Kennedy. This is an interpretive methodology, not a test. The schema has Doctrine and DoctrinalTest but no node type for 'interpretive methodology.' Modeled as Doctrine because it lacks clean numbered prongs, but this is an imperfect fit. The test_form field proposed in Issue #23 would add 'interpretive_methodology' as a test_form value. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The standard is stated but its operational content is actively being developed. Courts are disagreeing about which historical sources count, how much historical continuity is required, and how to handle novel contexts with no direct historical analogue.

doctrines.label: Expressive Association Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment protects the right to associate with others for purposes of engaging in activities protected by the First Amendment — speech, assembly, petition, and religious exercise. Government may not compel a group to accept members whose presence would significantly burden the group's expressive message. The doctrine balances the associational rights of the group against the state's interest in preventing discrimination. Groups with a clear expressive purpose and a message that would be burdened by compelled inclusion receive the strongest protection.
elements: The group engages in expressive activity protected by the First Amendment
Compelled inclusion of a member would significantly burden the group's expressive message
The government's interest in compelling inclusion must be weighed against the associational burden
The more expressive the group's purpose, the stronger the First Amendment protection
doctrines.notes: valid_from reflects Roberts v. United States Jaycees (1984), which articulated the expressive association doctrine in its modern form. NAACP v. Alabama (1958) established the broader freedom of association principle; Roberts gave it the expressive/intimate distinction.

doctrines.label: Compelled Membership Disclosure Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment protects the right to associate anonymously. Government compulsion of membership lists or donor disclosures burdens associational rights by exposing members to retaliation, harassment, or social pressure. Such compelled disclosure requirements are subject to exacting scrutiny: the government must demonstrate a substantial relationship between the disclosure requirement and a sufficiently important government interest, and the requirement must be narrowly tailored to that interest. The doctrine originates in NAACP v. Alabama (1958) and was recently reinforced in Americans for Prosperity Foundation v. Bonta (2021).
elements: Government requires disclosure of membership, donors, or associates
Disclosure would subject members to harassment, retaliation, or chill on association
Exacting scrutiny applies: substantial relationship between disclosure and sufficiently important government interest
Requirement must be narrowly tailored — not merely reasonably related — to the government's interest
The chilling effect on association is cognizable harm even without proof of specific retaliation
doctrines.notes: The compelled disclosure doctrine is a distinct sub-doctrine within freedom of association with its own test (exacting scrutiny) and its own case line. NAACP v. Alabama (1958) established the foundational principle; Americans for Prosperity (2021) clarified that exacting scrutiny requires narrow tailoring, not merely reasonable relationship. The doctrine connects to free_exercise doctrine — NAACP v. Alabama noted that disclosure could burden religious as well as political association.

doctrines.label: Intimate Association Doctrine
doctrines.description: The Constitution protects close personal relationships — family, marriage, intimate friendship — from undue government interference. Unlike expressive association, intimate association is grounded in substantive due process (14th Amendment) rather than the First Amendment. Government interference with intimate associations must satisfy heightened scrutiny. This doctrine is modeled as a stub pending the Due Process slice, where its full constitutional grounding can be properly represented.
elements: The relationship is a close personal relationship — family, marriage, intimate companionship
Government action directly interferes with the relationship
Heightened scrutiny applies proportionate to the intimacy and importance of the relationship

pending_establishment.note: Roberts articulated the intimate/expressive association distinction. Full modeling of intimate association deferred to the Due Process slice where its 14th Amendment grounding can be properly represented.

doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A (second instance): Intimate association is a doctrine within the freedom_of_association Area but is grounded in amendment_14_section_1, not amendment_1. The GROUNDED_IN edge from the Area to amendment_1 is accurate for expressive association but does not capture the intimate association doctrine's different constitutional basis. This is the clearest instance yet of the dual-grounding problem. Proposed fix: add GROUNDED_IN edges from individual Doctrine nodes to their specific constitutional provisions, not just from the Area node. The current schema allows GROUNDED_IN from Area or Doctrine to ConstitutionalProvision — use the Doctrine-level edge here.

doctrines.label: Petition Clause Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment protects the right of individuals and groups to communicate with government officials and bodies — through petitions, letters, lawsuits, lobbying, and demonstrations — for the purpose of seeking governmental action or redress of grievances. Government may not retaliate against petitioners for the content of their petitions. The right extends to all branches and levels of government. Courts, administrative agencies, and legislative bodies are all government bodies subject to the Petition Clause.
elements: Communication directed at government officials or bodies
For the purpose of seeking governmental action or redress of grievances
Government may not retaliate against petitioners based on petition content
Extends to all branches and levels of government
Includes litigation, lobbying, demonstrations, and direct communications
doctrines.notes: The petition doctrine has rarely been applied independently of speech doctrine. Cases involving petition activity are usually decided on Speech Clause grounds, with the Petition Clause cited as additional support. The most distinctive petition doctrine applications are in the Noerr-Pennington antitrust immunity context and in First Amendment retaliation claims by public employees and contractors.

doctrines.label: Noerr-Pennington Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment right to petition the government protects concerted efforts to influence public officials and governmental processes, even when those efforts would otherwise violate the antitrust laws. The doctrine immunizes from antitrust liability: lobbying of legislative bodies, petitioning administrative agencies, and filing lawsuits — all of which are forms of petitioning the government. The immunity is not absolute: the 'sham exception' denies immunity when petitioning activity is objectively baseless and is a pretext for anticompetitive harm rather than a genuine effort to influence government.
elements: Defendant engaged in petitioning activity directed at government — lobbying, litigation, administrative proceedings
Petitioning activity is genuine and not objectively baseless
The sham exception: immunity is lost when petitioning is objectively baseless AND subjectively intended to use governmental process itself as an anticompetitive weapon
Immunity applies regardless of the petitioner's anticompetitive intent, provided the petitioning is genuine

pending_establishment.note: Noerr (1961) established the antitrust immunity for petitioning activity. Full case node deferred — the doctrine is modeled with its foundational case as pending_establishment.

doctrines.notes: The Noerr-Pennington doctrine is the most practically significant petition doctrine application. It regularly arises in antitrust litigation when defendants claim their lobbying or litigation activity is protected petitioning. The sham exception (Professional Real Estate Investors v. Columbia Pictures, 1993) limits the immunity. The doctrine sits at the intersection of constitutional law and antitrust — it is modeled here as a constitutional petition doctrine though it has significant antitrust dimensions that will be better represented when the statutory/regulatory layer (Phase 2) is built.

doctrines.label: Student Speech Doctrine
doctrines.description: Public school students retain First Amendment rights on school grounds, but those rights are qualified by the school setting. The government may restrict student speech only when it can demonstrate that the speech would substantially disrupt or materially interfere with school operations or the rights of other students. Mere discomfort, disagreement, or a desire to avoid controversy does not justify restriction. The doctrine has three established exceptions: lewd or vulgar speech (Bethel), school-sponsored speech (Hazelwood), and speech promoting illegal drug use (Morse). Outside those exceptions, Tinker's substantial-disruption test governs.
elements: Students do not shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate
School officials may restrict student speech only upon demonstrating substantial disruption or material interference with school operations
Undifferentiated fear or apprehension of disturbance is not enough
Viewpoint-based restrictions are particularly suspect even in the school context
Exception: lewd, vulgar, or plainly offensive speech may be restricted without disruption showing (Bethel)
Exception: school-sponsored speech (newspapers, plays, curricular activities) may be regulated for legitimate pedagogical concerns (Hazelwood)
Exception: speech promoting illegal drug use may be restricted (Morse)
doctrines.notes: The student speech doctrine is a specialized sub-doctrine within core political speech. The general rule (Tinker's substantial-disruption test) protects student expression robustly; the three exceptions carve out specific categories where the school's institutional interest overrides ordinary First Amendment analysis. The doctrine has not been revisited comprehensively by the Supreme Court since Morse (2007), leaving open questions about social media speech, off-campus speech, and the scope of the Hazelwood curricular exception.

doctrines.label: Fighting Words Doctrine
doctrines.description: A narrow category of speech — words that by their very utterance inflict injury or tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace — falls outside First Amendment protection. Established in Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire (1942). The doctrine has been substantially limited by subsequent decisions: the Court has never upheld a fighting words conviction since Chaplinsky, has required that fighting words statutes not be overbroad or content-discriminatory, and R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul (1992) held that even within the fighting words category, government may not discriminate based on viewpoint or content.
elements: Words that by their very utterance inflict injury on the listener
Words that tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace
Face-to-face communication directed at a specific individual
Content must not discriminate based on viewpoint even within the fighting words category (R.A.V.)
doctrines.notes: Despite its formal validity, the fighting words doctrine has been effectively moribund since Chaplinsky. The Court has consistently found that challenged statutes were either overbroad, vague, or content-discriminatory. R.A.V. added a viewpoint-neutrality requirement even within the category. As a practical matter, fighting words prosecutions rarely survive constitutional scrutiny.

doctrines.label: Symbolic Speech / Expressive Conduct Doctrine
doctrines.description: Conduct that is sufficiently communicative in character may receive First Amendment protection. Spence v. Washington (1974) articulated the classic formulation by asking whether the actor intended to convey a message and whether observers were likely to understand it. Later cases do not always insist on a tightly particularized message. If the conduct qualifies as expressive, regulation of its noncommunicative aspect is ordinarily analyzed under the O'Brien test when the government's interest is unrelated to suppressing expression.
elements: Conduct must be sufficiently communicative — intended to convey particularized message
Government interest must be within constitutional power
Government interest must be unrelated to suppression of free expression
Incidental restriction on expression must be no greater than essential to further the interest
doctrines.notes: The O'Brien test applies when government regulates conduct that has an expressive element for reasons unrelated to its communicative impact. When the government's purpose is to suppress the message, strict scrutiny applies regardless of whether the conduct is labeled 'speech' or 'conduct'. The distinction between content-based and content-neutral regulation maps onto whether O'Brien or strict scrutiny applies.

doctrines.label: Obscenity Doctrine
doctrines.description: Obscene speech falls entirely outside First Amendment protection. The governing definition of obscenity was established in Miller v. California (1973), replacing the earlier Memoirs v. Massachusetts standard. Obscenity is not protected regardless of whether it has any social value, because the state's interest in prohibiting such material is compelling and the category is defined by community standards rather than by content discrimination.
elements: Whether the average person, applying contemporary community standards, would find the work appeals to prurient interest
Whether the work depicts or describes, in a patently offensive way, sexual conduct specifically defined by applicable state law
Whether the work, taken as a whole, lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value (SLAPS test)
doctrines.notes: Miller replaced the Memoirs v. Massachusetts 'utterly without redeeming social value' standard with the three-part SLAPS test. Community standards apply to the first two prongs but not the third — serious value is judged by a national reasonable person standard (Pope v. Illinois, 1987). Child pornography is categorically unprotected even if not obscene under Miller (New York v. Ferber, 1982).

doctrines.label: Hate Speech and Content Discrimination Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment does not recognize a general hate speech exception. Government may not prohibit expression of an idea simply because society finds it disagreeable or offensive. Even within otherwise unprotected categories of speech (fighting words, true threats), government may not selectively prohibit speech based on the viewpoint expressed or the subject matter targeted. Established most clearly in R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul (1992).
elements: No general exception for hateful, offensive, or discriminatory speech
Government may not selectively target a subset of unprotected speech based on viewpoint
Content discrimination within an unprotected category is presumptively unconstitutional
Bias-motivated crime enhancement statutes targeting conduct (not speech) may be permissible (Wisconsin v. Mitchell)
doctrines.notes: R.A.V. is the foundational case establishing that even unprotected speech categories cannot be further restricted based on viewpoint. Wisconsin v. Mitchell (1993) distinguished R.A.V. by upholding sentence enhancement for bias-motivated crimes — targeting conduct rather than expression. Virginia v. Black (2003) upheld a cross-burning statute but required proof of intent to intimidate, distinguishing expressive cross burning from true threats.

doctrines.label: Campaign Finance and Political Expenditure Doctrine
doctrines.description: Political expenditures and contributions in connection with elections are protected First Amendment activity. Buckley v. Valeo (1976) established that spending money to disseminate political speech is itself constitutionally protected expression, while distinguishing between expenditure limits (which receive strict scrutiny) and contribution limits (which receive a more deferential standard). The doctrine has evolved significantly through Citizens United v. FEC (2010), which extended full First Amendment protection to corporate political expenditures.
elements: Campaign expenditures are constitutionally protected political speech
Expenditure limits receive strict scrutiny — only compelling government interest (quid pro quo corruption or its appearance) justifies restriction
Contribution limits receive more deferential review — sufficiently important interest and closely drawn means
Disclosure requirements may be upheld under exacting scrutiny
doctrines.notes: Buckley's expenditure/contribution distinction remains the foundational framework despite subsequent modifications. McConnell v. FEC (2003) upheld BCRA's soft money ban and electioneering communications restrictions. Citizens United v. FEC (2010) overruled the corporate expenditure ban, holding that political speech does not lose protection because its source is a corporation. McCutcheon v. FEC (2014) struck down aggregate contribution limits.

doctrines.label: Public Forum Doctrine
doctrines.description: Government regulation of speech in public forums — streets, parks, and other traditional gathering places — receives heightened scrutiny. The doctrine distinguishes traditional public forums (streets, parks), designated public forums (government property opened for expressive activity), and nonpublic forums (government property not opened for general expression). In traditional and designated public forums, content-based restrictions must satisfy strict scrutiny; content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions must be narrowly tailored to serve a significant government interest and leave open alternative channels.
elements: Traditional public forum: government property historically available for expression — strict scrutiny for content-based restrictions
Designated public forum: government property opened by policy for expressive activity — same strict scrutiny as traditional
Nonpublic forum: government property not opened for expression — only reasonable and viewpoint-neutral restrictions required
Content-neutral TPM restrictions in public forums must be narrowly tailored, serve significant interest, leave open alternative channels
doctrines.notes: Hague v. CIO (1939) established that streets and parks have historically been held in trust for use by the public for assembly and communication. Perry Education Association v. Perry Local Educators' Association (1983) formalized the three-category framework. The doctrine applies not just to physical spaces but to government-controlled communication channels.

doctrines.label: Reporter's Privilege / Press Access Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment does not confer on the press a general privilege to withhold information from grand juries, though journalists may have some constitutional protection against compelled disclosure in certain circumstances. Branzburg v. Hayes (1972) held 5-4 that reporters must respond to grand jury subpoenas. Justice Powell's concurrence suggested a case-by-case balancing, which many lower courts have used to recognize a qualified privilege. The press also has no special constitutional right of access to government information beyond that available to the general public, though the public and press have a First Amendment right of access to criminal trials.
elements: No absolute privilege against grand jury testimony — Branzburg
Qualified privilege may exist in civil cases and possibly criminal cases outside grand jury context
First Amendment right of access to criminal trials — Richmond Newspapers
No general constitutional right of access to government information
doctrines.notes: Branzburg is the foundational case but its holding is complicated by Powell's concurrence. Most circuits recognize a qualified reporter's privilege in non-grand jury contexts based on Powell's balancing approach. Many states have enacted shield laws providing statutory protection. The constitutional status of the privilege remains contested.

doctrines.label: Government Aid to Religious Institutions Doctrine
doctrines.description: Government aid programs that flow to religious institutions raise Establishment Clause concerns but are not categorically prohibited. The doctrine has evolved from strict separationism under Lemon toward greater permissiveness for neutral aid programs. Everson v. Board of Education (1947) upheld bus transportation aid to parochial school students as a neutral public benefit. Zelman v. Simmons-Harris (2002) upheld a school voucher program that included religious schools, applying a neutrality and private choice framework rather than the Lemon test.
elements: Neutrality — aid program must be available to religious and secular institutions on equal terms
Private choice — aid must flow to religious institutions through independent choices of private individuals
No direct government funding of religious instruction or proselytization
No excessive entanglement between government and religious institutions (Lemon prong 3, now largely absorbed)
doctrines.notes: The aid doctrine has shifted substantially toward permissiveness since the 1990s. Mueller v. Allen (1983) upheld a tuition tax deduction. Zelman (2002) is the current high-water mark of permissiveness — upholding direct voucher payments to religious schools under a neutrality/private choice framework. Post-Kennedy, the historical practices standard may further erode restrictions on government-religion financial relationships.

doctrines.label: Hostile Audience Doctrine
doctrines.description: When a speaker's expression provokes a hostile reaction from listeners, the First Amendment generally prohibits government from silencing the speaker to appease the crowd. The heckler's veto — allowing audiences to suppress speech by threatening or engaging in disorder — is constitutionally disfavored. Government must take reasonable steps to protect the speaker rather than silence the expression. However, when imminent violence is unavoidable and cannot be controlled, authorities may order the speaker to cease. The line between protecting speakers from hostile audiences and permitting genuine public safety orders is contested.
elements: Speaker's expression must not itself constitute fighting words or incitement
Government may not silence speaker merely to appease hostile audience — heckler's veto is disfavored
Government must take reasonable steps to protect speaker before ordering cessation
If imminent violence is unavoidable and uncontrollable, order to cease may be permissible
doctrines.notes: The hostile audience doctrine developed through Terminiello v. Chicago (1949) and Feiner v. New York (1951), which reached seemingly inconsistent results. Terminiello protected inflammatory speech that provoked audience disorder; Feiner permitted an order to desist when a crowd became dangerously hostile. The cases are distinguished on the grounds that Feiner involved imminent violence that police could not control, while Terminiello involved a speaker silenced simply for giving offense. The doctrine remains contested, particularly for campus speech and public events where hostile counter-protesters can effectively veto disfavored speakers.

doctrines.label: Government Speech Doctrine
doctrines.description: When the government itself speaks, it is not bound by the First Amendment's viewpoint-neutrality requirements. Government may express its own views, promote its own programs, and choose what messages to fund or disseminate without violating the First Amendment. The doctrine distinguishes between government regulating private speech (where First Amendment limits apply) and government engaging in its own expression (where they do not). The boundary between government speech and government-regulated private speech is contested, particularly in subsidy and specialty license plate cases.
elements: Government may express its own viewpoint without providing equal access to opposing views
Government may choose what private speech to subsidize without viewpoint-neutrality
Government speech must be genuinely governmental — not a vehicle for private viewpoints disguised as official expression
When government creates a forum for private speech, forum doctrine applies rather than government speech doctrine
doctrines.notes: Rust v. Sullivan (1991) is the foundational government speech case — upholding restrictions on federally-funded family planning clinics from counseling about abortion. The Court held that government may selectively fund a program without violating the First Amendment. Rosenberger v. Rector (1995) distinguished Rust by holding that when government creates a limited public forum for private speech, viewpoint-neutrality is required. Pleasant Grove City v. Summum (2009) and Walker v. Texas Division (2015) extended the doctrine to monument placement and specialty license plates.

doctrines.label: Broadcast Regulation Doctrine
doctrines.description: Broadcast media — radio and television — receive less First Amendment protection than print media because of spectrum scarcity. The government may impose content obligations on broadcasters, including the Fairness Doctrine (requiring balanced coverage of public issues) and equal time requirements, that would be unconstitutional if applied to newspapers. The scarcity rationale has been criticized as increasingly anachronistic in an era of cable, satellite, and internet media, but has not been formally abandoned by the Supreme Court.
elements: Broadcast spectrum is scarce — not everyone who wishes to broadcast can do so
Government may impose content obligations on licensees including fairness and equal time requirements
Scarcity rationale does not apply to cable or print media
Broadcaster's editorial discretion is more limited than newspaper's but not eliminated
doctrines.notes: Red Lion Broadcasting v. FCC (1969) upheld the Fairness Doctrine on scarcity grounds. Miami Herald v. Tornillo (1974) refused to extend broadcast-style obligations to print. Turner Broadcasting v. FCC (1994, 1997) applied intermediate scrutiny — not Red Lion deference — to cable must-carry requirements, treating cable as intermediate between broadcast and print. The FCC eliminated the Fairness Doctrine in 1987, but the constitutional framework from Red Lion remains nominally intact.

doctrines.label: Secondary Effects Doctrine
doctrines.description: Government may regulate adult businesses — bookstores, theaters, cabarets — based on their secondary effects on the surrounding community (increased crime, decreased property values) rather than the content of the expression involved. When government's purpose is to address secondary effects rather than to suppress the message, the regulation is treated as content-neutral and subject to O'Brien intermediate scrutiny rather than strict scrutiny. Established in Young v. American Mini Theatres (1976) and refined in City of Renton v. Playtime Theatres (1986).
elements: Regulation must be justified by secondary effects — crime, property values, neighborhood character — not by hostility to content
Government need not conduct its own study — may rely on evidence from other cities
Regulation must leave open reasonable alternative avenues for communication
Regulation may not reduce adult expression to a de minimis level
doctrines.notes: The secondary effects doctrine has been criticized as a loophole in content-neutrality doctrine — allowing government to cite secondary effects as a pretext for suppressing disfavored expression. City of Los Angeles v. Alameda Books (2002) limited the doctrine by requiring government to provide actual evidence that its regulatory scheme will address the secondary effects it identifies. Justice Kennedy's plurality in Alameda Books signaled skepticism about whether the doctrine should be expanded.

doctrines.label: Press and Public Access Doctrine
doctrines.description: The public and press have a First Amendment right of access to criminal trials and certain other judicial proceedings. Government may not close proceedings to the public and press without compelling, case-specific justification and narrow tailoring. The right of access is implicit in the First Amendment guarantees of free speech and press and has deep roots in Anglo-American tradition. Established in Richmond Newspapers v. Virginia (1980) and extended to jury selection and preliminary hearings in the Press-Enterprise cases.
elements: Public and press have a First Amendment right of access to criminal trials
Right of access may be overcome only by a compelling, case-specific government interest
Closure orders must be narrowly tailored and alternatives must be considered
Right extends to jury selection and certain preliminary hearings
doctrines.notes: Richmond Newspapers established the access right; Globe Newspaper Co. v. Superior Court (1982) applied it to require case-by-case rather than mandatory closure. The Press-Enterprise cases extended the right to jury selection (1984) and preliminary hearings (1986).

doctrines.label: Suspect Classification Doctrine
doctrines.description: Classifications based on race, national origin, and alienage are suspect and trigger strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause. A classification is suspect when it is based on an immutable characteristic, bears no relation to ability or contribution to society, and has historically been used to oppress a discrete and insular minority. The strict scrutiny standard requires the government to demonstrate that the classification is necessary to achieve a compelling governmental interest and is narrowly tailored to that end.
elements: Classification is based on race, national origin, or alienage
Government must demonstrate a compelling governmental interest
Classification must be narrowly tailored to achieve that interest
Burden of justification falls on the government
doctrines.notes: The suspect classification doctrine emerged from United States v. Carolene Products (1938) footnote four, which suggested that prejudice against discrete and insular minorities may call for a correspondingly more searching judicial inquiry. Korematsu v. United States (1944) first applied the strict scrutiny label to racial classifications, though it upheld the Japanese internment. Loving v. Virginia (1967) applied strict scrutiny to strike an anti-miscegenation statute.

doctrines.label: Quasi-Suspect Classification Doctrine
doctrines.description: Classifications based on sex and illegitimacy are quasi-suspect and trigger intermediate scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause. A classification is quasi-suspect when it is based on a characteristic related to but not solely determined by immutable traits and has been used to relegate a class to inferior legal status, but where the characteristic may in some instances be a permissible basis for differential treatment. The intermediate scrutiny standard requires the government to demonstrate that the classification is substantially related to an important governmental interest.
elements: Classification is based on sex or illegitimacy
Government must demonstrate an important governmental interest
Classification must be substantially related to achieving that interest
Burden of justification falls on the government
Exceedingly persuasive justification required for sex classifications (post-VMI)
doctrines.notes: Craig v. Boren (1976) formally established intermediate scrutiny as a distinct tier for sex classifications. Prior to Craig, Reed v. Reed (1971) and Frontiero v. Richardson (1973) suggested heightened review without settling on a standard. United States v. Virginia (1996) strengthened intermediate scrutiny for sex to require an exceedingly persuasive justification, effectively approaching strict scrutiny.

doctrines.label: Rational Basis Review
doctrines.description: All equal protection challenges not involving suspect or quasi-suspect classifications or fundamental rights receive rational basis review — the most deferential standard. Under rational basis, a law survives if it is rationally related to any legitimate governmental interest. Courts will hypothesize legitimate interests even if not articulated by the legislature. The challenger bears the burden of negating every conceivable rational basis. In some cases, the Court has applied rational basis with more bite, striking down laws motivated by bare desire to harm a politically unpopular group.
elements: Any legitimate governmental interest suffices — even one not articulated by the legislature
Law must be rationally related to that interest
Challenger bears burden of negating all conceivable rational bases
Courts defer to legislative judgments about means-ends fit
doctrines.notes: Rational basis is the default standard — it applies unless the challenger can invoke a suspect classification or fundamental right. A handful of cases have applied rational basis with more scrutiny ('rational basis with bite'), striking laws motivated by animus against an unpopular group: U.S. Dept. of Agriculture v. Moreno (1973), City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center (1985), Romer v. Evans (1996). The doctrinal status of rational basis with bite remains contested.

doctrines.label: Discriminatory Intent Doctrine
doctrines.description: A facially neutral law does not trigger strict scrutiny solely because it has a racially disparate impact. The Equal Protection Clause requires proof of discriminatory intent — a purpose to discriminate on the basis of race — before heightened scrutiny applies to a facially neutral law. Discriminatory intent may be shown through direct evidence, circumstantial evidence of the decision-making process, historical background, departures from normal procedures, or legislative history. A law enacted with discriminatory purpose and having a disparate racial impact is subject to strict scrutiny even if facially neutral.
elements: Facially neutral law with disparate racial impact does not alone trigger strict scrutiny
Plaintiff must demonstrate discriminatory purpose, not merely disparate impact
Purpose may be shown through direct evidence or Arlington Heights factors
Once discriminatory purpose shown, burden shifts to government to show same decision would have been made absent that purpose
doctrines.notes: Washington v. Davis (1976) established the discriminatory intent requirement for facially neutral laws. Village of Arlington Heights v. MHDC (1977) elaborated the multi-factor test for proving discriminatory intent from circumstantial evidence. The doctrine has been extensively criticized for making it nearly impossible to challenge facially neutral laws that perpetuate historic racial discrimination.

doctrines.label: Affirmative Action Doctrine
doctrines.description: Racial classifications designed to benefit historically disadvantaged minorities — affirmative action — are subject to strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause, the same standard applied to invidious racial discrimination. Strict scrutiny applies regardless of which race is burdened or benefited. Under Grutter v. Bollinger (2003), student body diversity was held to be a compelling interest sufficient to justify race-conscious university admissions. SFFA v. Harvard (2023) overruled Grutter, holding that race-conscious admissions programs at Harvard and UNC violated the Equal Protection Clause because they lacked measurable objectives, used race as a negative, and were not limited in time.
elements: Racial classification — even benign — triggers strict scrutiny
Government must demonstrate compelling interest (diversity, remedying specific documented discrimination)
Program must be narrowly tailored — race used only as one factor among many
No racial quotas or mechanical point systems
Program must be limited in time
Race-neutral alternatives must be considered first
doctrines.notes: Adarand Constructors v. Pena (1995) established that all racial classifications — including affirmative action — are subject to strict scrutiny at the federal level, overruling Metro Broadcasting v. FCC (1990). Grutter v. Bollinger (2003) upheld law school affirmative action. SFFA v. Harvard (2023) effectively ended race-conscious admissions, narrowing the doctrine significantly.

doctrines.label: Fundamental Rights — Equal Protection
doctrines.description: When a law burdens the exercise of a fundamental right on an unequal basis, it triggers strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause even if no suspect classification is involved. Fundamental rights triggering equal protection strict scrutiny include voting, interstate travel, and access to courts in criminal proceedings. This strand of equal protection doctrine — sometimes called equal protection/fundamental rights — is distinct from substantive due process protection of fundamental rights, though the two doctrines substantially overlap in cases involving voting and travel.
elements: Law distributes access to a fundamental right on an unequal basis
Fundamental rights include voting, interstate travel, access to criminal courts
Strict scrutiny applies regardless of whether a suspect classification is involved
Government must show compelling interest and narrow tailoring
doctrines.notes: The fundamental rights strand of equal protection emerged from Harper v. Virginia Board of Elections (1966) (voting) and Shapiro v. Thompson (1969) (travel). San Antonio ISD v. Rodriguez (1973) declined to extend the doctrine to education, holding that education is not a fundamental right under the Equal Protection Clause.

doctrines.label: Substantive Due Process
doctrines.description: The doctrine that the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protects substantive rights, including those not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution but deemed fundamental because they are deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition and essential to ordered liberty.
doctrines.notes: Dobbs clarifies that substantive due process protection requires that a right be deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition and essential to ordered liberty. The Court rejected the application of this doctrine to abortion.

doctrines.label: Stare Decisis
doctrines.description: The doctrine that courts should respect and follow precedent, though it is not an inexorable command and is weakest when interpreting the Constitution.
doctrines.notes: Dobbs reaffirms that stare decisis is not an inexorable command and is at its weakest in constitutional interpretation. The Court identified five factors for determining when precedent should be overruled: nature of error, quality of reasoning, workability, effect on other areas of law, and reliance interests.

doctrines.label: Rational Basis Review (Substantive Due Process)
doctrines.description: The standard of review applicable to state regulations that do not implicate fundamental rights or suspect classifications, requiring only that the regulation be rationally related to a legitimate state interest.
doctrines.notes: Dobbs holds that rational-basis review is the appropriate standard for state abortion regulations, as abortion is not a fundamental constitutional right.

doctrines.label: Substantive Due Process Protection of Marriage
doctrines.description: The Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause protects the fundamental right to marry as an aspect of liberty and personal autonomy, extending to personal choices central to individual dignity and autonomy, including intimate choices defining personal identity and beliefs.
doctrines.notes: Established in Obergefell; builds on precedents including Griswold v. Connecticut, Eisenstadt v. Baird, Loving v. Virginia, Turner v. Safley, and Lawrence v. Texas.

doctrines.label: Equal Protection in Marriage
doctrines.description: The Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause prohibits laws that deny same-sex couples the benefits afforded opposite-sex couples and bar them from exercising the fundamental right to marry, particularly where such denial works grave and continuing harm by disrespecting and subordinating gays and lesbians.
doctrines.notes: Established in Obergefell; recognizes the interlocking nature of Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses in the context of same-sex marriage.

doctrines.label: Right to Marital Privacy
doctrines.description: A constitutional right to marital privacy protected within the penumbra of specific guarantees of the Bill of Rights, which protects married persons' use of contraceptives from state prohibition.
doctrines.notes: Established in Griswold v. Connecticut. The doctrine protects intimate decisions within marriage from state interference.

doctrines.label: Substantive Due Process Liberty
doctrines.description: The Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protects a sphere of personal liberty that extends beyond spatial bounds to include autonomy of self, freedom of thought, belief, expression, and certain intimate conduct. Liberty presumes an autonomy of self that includes the right to make fundamental decisions affecting one's destiny without unwarranted government intrusion.
doctrines.notes: Established and clarified in Lawrence v. Texas. Builds on Griswold v. Connecticut, Eisenstadt v. Baird, Roe v. Wade, and Planned Parenthood v. Casey. Protects personal decisions relating to marriage, procreation, contraception, family relationships, child rearing, education, and intimate sexual conduct between consenting adults in private.

doctrines.label: Protection of Private Consensual Conduct
doctrines.description: Adults have a liberty interest under the Due Process Clause in making personal decisions concerning intimate conduct and relationships in private settings without criminal prohibition. The state cannot criminalize such conduct merely because a majority views it as immoral.
doctrines.notes: Established in Lawrence v. Texas. Applies to consensual conduct between adults in private settings. Does not extend to minors, coerced conduct, public conduct, or prostitution.

doctrines.label: Right to Refuse Medical Treatment
doctrines.description: A liberty interest under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protecting a competent person's right to refuse unwanted medical treatment, including life-sustaining hydration and nutrition.
doctrines.notes: Cruzan established that this right exists for competent persons but does not automatically extend to incompetent persons. States may impose procedural safeguards, including clear and convincing evidence standards, to protect this interest.

doctrines.label: Parental Rights in Education
doctrines.description: Parents have a fundamental liberty interest in directing the education and upbringing of their children, including the right to engage qualified teachers to instruct their children in subjects such as foreign languages. This right is protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause.
doctrines.notes: Meyer recognized the natural duty of parents to provide suitable education for their children and the constitutional protection of this parental prerogative.

doctrines.label: Liberty of Contract
doctrines.description: The right of individuals to enter into contracts regarding labor and employment, protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause. This doctrine recognizes that both employers and employees have the right to contract on terms they agree upon, subject to legitimate state police power regulations that have a fair, reasonable, and direct relation to public health, safety, morals, or welfare.
doctrines.notes: Lochner v. New York is the foundational case establishing the scope and limits of liberty of contract doctrine under the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court held that while states have police powers, those powers cannot be exercised arbitrarily or without a substantial connection to a legitimate public purpose.

doctrines.label: Police Power
doctrines.description: The inherent sovereign power of states to enact legislation to protect the safety, health, morals, and general welfare of the public. However, this power is not unlimited and must be exercised in a manner that does not arbitrarily or unreasonably interfere with individual liberty and freedom of contract protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.
doctrines.notes: Lochner establishes that while police power is broad, it has limits. The Court must determine whether a regulation is a fair, reasonable, and appropriate exercise of police power or an unreasonable, unnecessary, and arbitrary interference with individual rights.

doctrines.label: Ordered Liberty Doctrine
doctrines.description: A framework for determining which rights in the Bill of Rights are so fundamental to ordered liberty and the concept of due process that they are binding on the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. Rights are incorporated only if they are rooted in the traditions and conscience of the people and essential to a scheme of ordered liberty, not merely because they appear in the first eight amendments.
doctrines.notes: This doctrine represents the Court's selective incorporation approach, distinguishing between fundamental rights that apply to the states and procedural protections that do not. The test asks whether a right is 'of the very essence of a scheme of ordered liberty' and whether it is 'a principle of justice so rooted in the traditions and conscience of our people as to be ranked as fundamental.'

doctrines.label: Selective Incorporation Doctrine
doctrines.description: The constitutional doctrine that certain fundamental rights and protections enumerated in the Bill of Rights are incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause and thus apply to the states, while others are not. Incorporation occurs on a case-by-case basis depending on whether a right is fundamental to ordered liberty.
doctrines.notes: Palko established the framework for selective incorporation, rejecting the 'total incorporation' theory that all Bill of Rights protections automatically apply to the states. The Court identified certain rights (freedom of speech, press, religion, assembly, and right to counsel in capital cases) as fundamental, while rejecting others (grand jury indictment, jury trial, immunity from self-incrimination, double jeopardy protection) as not fundamental to ordered liberty.

doctrines.label: Fundamental Right to Procreation
doctrines.description: The right to marry and procreate is fundamental to the existence and survival of the race and is protected as a basic civil right under the Fourteenth Amendment. Laws affecting this right are subject to strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause.
doctrines.notes: Established in Skinner v. Oklahoma as a foundational principle requiring strict scrutiny of classifications affecting procreation and marriage.

doctrines.label: Strict Scrutiny in Equal Protection
doctrines.description: When legislation affects fundamental rights or involves suspect classifications, the state must demonstrate that the classification serves a compelling governmental interest and is narrowly tailored to achieve that interest. The classification must not be arbitrary or based on invidious discrimination.
doctrines.notes: Skinner establishes that strict scrutiny is essential when a state law involves fundamental rights such as procreation, to prevent invidious discriminations against groups or types of individuals.

doctrines.label: Strict Scrutiny for Racial Classifications
doctrines.description: Racial classifications, especially in criminal statutes, are subject to the most rigid scrutiny and can be upheld only if shown to be necessary to accomplish a permissible state objective independent of the racial discrimination the Fourteenth Amendment was designed to eliminate.
doctrines.notes: Established in Loving v. Virginia as the applicable standard for reviewing state statutes containing racial classifications. The Court emphasized that the mere fact of equal application of a statute containing racial classifications does not remove it from the Fourteenth Amendment's proscription of invidious racial discrimination.

doctrines.label: Fundamental Right to Marry
doctrines.description: The freedom to marry is a fundamental personal right essential to the orderly pursuit of happiness by free persons and is one of the basic civil rights of man, fundamental to human existence and survival. This right cannot be restricted by invidious racial discriminations.
doctrines.notes: Established in Loving v. Virginia as a fundamental liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court held that the freedom to marry, or not marry, a person of another race resides with the individual and cannot be infringed by the State.

doctrines.label: Family Living Arrangements and Substantive Due Process
doctrines.description: The doctrine that decisions regarding family composition and living arrangements are protected liberty interests under the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause, and that government restrictions on such arrangements must satisfy heightened constitutional scrutiny.
doctrines.notes: Established in Moore v. City of East Cleveland as a limitation on the holding in Village of Belle Terre v. Boraas, which had upheld restrictions on unrelated persons. Moore extended protection to blood relatives and family living arrangements.

doctrines.label: Parental Fundamental Right to Direct Upbringing
doctrines.description: A fundamental right under the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause for parents to make decisions concerning the care, custody, and control of their children, with a presumption that fit parents act in their children's best interests and are entitled to deference in such decisions.
doctrines.notes: Established in Troxel v. Granville as a fundamental liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause. The doctrine requires that state interference with parental decision-making receive heightened protection and that fit parents' determinations be accorded deference.

doctrines.label: Discriminatory Purpose Requirement for Equal Protection
