The Jaycees lost because their size, public accessibility, and non-selective membership criteria meant that forced inclusion of women did not burden their expressive mission. This framework set the stage for the harder question in Boy Scouts v. Dale — what happens when a group claims its selectivity IS integral to its expressive mission.

cases.full_name: Eastern Railroad Presidents Conference v. Noerr Motor Freight, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Railroad companies that lobbied state legislatures and the executive branch for laws and regulations disadvantageous to trucking competitors did not violate the Sherman Act. The right to petition government for legislation, even with anticompetitive intent, is protected. The Sherman Act does not apply to conduct that is 'an essential part of a program of lobbying' to influence government action.
cases.notes: Noerr was decided as a pure antitrust case under the Sherman Act — Black's opinion grounded the immunity in statutory interpretation and the right to petition, not explicitly in the First Amendment. The full constitutional grounding came in California Motor Transport (1972). Together they form the Noerr-Pennington doctrine.

cases.full_name: California Motor Transport Co. v. Trucking Unlimited
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Noerr immunity extends to petitioning administrative agencies and courts, not just legislatures. The First Amendment's Petition Clause protects the right to access all branches of government. However, a conspiracy to use the governmental process as a weapon to destroy competition — filing objectively baseless petitions solely to harm competitors through the process itself rather than through the outcome sought — falls outside First Amendment protection as a sham.
cases.notes: California Motor Transport gave Noerr-Pennington its full First Amendment grounding, extending the doctrine beyond legislative lobbying to courts and administrative agencies. It also articulated the sham exception — the first limitation on the doctrine. The sham exception was further refined in Professional Real Estate Investors v. Columbia Pictures (1993), which established the two-part test for sham litigation: (1) objectively baseless, and (2) subjective bad faith. That case is not modeled here but is part of the Noerr-Pennington lineage.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public employee's petition under the Petition Clause must address a matter of public concern to be protected from employer retaliation — the same standard that applies to public employee speech under the Speech Clause. The Petition Clause does not provide greater protection than the Speech Clause for public employee petitioning. Courts should generally analyze public employee speech and petition claims under the same framework.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Thomas argued the Petition Clause has independent historical meaning separate from the Speech Clause and should be interpreted through its own historical tradition rather than through the speech doctrine framework. His dissent reflects a broader originalist critique of the Court's tendency to subsume petition rights into speech analysis.

cases.notes: Guarnieri is the clearest statement that the Petition Clause does not operate independently of speech doctrine in the public employee context. Kennedy's majority held that treating petition and speech differently would create anomalies — a public employee who files a grievance gets no more protection than one who speaks publicly about the same matter. Thomas's dissent is significant as the most sustained recent argument for an independent Petition Clause. The public concern doctrine from Snyder v. Phelps (core_political_speech slice) applies here.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Espionage Act convictions of Russian immigrants who threw leaflets from a building criticizing U.S. intervention in Russia are affirmed. The leaflets were intended to provoke resistance to the war effort and constituted speech that created a clear and present danger of an evil Congress has a right to prevent.

opinions.author: Holmes
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Holmes's Abrams dissent is the foundational statement of marketplace-of-ideas free speech theory. He argued that 'the ultimate good desired is better reached by free trade in ideas — that the best test of truth is the power of the thought to get itself accepted in the competition of the market.' He argued the majority's 'clear and present danger' test required immediate, proximate danger — not the speculative threat the leaflets posed. This dissent, along with Gitlow (1925), became the intellectual foundation for Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969) fifty years later.

cases.notes: Abrams is significant primarily for Holmes's dissent, not the majority holding. The majority's expansive reading of 'clear and present danger' was effectively repudiated by Brandenburg (1969). Holmes's marketplace-of-ideas argument and his insistence on imminence and likelihood of harm became the doctrinal foundation of modern incitement law. status: limited because the majority's reasoning was superseded by Brandenburg.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: New York's Criminal Anarchy Act conviction of a Socialist Party member who published a manifesto calling for mass strikes and revolutionary action is affirmed. The First Amendment is incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment, but states may punish speech that tends to subvert or imperil the government even without showing immediate danger.

opinions.author: Holmes
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Holmes reiterated and deepened his Abrams argument — the manifesto posed no immediate danger and the marketplace of ideas should be trusted to reject dangerous ideas without government suppression. He argued that the test should be whether the speech creates a clear and present danger of an immediate evil, not whether it has a bad tendency. This dissent continued the intellectual lineage that culminated in Brandenburg.

cases.notes: Gitlow is significant for two holdings: (1) it incorporated the First Amendment against the states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause — the first incorporation of a Bill of Rights provision; (2) it applied the 'bad tendency' test rather than Holmes's clear and present danger standard. The bad tendency test was effectively overruled by Brandenburg (1969). Holmes's dissent continued the marketplace-of-ideas argument from Abrams.

cases.full_name: Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Public school students who wore black armbands to protest the Vietnam War engaged in constitutionally protected symbolic speech. School officials may not suppress student expression unless they can demonstrate facts that reasonably support a forecast that the expression will substantially disrupt or materially interfere with school activities. Students do not shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Justice Black argued that the Constitution does not guarantee students the right to use schools as platforms for political expression — schools are for education, not political activity. His dissent anticipated later decisions narrowing Tinker in specific contexts (Bethel, Hazelwood, Morse).

cases.notes: Tinker is the foundational student speech case and one of the most cited First Amendment decisions ever issued. The armband facts make it a particularly clean precedent — silent, passive, political expression with no evidence of disruption. Three subsequent cases (Bethel, Hazelwood, Morse) carved out specific exceptions to Tinker's substantial-disruption test, but the core rule remains good law. The Court's 2021 decision in Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. reaffirmed Tinker while declining to set a comprehensive rule for off-campus speech.

cases.full_name: Bethel School District No. 403 v. Fraser
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public school may discipline a student for delivering a lewd and sexually suggestive speech at a school assembly even without demonstrating substantial disruption. The First Amendment does not prevent school officials from determining that such speech is inappropriate for a school assembly. The constitutional rights of students in public schools are not automatically coextensive with the rights of adults in other settings.
cases.notes: Bethel established the first exception to Tinker's substantial-disruption requirement: lewd, vulgar, or plainly offensive speech may be restricted in the school context without showing disruption. The exception is limited to speech that is plainly offensive — not merely controversial or provocative.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public school may exercise editorial control over the style and content of student speech in school-sponsored expressive activities — such as a school newspaper — so long as the editorial decisions are reasonably related to legitimate pedagogical concerns. School-sponsored speech does not receive the full Tinker protection because the school itself is the speaker.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued the majority's 'legitimate pedagogical concerns' standard gave schools virtually unlimited authority to censor student expression in any school-sponsored activity, effectively gutting Tinker for the most important forum of student expression.

cases.notes: Hazelwood established the second Tinker exception: school-sponsored speech (newspapers, plays, curricular activities) may be regulated for legitimate pedagogical concerns without showing substantial disruption. The key distinction is between student expression that the school sponsors and bears responsibility for versus student expression that merely occurs on school grounds.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public school principal did not violate the First Amendment by confiscating a student's banner reading 'Bong Hits 4 Jesus' at a school-supervised event. Schools may restrict student speech that can reasonably be viewed as promoting illegal drug use.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stevens argued the majority created a dangerously broad exception that could be used to suppress any speech school officials deem inconsistent with school values — far beyond drug promotion. He argued Tinker should govern and the banner caused no disruption.

cases.notes: Morse established the third Tinker exception: speech that can reasonably be viewed as promoting illegal drug use may be restricted without a disruption showing. The exception is narrow — the Court explicitly declined to extend it to political speech about drug policy (e.g., advocating marijuana legalization would be protected). The 5-4 split reflects genuine disagreement about the breadth of the exception.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Texas sales tax exemption limited exclusively to periodicals published by religious organizations violates the Establishment Clause. A tax exemption that benefits only religious publications — without a secular equivalent — impermissibly endorses religion and subsidizes the dissemination of religious doctrine.

opinions.author: Blackmun
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Blackmun's concurrence distinguished Texas Monthly from Amos — a religious exemption from a civil regulation burden is permissible accommodation, but a tax exemption that bestows an affirmative financial benefit on religion without a corresponding burden is impermissible preference.

cases.notes: Texas Monthly draws the critical line between permissible accommodation and impermissible preference in religious exemption doctrine. An exemption that relieves a genuine burden on religious exercise (Amos) is constitutionally permissible; an exemption that confers a pure financial benefit on religion with no secular equivalent (Texas Monthly) violates the Establishment Clause. This distinction survives Kennedy v. Bremerton — the no-establishment core doctrine (which Texas Monthly enforces) was explicitly preserved by Kennedy.

cases.short_name: Corporation of Presiding Bishop v. Amos
cases.full_name: Corporation of the Presiding Bishop of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints v. Amos
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Section 702 of Title VII, which exempts religious organizations from the statute's prohibition on religious discrimination in employment, does not violate the Establishment Clause. Congress may permissibly accommodate religious organizations by exempting them from generally applicable civil rights laws, because doing so relieves the government-imposed burden on their ability to define their own religious community.
cases.notes: Amos is the foundational case for permissible religious accommodation doctrine. A unanimous Court held that exempting religious organizations from civil rights compliance is not an establishment of religion — it is a permissible accommodation that protects religious autonomy. Read alongside Texas Monthly (1989), Amos establishes that exemptions relieving genuine regulatory burdens on religious organizations are constitutional, while exemptions that confer pure financial benefits without corresponding burdens are not.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Jehovah's Witness convicted under a New Hampshire statute for calling a city marshal a 'damned Fascist' and a 'damned racketeer' in a public altercation could be criminally punished without violating the First Amendment. Certain well-defined and narrowly limited classes of speech — including the lewd and obscene, the profane, the libelous, and the insulting or fighting words — are not protected by the First Amendment because they are of slight social value and any benefit is clearly outweighed by the social interest in order and morality.
cases.notes: Chaplinsky established the fighting words doctrine and, more broadly, the concept that certain categories of speech fall entirely outside First Amendment protection. The categorical approach it announced — lewd, obscene, profane, libelous, fighting words — has been substantially modified by subsequent decisions. The Court has never upheld a fighting words conviction since Chaplinsky and has consistently found statutes that try to codify the doctrine overbroad or content-discriminatory. Status: limited because subsequent decisions have significantly narrowed the practical scope of fighting words.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: California could not criminally punish a man for wearing a jacket bearing the words 'Fuck the Draft' in a courthouse corridor. Absent a more particularized and compelling reason for its actions, the state may not make the simple public display of a single four-letter expletive a criminal offense. One man's vulgarity is another's lyric, and the government may not prohibit expression of an idea simply because society finds the idea disagreeable or its expression offensive.
cases.notes: Cohen is one of the most important First Amendment decisions for its articulation of why offensive speech is protected. Justice Harlan's majority opinion contains the famous line 'one man's vulgarity is another's lyric' and emphasizes that the government cannot be trusted as a guardian of public morality in restricting speech. The case is foundational for understanding why there is no general hate speech or offensive speech exception to the First Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A federal law prohibiting the knowing destruction of Selective Service registration certificates did not violate the First Amendment as applied to a Vietnam War protester who burned his draft card at a public demonstration. When speech and non-speech elements are combined in the same course of conduct, a sufficiently important government interest in regulating the non-speech element can justify incidental limitations on First Amendment freedoms if the regulation is unrelated to the suppression of expression and the incidental restriction is no greater than essential.
cases.notes: O'Brien established the foundational test for evaluating government regulation of expressive conduct. The four-part test it articulated is still applied whenever government regulates conduct that has an expressive component for reasons unrelated to its communicative impact. When the government's purpose is to suppress the message, O'Brien does not apply — strict scrutiny governs instead. The case is also notable for Chief Justice Warren's instruction that courts should not accept post-hoc rationalizations for congressional intent when the actual purpose was to suppress expression.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Obscene material is not protected by the First Amendment. The Court established a new three-part test for obscenity, replacing the prior Memoirs v. Massachusetts standard: (1) whether the average person, applying contemporary community standards, would find the work appeals to prurient interest; (2) whether the work depicts or describes, in a patently offensive way, sexual conduct specifically defined by applicable state law; and (3) whether the work, taken as a whole, lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value.

opinions.author: Douglas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Douglas argued there is no such thing as an obscenity exception to the First Amendment — the Framers protected all speech and obscenity prosecutions violate the guarantee of free expression.

cases.notes: Miller is the foundational obscenity case and replaced Memoirs v. Massachusetts (1966) with a more workable three-part test. The SLAPS test (serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value) in prong 3 was clarified in Pope v. Illinois (1987) to apply a national reasonable person standard rather than community standards. New York v. Ferber (1982) extended categorical unprotected status to child pornography even if not obscene under Miller.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A New York statute prohibiting the distribution of material depicting children engaged in sexual conduct does not violate the First Amendment even if the material would not be obscene under Miller v. California. Child pornography is categorically unprotected regardless of whether it satisfies the Miller test because the government's compelling interest in protecting children from exploitation justifies the restriction.
cases.notes: Ferber created a new categorical exception to First Amendment protection — child pornography — that operates independently of the Miller obscenity test. The Court's reasoning focused on the harm to actual children in the production process. Ashcroft v. Free Speech Coalition (2002) limited Ferber by striking down a federal statute prohibiting virtual child pornography (computer-generated images not involving actual children), holding that Ferber's rationale does not extend to images that do not involve the abuse of real children.

cases.short_name: R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul
cases.full_name: R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A St. Paul ordinance prohibiting the display of symbols that one knows or has reason to know arouses anger, alarm, or resentment in others on the basis of race, color, creed, religion, or gender — applied to a teenager who burned a cross on a Black family's lawn — violated the First Amendment. Even within categories of unprotected speech like fighting words, government may not prohibit speech based on the viewpoint or subject matter of the expression. The ordinance was unconstitutional because it selectively prohibited fighting words that communicated messages of racial, religious, or gender-based hatred while permitting fighting words on other topics.

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: White's concurrence agreed the ordinance was unconstitutional on overbreadth grounds but rejected Scalia's majority reasoning that content discrimination within unprotected categories is presumptively invalid. White argued the majority's reasoning was unnecessarily broad and would create doctrinal confusion.

cases.notes: R.A.V. is the foundational case establishing that government may not discriminate based on viewpoint even within categories of unprotected speech. Scalia's majority opinion articulated the principle that a legislature cannot 'license one side of a debate to fight freestyle while requiring the other to follow the Marquis of Queensberry rules.' Wisconsin v. Mitchell (1993) upheld bias-motivated crime sentence enhancements the following year, distinguishing R.A.V. on the ground that Mitchell targeted conduct rather than expression.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Virginia statute prohibiting cross burning with intent to intimidate is constitutional on its face because cross burning with intent to intimidate is a true threat unprotected by the First Amendment. However, the statute's provision treating any cross burning as prima facie evidence of intent to intimidate is unconstitutional because it allows conviction without proof of actual intent, thereby punishing expressive cross burning that does not constitute a true threat.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Thomas argued cross burning is conduct with no expressive value — it is pure intimidation with no legitimate communicative purpose and the First Amendment provides no protection for it.

cases.notes: Virginia v. Black navigated the tension between R.A.V.'s prohibition on content discrimination within unprotected categories and the state's interest in prohibiting cross burning as intimidation. The Court held that a ban on cross burning with intent to intimidate was constitutional because it targeted the true threat aspect (intimidation) rather than the viewpoint (racial hatred). The prima facie evidence provision was struck because it eliminated the intent requirement that saved the statute.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Wisconsin statute enhancing criminal sentences for bias-motivated crimes does not violate the First Amendment. Unlike R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul, which prohibited expression of certain viewpoints, the sentence enhancement provision targets the defendant's conduct — the commission of a crime — not his abstract beliefs or expression. The First Amendment does not prohibit the evidentiary use of speech to establish the elements or motive of a crime.
cases.notes: Mitchell is the companion case to R.A.V. decided the following Term. While R.A.V. struck a hate speech ordinance targeting expression, Mitchell upheld a bias crime sentence enhancement targeting conduct. The distinction between regulating expression (unconstitutional) and using expression as evidence of criminal motive (permissible) is the core of the Mitchell holding. The case established that legislatures may increase penalties for bias-motivated crimes without violating the First Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Federal Election Campaign Act's contribution limits are constitutional as a means of preventing quid pro quo corruption or its appearance. The Act's expenditure limits are unconstitutional because spending money to express political views is itself protected speech, and the anti-corruption interest alone cannot justify suppressing the quantity of political expression. Disclosure requirements and public financing provisions are constitutional.
cases.vote: per curiam with varying majorities by section
cases.notes: Buckley is the foundational campaign finance case establishing the expenditure/contribution distinction that still structures the doctrine. Status: limited because Citizens United v. FEC (2010) extended full First Amendment protection to corporate independent expenditures, significantly modifying Buckley's framework. McCutcheon v. FEC (2014) struck aggregate contribution limits. The core expenditure/contribution distinction from Buckley remains good law but its practical scope has been significantly altered.

cases.short_name: Metromedia v. City of San Diego
cases.full_name: Metromedia, Inc. v. City of San Diego
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: San Diego's ordinance banning most billboard advertising is unconstitutional. A plurality held the ordinance was an impermissible regulation of noncommercial speech because it allowed commercial advertising while prohibiting noncommercial messages, impermissibly preferring commercial speech over noncommercial speech. The case produced no majority rationale, with various justices applying different frameworks, but established that sign ordinances must be carefully tailored and cannot discriminate between commercial and noncommercial speech in ways that disadvantage the latter.

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: The White plurality applied Central Hudson to commercial speech and held that San Diego could regulate commercial billboards but not if it simultaneously restricted noncommercial messages — that preference for commercial over noncommercial speech is impermissible.

cases.notes: Metromedia is directly relevant to sign ordinance litigation and is the closest Supreme Court precedent to the vape shop hypothetical. Its fragmented plurality makes it difficult to apply but the core principle — sign ordinances cannot prefer commercial over noncommercial speech — is well-established. The case applies Central Hudson to outdoor advertising and establishes that content-based distinctions in sign ordinances trigger heightened scrutiny.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First Amendment does not exempt reporters from the general duty of citizens to respond to grand jury subpoenas and answer questions about criminal conduct they have witnessed. The press has no absolute privilege to refuse to disclose sources or information to grand juries. The newsgathering function is protected by the First Amendment, but the burden on newsgathering from requiring reporters to appear and testify before grand juries is not sufficient to override the general obligation of citizens to provide relevant information.

opinions.author: Powell
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Powell's concurrence, though joining the majority, emphasized that courts should balance the competing interests case by case and that a reporter's privilege claim should receive 'serious weight' when the reporter can demonstrate that the grand jury investigation is not conducted in good faith or that the information sought is not genuinely relevant. Most lower courts have used Powell's concurrence to recognize a qualified reporter's privilege in non-grand jury contexts.

cases.notes: Branzburg is the foundational reporter's privilege case but its practical effect is complicated by Powell's concurrence. The five-justice majority (White + Powell) technically rejected an absolute privilege, but Powell's separate reasoning has been used by most circuits to recognize a qualified privilege in civil cases and criminal cases outside the grand jury context. Many states have enacted shield laws providing broader protection than Branzburg's constitutional floor.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The public and press have a First Amendment right of access to criminal trials. A trial judge's order closing a criminal trial to the public and press over the objection of the press was unconstitutional. The right of access to criminal trials is implicit in the First Amendment guarantees of free speech and free press and has been part of the Anglo-American tradition for centuries.
cases.notes: Richmond Newspapers established a constitutional right of access to criminal proceedings rooted in the First Amendment. The right is qualified — courts may close proceedings to protect compelling interests such as a fair trial or victim privacy — but closure orders must be narrowly tailored and the court must consider alternatives. The right of access has been extended to jury selection (Press-Enterprise I) and preliminary hearings (Press-Enterprise II).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Ohio's school voucher program, which provided tuition aid to students in failing public schools including those attending religious schools, does not violate the Establishment Clause. The program is neutral with respect to religion and provides assistance to a broad class of citizens, with aid flowing to religious schools only as a result of the independent and private choices of parents. A program that is genuinely neutral and provides assistance directly to a broad class of citizens does not offend the Establishment Clause simply because some recipients choose to use it at religious schools.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Souter's dissent argued that direct government payment of tuition at religious schools violates Everson's prohibition on using tax funds to support religious education, regardless of how the program is structured.

cases.notes: Zelman is the high-water mark of permissiveness in the government aid to religion area. The neutrality/private choice framework it applied effectively replaced the Lemon test for aid programs. The decision has been understood to permit voucher programs to include religious schools as long as the program is genuinely neutral and families make the actual choice. Post-Kennedy v. Bremerton, the historical practices standard may further expand government latitude to fund religious education.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city's inclusion of a nativity scene in an annual Christmas display does not violate the Establishment Clause. The display, which also included secular holiday decorations, served legitimate secular purposes including celebrating the historical origins of the holiday and depicting the origins of the Christmas holiday. The religious benefit was indirect, remote, and incidental in the context of the broader display.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: O'Connor's concurrence introduced the endorsement test — the Establishment Clause prohibits government from making adherence to religion relevant to a person's standing in the political community. Government endorses religion when it conveys a message that religion is a favored or preferred belief. This concurrence became the foundation of the nonendorsement principle.

cases.notes: Lynch v. Donnelly is significant primarily for Justice O'Connor's endorsement test concurrence, which became the governing standard for holiday display cases in subsequent decisions. The majority's Lemon-based analysis is now superseded by Kennedy v. Bremerton's historical practices standard. Status: limited because the Lemon-based reasoning is no longer the governing framework, though the result (government holiday displays are constitutional in the right context) likely survives under the historical practices standard.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Espionage Act convictions of a Socialist Party official who distributed leaflets urging resistance to the military draft are affirmed. The leaflets were intended to obstruct the recruiting service and were not protected by the First Amendment in the context of wartime. The question is whether the words are used in such circumstances and are of such a nature as to create a clear and present danger that they will bring about the substantive evils that Congress has a right to prevent.
cases.notes: Schenck is historically significant as the first major Supreme Court decision on free speech and the source of Holmes's 'clear and present danger' test and his famous 'falsely shouting fire in a crowded theater' analogy. The case upheld conviction, but Holmes's clear and present danger test was soon applied more protectively in his Abrams and Gitlow dissents. Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969) effectively replaced the Schenck test with the incitement test, requiring imminence and likelihood of harm. Status: limited because the governing incitement standard is Brandenburg, not Schenck.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Smith Act convictions of leaders of the Communist Party of America for conspiracy to advocate violent overthrow of the government are affirmed. The clear and present danger test must be calibrated to the gravity of the evil discounted by its improbability — when the evil is sufficiently grave (violent overthrow of government), even a less immediate danger may justify suppression. The government need not wait until the attempt is made before acting against the conspiracy.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black argued the convictions were a virulent form of prior censorship — punishing speech about overthrowing the government rather than any overt act, violating the First Amendment.

opinions.author: Douglas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Douglas argued the clear and present danger test had been so diluted as to be meaningless — the defendants were convicted for their ideas, not their acts.

cases.notes: Dennis represents the nadir of First Amendment protection in the Cold War era. The Hand formula (probability of harm × gravity of harm) the plurality applied gave the government enormous latitude to prosecute political speech it deemed dangerous. Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969) effectively repudiated Dennis's approach by requiring imminence and likelihood of harm. Status: limited — the governing incitement standard is Brandenburg, which Dennis's holding is inconsistent with.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: California's Criminal Syndicalism Act, applied to a member of the Communist Labor Party who participated in its organization, does not violate the First or Fourteenth Amendments. The state may punish those who abuse the rights of speech and assembly by using them to incite violence and crime and threaten the overthrow of organized government.

opinions.author: Brandeis
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brandeis's Whitney concurrence is one of the most celebrated First Amendment opinions ever written. He argued that the founders believed that freedom to think as you will and to speak as you think are means indispensable to the discovery and spread of political truth. No danger flowing from speech can be deemed clear and present unless the incidence of the evil apprehended is so imminent that it may befall before there is opportunity for full discussion. The concurrence articulated a robust vision of free speech as a precondition for democratic self-government that became foundational to modern First Amendment jurisprudence.

cases.notes: Whitney is significant primarily for Brandeis's concurrence, which articulated the imminence and likelihood requirements that eventually became Brandenburg's incitement test. The majority holding was overruled by Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969). Like Abrams and Gitlow, Whitney's lasting importance is the minority opinion that became the law fifty years later.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act's (McCain-Feingold) soft money ban and electioneering communications restrictions are largely upheld. The anti-corruption interest is broad enough to support restrictions on large soft money contributions to political parties and restrictions on corporate and union-funded electioneering communications close to elections. Congress may address not just quid pro quo corruption but also the appearance of corruption from large financial contributions.
cases.notes: McConnell's core holding upholding BCRA was substantially overruled by Citizens United v. FEC (2010), which struck the electioneering communications restrictions as applied to corporate independent expenditures. Status: limited — significant portions of McConnell's reasoning were rejected by Citizens United, which narrowed the anti-corruption interest to quid pro quo corruption only.

cases.full_name: Hague v. Committee for Industrial Organization
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Streets and parks have immemorially been held in trust for the use of the public and have been used for purposes of assembly, communicating thought between citizens, and discussing public questions. The privilege to use public streets and parks for communication of views may be regulated in the interest of all but must not be abridged or denied. A Jersey City ordinance requiring a permit for public meetings in streets and parks was unconstitutional as applied.
cases.notes: Hague v. CIO is the foundational public forum case. Justice Roberts's plurality articulated the principle that streets and parks have historically been held in trust for public expression, which became the basis for the traditional public forum category. Perry Education Association v. Perry Local Educators' Association (1983) systematized the public forum doctrine into the three-category framework that governs today.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Virginia's statute prohibiting the NAACP from soliciting clients for civil rights litigation violates the First Amendment. Abstract discussion is not the only species of communication protected by the First Amendment — the NAACP's litigation activities are a form of political expression and association that receives full constitutional protection. Litigation itself is a form of political activity protected by the First and Fourteenth Amendments.
cases.notes: NAACP v. Button is significant for recognizing litigation as a form of protected political expression and for establishing that the NAACP's pattern of civil rights litigation constituted expressive association. The case is in the Freedom of Petition slice as well as Freedom of Association — it bridges the two doctrinal areas and established that coordinated legal advocacy is constitutionally protected activity.

cases.full_name: Dun & Bradstreet, Inc. v. Greenmoss Builders, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When a defamatory statement does not involve a matter of public concern, the First Amendment does not restrict the award of presumed and punitive damages even without proof of actual malice. Gertz v. Robert Welch imposed First Amendment limits on defamation law only when the speech involves matters of public concern. Speech on purely private matters — such as a credit reporting agency's erroneous report of a business's bankruptcy — does not implicate the same First Amendment interests and states may apply traditional common law defamation standards.
cases.notes: Dun & Bradstreet established that the First Amendment's limits on defamation law — established in New York Times v. Sullivan and Gertz — apply only to speech on matters of public concern. For purely private speech (like a credit report), states may apply common law defamation standards including presumed and punitive damages without proof of actual malice. The public concern/private matter distinction is now a critical threshold in defamation analysis.

cases.full_name: Miami Herald Publishing Co. v. Tornillo
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Florida's right-of-reply statute, requiring newspapers to publish replies from political candidates who have been attacked or criticized, violates the First Amendment. The Constitution prohibits government from commanding editors what must be printed. The choice of material to publish, treatment of public issues and officials, decisions about the content of the newspaper — these represent the exercise of editorial control and judgment that the First Amendment protects.
cases.notes: Tornillo established that the First Amendment protects newspapers' editorial autonomy from government compelled-speech mandates. The decision was decided the same Term as Gertz v. Robert Welch and together they established the outer limits of government regulation of newspaper content. Tornillo is contrasted with Red Lion Broadcasting v. FCC (1969), which upheld the Fairness Doctrine for broadcast media — the Court distinguished newspapers from broadcasters based on spectrum scarcity.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Pennsylvania's Sunday closing laws do not unconstitutionally burden the free exercise of religion of Orthodox Jewish merchants who observe Saturday as the Sabbath. Although the law makes observance of their religion more expensive, it is a law of general applicability that does not make religious practice unlawful — only economically more burdensome. The state's interest in providing a uniform day of rest is sufficient to justify this incidental burden on religious practice.
cases.notes: Braunfeld is the precursor to Sherbert v. Verner (1963), decided two years later. The two cases appear to reach inconsistent results — Braunfeld upheld Sunday closing laws that burdened Jewish merchants, while Sherbert struck a denial of unemployment benefits that burdened a Seventh-day Adventist. Employment Division v. Smith (1990) resolved the tension by returning to the Braunfeld approach — neutral, generally applicable laws do not require religious exemptions. Status: limited because Smith significantly narrowed Sherbert's compelling interest framework.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Chicago breach of peace ordinance, as applied to a suspended Catholic priest whose inflammatory speech provoked audience disorder, was unconstitutional. The ordinance was construed by the trial court to prohibit speech that stirs the public to anger, invites dispute, brings about unrest, or creates a disturbance — but a function of free speech is to invite dispute and create dissatisfaction with conditions as they are. Speech may best serve its purpose when it induces a condition of unrest or stirs people to anger.

opinions.author: Jackson
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Jackson's Terminiello dissent contains the famous line that the Constitution is not a suicide pact — arguing that the First Amendment cannot be interpreted to prevent government from maintaining public order when inflammatory speech triggers genuine threats of violence.

cases.notes: Terminiello established that speech retains First Amendment protection even when it provokes audience hostility and disorder. The decision is the foundational authority against the heckler's veto. Feiner v. New York (1951) is sometimes read as in tension with Terminiello, but is distinguished on the ground that the Feiner crowd presented imminent violence that police could not control, while the Terminiello prosecution was based on the speaker's inflammatory content, not an actual uncontrollable emergency.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A disorderly conduct conviction of a speaker who refused to stop speaking after police ordered him to cease when an increasingly hostile crowd threatened imminent violence did not violate the First Amendment. When the speaker passes the bounds of argument and undertakes incitement to riot and police are unable to maintain order, they may act to prevent a breach of the peace.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black argued the police were permitting the crowd to exercise a veto over the speaker's First Amendment rights — the heckler's veto problem. Police should have protected the speaker, not silenced him.

cases.notes: Feiner is the companion case to Terminiello and represents the outer limit of hostile audience doctrine. The case is often read as permitting police to silence a speaker when the crowd presents genuinely imminent, uncontrollable violence. Courts have generally read Feiner narrowly — requiring that police first attempt to control the crowd before ordering the speaker to desist. The case is frequently invoked in campus free speech and public demonstration contexts.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Obscenity is not within the area of constitutionally protected speech or press. The test for obscenity is whether, to the average person applying contemporary community standards, the dominant theme of the material taken as a whole appeals to prurient interest. Material with even the slightest redeeming social importance is not obscene.
cases.notes: Roth established that obscenity is categorically unprotected and introduced the community standards / prurient interest test. The 'slightest redeeming social importance' standard proved unworkable and was replaced by Miller v. California (1973), which substituted the SLAPS test (serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value) for Roth's minimal-value standard. Status: limited because Miller replaced the Roth test, though the core holding that obscenity is unprotected remains.

cases.short_name: Paris Adult Theatre I v. Slaton
cases.full_name: Paris Adult Theatre I v. Slaton
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: States may regulate obscene material shown in adult theaters even when access is limited to consenting adults and the material is not displayed publicly. The First Amendment does not protect the commercial exhibition of obscene films. There is a legitimate state interest in maintaining a decent society that extends beyond protecting unconsenting adults or minors.
cases.notes: Paris Adult Theatre was decided the same day as Miller v. California and together they established the modern obscenity framework. Paris addressed the question Miller left open — whether the state may regulate obscenity even when only consenting adults are present. The Court said yes, rejecting the Stanley v. Georgia privacy-based argument that what adults watch in private is none of the government's business when extended to commercial exhibition.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Child Pornography Prevention Act's prohibition on virtual child pornography — sexually explicit images that appear to depict minors but are produced without using real children — violates the First Amendment. Ferber's categorical exclusion of child pornography from First Amendment protection was based on the harm to actual children in production. That rationale does not extend to virtual images that do not involve the abuse of real children.
cases.notes: Ashcroft v. Free Speech Coalition distinguished and limited Ferber by holding that virtual child pornography (not involving actual children) does not fall within the Ferber categorical exception. The Court rejected the government's argument that virtual images could be banned because they might be used to groom children or because they could be confused with real images — speculative harms insufficient to justify categorical suppression of non-obscene expression.

cases.short_name: Police Department of Chicago v. Mosley
cases.full_name: Police Department of Chicago v. Mosley
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Chicago ordinance prohibiting all picketing within 150 feet of a school except for labor picketing violated the First Amendment by making an impermissible distinction between labor and non-labor picketing based on the content of the message. Above all else, the First Amendment means that government has no power to restrict expression because of its message, its ideas, its subject matter, or its content. Once a forum is opened to assembly or speaking, government may not prohibit others from assembling or speaking on the basis of what they intend to say.
cases.notes: Mosley is one of the foundational content-neutrality cases. Justice Marshall's statement that 'above all else, the First Amendment means that government has no power to restrict expression because of its message' is among the most frequently cited formulations of the content-neutrality principle. The case established that government may not selectively exempt favored speakers or messages from otherwise applicable restrictions.

cases.short_name: Perry Education Ass'n v. Perry Local Educators' Ass'n
cases.full_name: Perry Education Association v. Perry Local Educators' Association
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First Amendment does not require a school district to give a rival union equal access to an internal mail system when the exclusive access was granted to the incumbent union as part of a collective bargaining agreement. The school's internal mail system is a nonpublic forum — government property not opened for general expression — and the access restrictions are reasonable and viewpoint-neutral.
cases.notes: Perry Education Association formalized the three-category public forum framework: (1) traditional public forums (streets, parks) where content-based restrictions require strict scrutiny; (2) designated public forums (government property opened for expressive activity) where the same strict scrutiny applies; and (3) nonpublic forums (government property not opened for expression) where only reasonable and viewpoint-neutral restrictions are required. This taxonomy now structures all public forum analysis.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Federal regulations prohibiting federally-funded family planning clinics from providing abortion counseling or referrals do not violate the First Amendment. The government may choose to fund a program to encourage childbirth over abortion and may implement that choice by restricting the use of federal funds for abortion-related speech. The government is not required to subsidize the exercise of fundamental rights.

opinions.author: Blackmun
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Blackmun argued the regulations constituted viewpoint-based suppression — prohibiting doctors from speaking about abortion while permitting speech encouraging childbirth, in a setting where the doctor-patient relationship creates a captive audience unable to seek information elsewhere.

cases.notes: Rust v. Sullivan is the foundational government speech / selective subsidy case. The Court held that when government subsidizes one viewpoint and withholds subsidy from another, it is not restricting speech but exercising its own expressive prerogative. Rosenberger v. Rector (1995) distinguished Rust by holding that when government creates a forum for private speech (student activity fund), it cannot exclude viewpoints — the distinction is between government speaking itself versus government facilitating private speech.

cases.full_name: Red Lion Broadcasting Co. v. FCC
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The FCC's Fairness Doctrine — requiring broadcasters to present contrasting views on controversial public issues — and its personal attack rules are consistent with the First Amendment. Because broadcast spectrum is scarce and not everyone who wishes to broadcast can do so, the government may impose content obligations on licensees. Broadcasters' First Amendment rights are not absolute and must be balanced against the public's right to receive balanced information.
cases.notes: Red Lion established the scarcity rationale for broadcast regulation, permitting content obligations that would be unconstitutional if applied to print media. The FCC eliminated the Fairness Doctrine in 1987, so the specific holding has little practical significance. The broader scarcity rationale survives formally but has been criticized as anachronistic. Turner Broadcasting v. FCC (1994) applied intermediate scrutiny to cable must-carry requirements — refusing to extend Red Lion's deferential standard to cable while also refusing to apply strict scrutiny.

cases.full_name: Turner Broadcasting System, Inc. v. FCC
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Cable television operators are not subject to the same reduced First Amendment protection as broadcast licensees. The must-carry provisions of the Cable Television Consumer Protection and Competition Act — requiring cable operators to carry local broadcast stations — are subject to intermediate scrutiny rather than either Red Lion deference or strict scrutiny. Cable operators exercise significant editorial discretion and are not subject to the scarcity rationale that justifies broadcast regulation.
cases.notes: Turner Broadcasting placed cable between broadcast (Red Lion, reduced protection) and print (Tornillo, full protection) in terms of First Amendment scrutiny. The must-carry provisions were ultimately upheld on remand (Turner II, 1997) after the government produced sufficient evidence that they served the government's interest in preserving local broadcasting. Turner's intermediate scrutiny framework is now the baseline for cable and arguably internet platform regulation.

cases.short_name: City of Renton v. Playtime Theatres
cases.full_name: City of Renton v. Playtime Theatres, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Renton, Washington zoning ordinance prohibiting adult theaters within 1,000 feet of residential zones, churches, parks, or schools is constitutional. The ordinance is properly analyzed as a content-neutral time, place, and manner restriction rather than a content-based restriction, because its predominant purpose is to address the secondary effects of adult theaters — crime, neighborhood deterioration — rather than to suppress their protected expression.
cases.notes: Renton established the secondary effects doctrine as a formal exception to content-neutrality analysis. A zoning ordinance targeting adult businesses based on their content (sexually explicit expression) was treated as content-neutral because its purpose was to address secondary effects (crime, property values) rather than to suppress the expression itself. City of Los Angeles v. Alameda Books (2002) tightened the standard by requiring actual evidence linking the secondary effects to the regulated businesses.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An Indiana public indecency statute requiring nude dancers in adult entertainment establishments to wear pasties and a G-string does not violate the First Amendment. Although nude dancing is expressive conduct that falls within the outer perimeters of the First Amendment, the requirement to wear minimal clothing is justified by the state's substantial interest in societal order and morality and is no greater than essential to further that interest.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Souter concurred but applied the secondary effects rationale from Renton rather than the societal order and morality justification used by the plurality — the requirement could be upheld to prevent the secondary effects associated with nude dancing establishments.

cases.notes: Barnes v. Glen Theatre held that nude dancing is expressive conduct entitled to some First Amendment protection but that modest clothing requirements survive the O'Brien test. The fragmented plurality produced a confusing result — Rehnquist applied O'Brien's morality rationale while Souter applied Renton's secondary effects rationale. City of Erie v. Pap's A.M. (2000) reaffirmed Barnes but a majority adopted Souter's secondary effects rationale, clarifying that the secondary effects approach governs nude dancing regulations.

cases.short_name: City of Erie v. Pap's A.M.
cases.full_name: City of Erie v. Pap's A.M.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Erie's public indecency ordinance requiring nude dancers to wear pasties and G-strings is constitutional. Although nude dancing is expressive conduct, the ordinance is justified by the city's interest in combating the secondary effects associated with nude dancing establishments. A majority adopted the secondary effects rationale from Renton and Souter's Barnes concurrence, applying O'Brien intermediate scrutiny.
cases.notes: Pap's A.M. reaffirmed Barnes v. Glen Theatre and clarified that the secondary effects rationale governs nude dancing regulations. A majority adopted Justice Souter's Barnes concurrence approach, establishing that nude dancing ordinances are properly analyzed under the O'Brien framework as content-neutral regulations justified by secondary effects. The case resolved the plurality confusion in Barnes.

cases.short_name: Village of Schaumburg v. Citizens for a Better Environment
cases.full_name: Village of Schaumburg v. Citizens for a Better Environment
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A village ordinance prohibiting solicitation by charitable organizations that do not use 75% of receipts for charitable purposes is unconstitutional. Charitable solicitation is protected First Amendment activity that is at the heart of the freedom guaranteed by the First Amendment. The 75% requirement is not narrowly tailored — it restricts protected solicitation even when no fraud or misrepresentation is involved.
cases.notes: Village of Schaumburg established that charitable solicitation — going door to door to raise funds for a cause — is protected First Amendment activity, not merely commercial speech. The case is significant for its recognition that solicitation combines the protected activities of informing the public, advocating a cause, and asking for support. Restrictions on charitable solicitation receive more searching scrutiny than restrictions on purely commercial fundraising.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Minnesota law allowing state income tax deductions for educational expenses — including tuition at private religious schools — does not violate the Establishment Clause. The deduction is available to all parents, public and private school alike, and any benefit to religious schools flows only through the independent decisions of parents. The neutrality of the program and the private choice of parents to send children to religious schools prevents the program from constituting government support for religion.
cases.notes: Mueller v. Allen was an early step in the evolution from strict separationism toward permissive neutrality in government aid to religion cases. The neutrality/private choice framework Mueller applied was refined in subsequent cases and became the governing standard in Zelman v. Simmons-Harris (2002), which upheld a full school voucher program. Mueller is the immediate precursor to the modern aid doctrine.

cases.short_name: Board of Education of Kiryas Joel v. Grumet
cases.full_name: Board of Education of Kiryas Joel Village School District v. Grumet
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A New York law creating a special school district coextensive with the boundaries of a Satmar Hasidic community violates the Establishment Clause. The legislature drew the district specifically for a religious community — delegating governmental authority along religious lines — which constitutes a form of government recognition or accommodation of a religious community that crosses the line into establishment.
cases.notes: Kiryas Joel addressed the line between permissible accommodation of religion and impermissible establishment. The Court held that New York crossed the line by drawing a school district specifically to accommodate a single religious community — delegation of governmental authority along religious lines constitutes establishment even if the legislature's motive was accommodating rather than promoting religion. The case is important for defining when government accommodation of religion becomes preference.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A radio commentator who broadcast an illegally intercepted phone call — which he received from an unknown source and had no role in intercepting — cannot be held civilly liable under federal wiretapping law for broadcasting the contents of a conversation on a matter of public concern. The First Amendment interest in the free flow of information about public matters outweighs the privacy interest of parties to the intercepted conversation, at least when the publisher had no role in the illegal interception and the content involved a matter of public concern.
cases.notes: Bartnicki v. Vopper addressed the tension between press freedom and privacy in the context of illegally obtained information. The Court held that a media defendant who receives and publishes illegally intercepted information cannot be held liable when the content is of public concern and the media defendant played no role in the illegal interception. The case is significant for refusing to impose liability on good-faith publishing of newsworthy information even when the original acquisition was illegal.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Georgia's law imposing civil liability on broadcasters for publishing the name of a rape victim obtained from official court records violates the First Amendment. The press may not be sanctioned for publishing truthful information that it has lawfully obtained from the public record. The state's interest in protecting the privacy of rape victims cannot justify suppressing accurate information obtained from open public proceedings.
cases.notes: Cox Broadcasting established the principle that the press may freely publish truthful information lawfully obtained from public records. The case is foundational for understanding the press's relationship to official proceedings and documents. It was later extended in Florida Star v. B.J.F. (1989), which held that a state may not impose liability on a newspaper for publishing a rape victim's name obtained from a publicly released police report.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Municipal ordinances prohibiting the distribution of handbills on public streets, even for legitimate anti-litter purposes, violate the First Amendment. Municipalities may not abridge freedom of speech and press even under the guise of local regulation. The purpose of keeping streets clean does not outweigh the citizen's right to distribute literature.
cases.notes: Schneider v. State is an early and important case establishing that leafleting and handbill distribution on public streets is protected First Amendment activity. The case established that while government may address the anti-litter interest through other means (requiring distributors to clean up or punishing litterbugs), it may not prohibit the protected expressive activity itself. Schneider is foundational for public forum and handbilling doctrine.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Georgia breach of peace statute prohibiting opprobrious or abusive language is unconstitutionally overbroad. The statute sweeps within its prohibition protected expression — words that are merely offensive or insulting but do not constitute fighting words under Chaplinsky's narrow definition. A statute that punishes constitutionally protected conduct must be precisely drawn.
cases.notes: Gooding v. Wilson illustrates why the fighting words doctrine has been practically unenforceable since Chaplinsky. Statutes broad enough to capture genuinely threatening face-to-face speech also sweep in protected offensive speech, making them unconstitutionally overbroad. The Court has consistently struck fighting words statutes that are broader than Chaplinsky's narrow face-to-face incitement-to-immediate-breach standard.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Griffin, Georgia ordinance requiring a permit from the City Manager before distributing literature of any kind is unconstitutional as a prior restraint on freedom of speech and press. An ordinance that grants an official broad discretion to grant or deny permission to distribute literature is invalid on its face — it gives an official the power to decide what ideas may be circulated.
cases.notes: Lovell v. City of Griffin is one of the earliest prior restraint cases and established the foundational principle that licensing systems granting officials broad discretion to permit or prohibit the distribution of literature are unconstitutional prior restraints. The case involved a Jehovah's Witness and reflects the mid-20th century pattern of prior restraint cases arising from municipal attempts to suppress religious proselytizing.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A trial court's order restraining the press from publishing accounts of confessions and other evidence in a murder case was an unconstitutional prior restraint. Gag orders imposed on the press to protect a defendant's fair trial right face a heavy presumption of unconstitutionality. Before imposing such an order, a court must find that the danger to a fair trial is clear and present, that less restrictive alternatives will not adequately protect the right, and that the order will effectively prevent the threatened harm.
cases.notes: Nebraska Press Association established the framework for evaluating gag orders — court orders restraining the press from publishing information about pending criminal cases. The case is a major prior restraint precedent and effectively ended the practice of prior restraints in criminal cases except in extraordinary circumstances. The three-part test it articulated (clear danger, no adequate alternatives, effectiveness) is extremely difficult to satisfy.

cases.short_name: Thompson v. Western States Medical Center
cases.full_name: Thompson v. Western States Medical Center
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A federal law prohibiting pharmacies from advertising compounded drugs violated the First Amendment commercial speech protections. The government failed to demonstrate that the advertising ban directly and materially advanced its interest in preventing large-scale manufacturing under the guise of compounding, or that the restriction was no more extensive than necessary. If the government's concern is that people will make uninformed decisions, the response should be more speech, not enforced silence.
cases.notes: Thompson v. Western States Medical Center is a significant commercial speech case applying Central Hudson to pharmaceutical advertising restrictions. The case reaffirmed that the government cannot simply suppress commercial speech to prevent consumers from making choices the government disapproves of — it must demonstrate that the restriction directly and materially advances a substantial interest and is narrowly tailored. The decision strengthened commercial speech protection in the pharmaceutical advertising context.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A municipal theater board's refusal to allow a production of Hair to be staged in a city-owned theater was an unconstitutional prior restraint because it lacked the procedural safeguards required for licensing schemes under Freedman v. Maryland. Any system of prior restraint must provide prompt judicial review, limit the time for the censor to act, and place the burden of proving the speech is unprotected on the censor. The theater board's exercise of unbridled discretion without these procedural prerequisites constituted an unconstitutional prior restraint.
cases.notes: Southeastern Promotions v. Conrad combined prior restraint doctrine with public forum analysis — holding that a government-operated theater is a public forum and that content-based exclusions from such a forum constitute prior restraints requiring procedural safeguards. The case is significant for extending prior restraint doctrine beyond traditional media (newspapers, pamphlets) to government-operated performance venues.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An Illinois criminal libel statute making it unlawful to publish material that exposes citizens of any race, color, creed, or religion to contempt, derision, or obloquy does not violate the First Amendment as applied to a man who distributed leaflets portraying Black Americans as criminals. Group libel — defamation of a defined group — may be criminally punished.
cases.notes: Beauharnais has never been formally overruled but its continued validity is seriously questioned. New York Times v. Sullivan (1964) and subsequent cases significantly expanded First Amendment protection for speech on public concern, undermining the categorical approach Beauharnais employed. R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul (1992) further limited the government's ability to target speech based on its viewpoint. Most scholars and lower courts treat Beauharnais as effectively overruled or at minimum severely limited, but the Supreme Court has not formally overruled it. Status: questioned.

cases.short_name: Globe Newspaper Co. v. Superior Court
cases.full_name: Globe Newspaper Co. v. Superior Court
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Massachusetts law requiring closure of criminal trials during the testimony of minor victims of sex offenses violates the First Amendment right of access to criminal proceedings. The right of access established in Richmond Newspapers v. Virginia may be overcome only by a compelling government interest, and the mandatory closure statute — which does not permit case-by-case consideration of whether closure is actually necessary — fails that standard.
cases.notes: Globe Newspaper applied and extended Richmond Newspapers by establishing that while courts may close proceedings to protect minor sex offense victims in appropriate cases, a mandatory closure statute is unconstitutional because it prevents case-by-case determination of whether closure is actually necessary. The case reaffirmed that the First Amendment right of access to criminal proceedings can be overcome only by compelling, case-specific justification.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Eugene Debs's conviction under the Espionage Act for an anti-war speech in Canton, Ohio is affirmed. The speech had as its natural tendency and reasonably probable effect the obstruction of recruiting, and Debs intended that effect. The clear and present danger test from Schenck applies.
cases.notes: Debs was decided the same week as Schenck and applied the same clear and present danger test to uphold conviction of the Socialist Party's presidential candidate for anti-war speech. The decision is remarkable for its breadth — Debs's speech did not explicitly urge draft resistance but praised those who had resisted. Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969) replaced the Schenck/Debs test with the imminence and likelihood requirements that now govern. Status: limited — governing standard is Brandenburg.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Espionage Act conviction of a newspaper editor who published articles criticizing the war and the draft is affirmed. The articles had a natural tendency to produce the prohibited result of obstructing recruiting. The First Amendment does not prevent punishment of speech that creates a clear and present danger of the evils Congress has the right to prevent.
cases.notes: Frohwerk is the third of the three Espionage Act cases decided in March 1919, along with Schenck and Debs. All three applied the clear and present danger test to uphold convictions. The trilogy is significant as the starting point of modern free speech doctrine, though all three holdings have been superseded by Brandenburg. Holmes began dissenting from this approach in Abrams v. United States just months later.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A young man's statement at a political rally — 'If they ever make me carry a rifle the first man I want to get in my sights is L.B.J.' — did not constitute a true threat against the President within the meaning of a federal statute. The statement was clearly a crude political hyperbole made in the context of a political meeting, not a genuine threat. True threats — statements made under circumstances where reasonable persons would interpret them as serious expression of intent to commit violence — fall outside First Amendment protection, but political hyperbole does not.
cases.notes: Watts v. United States is the foundational true threats case — establishing that only genuine expressions of intent to commit violence, not political hyperbole or crude rhetoric, fall outside First Amendment protection. The case is brief (per curiam) but has been cited extensively in subsequent true threats cases. Virginia v. Black (2003) and Counterman v. Colorado (2023) further developed the true threats doctrine, with Counterman adding a subjective mental state requirement.

cases.full_name: Planned Parenthood of the Columbia/Willamette Inc. v. American Coalition of Life Activists
cases.court_name: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit
cases.holding: Wanted-style posters naming abortion providers and a website listing their personal information constituted true threats unprotected by the First Amendment, even though they did not explicitly threaten violence. When statements are made in a context where prior similar statements have been followed by acts of violence, a reasonable person in the recipient's position could interpret them as threats of serious bodily harm. The true threat inquiry examines whether a reasonable person in the recipient's position — not just a reasonable speaker — would perceive the statement as a threat.

opinions.author: Reinhardt
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Reinhardt argued the majority's standard chilled protected political advocacy — the posters and website contained no explicit threats and were indistinguishable from legitimate political communication about abortion providers.

cases.notes: Planned Parenthood v. ACLA is the leading circuit court case on true threats in the context of anti-abortion advocacy. The en banc Ninth Circuit held that context matters enormously — when similar statements have preceded actual violence against the threatened class, a reasonable recipient could interpret even facially non-threatening statements as genuine threats. The case is significant for adopting the reasonable recipient (rather than reasonable speaker) standard for true threats, a position the Supreme Court addressed in Counterman v. Colorado (2023).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Contempt citations against a labor leader and a newspaper for out-of-court publications commenting on pending litigation violated the First Amendment. The clear and present danger test requires that the threatened evil be imminent and substantial — the mere reasonable tendency of publications to influence courts is insufficient to justify punishment. Out-of-court criticism of judicial proceedings receives First Amendment protection absent a clear and present danger of obstruction of justice.
cases.notes: Bridges v. California is significant for applying the clear and present danger test to contempt of court prosecutions and requiring that the danger be substantially imminent — not merely that publications could tend to influence judicial proceedings. The case extended First Amendment protection to commentary on pending litigation and established that courts are not immune from public criticism. The decision strengthened the imminence requirement that would eventually become central to Brandenburg.

cases.short_name: City of Los Angeles v. Alameda Books
cases.full_name: City of Los Angeles v. Alameda Books, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Los Angeles's ordinance prohibiting adult establishments from operating multiple businesses (bookstores and arcades) under one roof, justified by secondary effects, survives the threshold requirement of providing evidence that the regulation will reduce the secondary effects it is designed to address. The city may rely on a prior study of adult establishments even if the study does not specifically address the combination-use restriction, as long as the city reasonably relies on the study and the challengers fail to cast doubt on the study's relevance.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kennedy's concurrence signaled skepticism about the secondary effects doctrine, arguing that government should not be permitted to invoke secondary effects as a pretext for suppressing disfavored expression and that the doctrine requires genuine evidentiary support.

cases.notes: Alameda Books tightened the evidentiary requirements for secondary effects justifications without abandoning the doctrine. The plurality held that cities must provide some evidence that their regulatory scheme will actually reduce secondary effects, and that challengers who cast doubt on the evidence can require the city to present additional justification. Kennedy's concurrence is significant for suggesting the secondary effects doctrine may need further limitation.

cases.short_name: Minneapolis Star & Tribune Co. v. Minnesota Commissioner of Revenue
cases.full_name: Minneapolis Star and Tribune Co. v. Minnesota Commissioner of Revenue
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Minnesota use tax on ink and paper used in publication, with an exemption for the first $100,000 of paper and ink consumed, violates the First Amendment by singling out the press for special taxation. A tax that targets the press and burdens a small number of publishers imposes a burden that raises serious concerns about government interference with a constitutionally protected activity.
cases.notes: Minneapolis Star & Tribune established that government may not single out the press for special taxation that does not apply to other businesses. The decision reflects the principle that the press occupies a protected position under the First Amendment and that differential treatment — even in the form of taxation — raises constitutional concerns. Grosjean v. American Press Co. (1936) is the precursor case striking a Louisiana tax on newspaper advertising revenue.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Virginia statute making it a crime to divulge information about confidential proceedings of the state judicial conduct commission violates the First Amendment as applied to a newspaper that published accurate information about a pending inquiry. The Commonwealth's interest in maintaining confidentiality of judicial disciplinary proceedings does not override the First Amendment interest in publishing truthful information about matters of significant public concern.
cases.notes: Landmark Communications extended the principle from Cox Broadcasting v. Cohn — that the press may publish truthful information lawfully obtained about matters of public concern — to judicial disciplinary proceedings.
