The case is part of a line of decisions establishing that government cannot impose criminal liability on the press for accurately reporting on official proceedings, even when those proceedings are nominally confidential.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Florida's trespass conviction of civil rights demonstrators who marched to a county jail and refused to leave when ordered by the sheriff did not violate the First Amendment. The jailhouse grounds are not a public forum — they are government property that is not traditionally open to public expression. Government, no less than private owners, may exclude people from property that is not opened for general public use.

opinions.author: Douglas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Douglas argued that the right to petition the government — including its jails — for redress of grievances is among the most precious First Amendment rights and that the majority's distinction between different types of government property undermined the public forum doctrine.

cases.notes: Adderley v. Florida is foundational for the nonpublic forum category — government property that is not traditionally open to public expression may be closed to expressive activity. The case established that not all government property is a public forum and that the government may restrict access to property like jails, military bases, and similar facilities. Perry Education Association v. Perry (1983) systematized this principle into the three-category public forum framework.

cases.short_name: International Society for Krishna Consciousness v. Lee
cases.full_name: International Society for Krishna Consciousness, Inc. v. Lee
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Airport terminals operated by a public authority are not public forums. The Port Authority may prohibit the repetitive solicitation of money inside airport terminals. Airports are not traditional public forums — they have not historically been used for expressive activity and were not opened by the government for such purposes. Reasonable and viewpoint-neutral restrictions on expressive activity in nonpublic forums are constitutional.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kennedy argued that airports are functionally equivalent to traditional public forums because they serve as major hubs of public discourse, and that the majority's historical test for identifying public forums is too narrow.

cases.notes: ISKCON v. Lee held airports are nonpublic forums, permitting solicitation bans. On the same day the Court struck a ban on distribution of literature in the same airports (Lee v. ISKCON) — distinguishing solicitation (properly banned) from leafleting (protected even in nonpublic forums). The two cases together establish that not all expressive activities receive the same treatment even within the same forum.

cases.short_name: Lehman v. City of Shaker Heights
cases.full_name: Lehman v. City of Shaker Heights
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city transit system may refuse to display political advertising in its buses while accepting commercial advertising. Captive audiences on public transit have a right not to be subjected to political messages they cannot avoid. The city has a legitimate interest in maintaining its advertising program free from political propaganda that could embarrass the transit system or create the appearance of political endorsement.
cases.notes: Lehman v. City of Shaker Heights introduced the captive audience doctrine into public forum analysis — the idea that when people cannot avoid hearing or seeing a message, their interest in not being subjected to it may outweigh the speaker's interest in communicating it. The case is limited to its specific context (transit advertising) and its reasoning has not been broadly extended.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Smith Act's prohibition on advocacy of violent overthrow of the government must be distinguished between advocacy of abstract doctrine and advocacy of action. The trial court's instructions failed to distinguish between advocacy of abstract doctrine — which is protected — and advocacy directed at promoting unlawful action — which may be punished. Convictions of Communist Party members for organizing and conspiring to advocate overthrow are reversed where the advocacy charged was of ideas rather than of action.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black and Douglas concurred in part and dissented in part, arguing the Smith Act was entirely unconstitutional — a more absolutist First Amendment position that foreshadowed the Warren/Burger Court's ultimate shift in Brandenburg.

cases.notes: Yates is the critical bridge case between Dennis (1951) and Brandenburg (1969) in the incitement doctrine's evolution. Without formally overruling Dennis, the Court gutted it by requiring that prosecution prove advocacy of illegal action rather than mere abstract advocacy of dangerous ideas. This distinction — abstract doctrine versus action advocacy — became central to Brandenburg's reformulation of the incitement standard twelve years later. Yates effectively rendered the Smith Act unenforceable against Communist Party members who stuck to theoretical advocacy.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A town sign code that imposed different restrictions on temporary directional signs, ideological signs, and political signs based on their content is a content-based regulation subject to strict scrutiny. A law that singles out a specific subject matter for differential treatment is content-based on its face, regardless of the government's benign motive or the lack of evidence of viewpoint discrimination. Content-based laws — those that target speech based on its communicative content — are presumptively unconstitutional and may be justified only if they are narrowly tailored to serve compelling government interests.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Alito's concurrence emphasized that Reed should not be read to subject all content-based distinctions to strict scrutiny — many ordinary regulations (traffic laws, noise ordinances, etc.) necessarily differentiate based on subject matter without raising serious First Amendment concerns.

opinions.author: Kagan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kagan argued the majority's categorical approach was too broad and would subject a vast range of ordinary regulations to strict scrutiny, preferring a more functional inquiry into whether the regulation actually threatens First Amendment values.

cases.notes: Reed v. Town of Gilbert significantly hardened and broadened the content-based/content-neutral distinction established in Police Department of Chicago v. Mosley (1972) and refined through subsequent cases. The decision is now one of the most frequently cited First Amendment cases in lower court litigation because it applies strict scrutiny to any law that facially distinguishes based on subject matter, regardless of motive. The concurrences signal ongoing disagreement about how broadly Reed should be read — whether any subject-matter distinction triggers strict scrutiny or only those that actually threaten free expression values.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Millionaire's Amendment to BCRA — which raised contribution limits for candidates whose opponents spend more than $350,000 of personal funds — violates the First Amendment. A candidate has a First Amendment right to spend personal funds on their own campaign, and this right cannot be burdened by a scheme that advantages opponents based on the candidate's decision to exercise that right. The government may not impose a penalty on a candidate for spending personal funds, even if the penalty takes the form of asymmetric contribution limits rather than a direct cap.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stevens argued the Millionaire's Amendment was a reasonable anti-corruption measure that leveled the playing field without directly capping expenditures — the asymmetric structure should be constitutional because it leaves the self-funding candidate free to spend as much as desired.

cases.notes: Davis v. FEC is the most directly on-point case for personal expenditure caps on self-funded candidates. It established that the government cannot penalize a candidate for exercising the First Amendment right to spend personal funds, even indirectly through asymmetric regulatory schemes. The decision extends Buckley v. Valeo's protection of candidate expenditures and forecloses legislative attempts to level the playing field between wealthy self-funded candidates and candidates dependent on contributions.

cases.short_name: Arizona Free Enterprise Club v. Bennett
cases.full_name: Arizona Free Enterprise Club's Freedom Club PAC v. Bennett
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Arizona's public campaign finance system, which provided matching funds to publicly financed candidates when their privately financed opponents spent beyond a threshold, violated the First Amendment. Triggered matching funds impose a burden on privately financed candidates' political speech by requiring them to choose between spending more and thereby triggering additional public funds for their opponents. The anti-corruption interest does not justify a scheme that directly penalizes a candidate's decision to speak.

opinions.author: Kagan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kagan argued the majority mischaracterized the matching funds as a burden on speech — they simply subsidize more speech, which is what the First Amendment should encourage. A law that results in more political speech cannot be a First Amendment violation.

cases.notes: Arizona Free Enterprise Club v. Bennett applied Davis v. FEC's reasoning to triggered matching fund systems, holding that any mechanism that penalizes privately financed candidates for exercising their First Amendment right to spend violates the Constitution. Together with Davis, this case forecloses most legislative attempts to reduce the influence of wealthy candidates through asymmetric or reactive funding schemes. The decision confirmed that the anti-corruption rationale cannot justify regulations that burden expenditure decisions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First and Fourteenth Amendments prohibit making the private possession of obscene material a crime. A person has the right to receive information and ideas regardless of their social worth, and that right is fundamental to a free society. The state has no legitimate interest in controlling what a person reads or views in the privacy of his own home.
cases.notes: Stanley v. Georgia established a constitutional right to possess obscene material in one's home, rooted in privacy and the First Amendment. However, the Court has consistently refused to extend Stanley to commercial distribution, sale, or transportation of obscene material. Paris Adult Theatre I v. Slaton (1973) explicitly held that Stanley does not create a right to receive obscene material through commercial channels or to view it in adult theaters. The private-possession right established in Stanley therefore has narrow practical reach — it protects personal possession at home but does not immunize the commercial distribution chain that gets obscene material into private hands.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state supreme court rule restricting attorney advertising to specified categories of information and prohibiting mailings to persons other than lawyers violates the First Amendment as applied to an attorney who deviated from the prescribed forms in truthful, non-deceptive ways. The state must demonstrate that its restrictions on commercial speech directly and substantially advance a substantial interest and are no more extensive than necessary — the mere possibility that some advertising might be misleading is insufficient to justify categorical prohibition of non-misleading advertising.
cases.notes: In Re R.M.J. is the first post-Central Hudson application of the four-part test to attorney advertising, establishing that truthful, non-deceptive professional advertising is protected and that states must demonstrate actual harm from the specific type of advertising restricted rather than relying on generalized concerns about potential deception.

cases.full_name: Members of the City Council of the City of Los Angeles v. Taxpayers for Vincent
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Los Angeles ordinance prohibiting the posting of signs on public property does not violate the First Amendment. The ordinance is a content-neutral regulation of the time, place, and manner of expression that serves the substantial government interest in eliminating visual clutter and blight. It leaves open ample alternative channels of communication and is no broader than necessary to advance the aesthetic interest.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued the ordinance was not sufficiently narrowly tailored because it was a total ban on a traditional form of political communication — posted signs — without any showing of actual harm from such signs.

cases.notes: Taxpayers for Vincent is the foundational case for content-neutral sign ordinances on public property, establishing that aesthetic interests can constitute a substantial government interest sufficient to justify restrictions on posted signs. The case is frequently cited alongside Metromedia v. San Diego in sign ordinance litigation. City of Ladue v. Gilleo (1994) distinguished Taxpayers for Vincent in the residential sign context.

cases.short_name: Bolger v. Youngs Drug Products Corp.
cases.full_name: Bolger v. Youngs Drug Products Corp.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A federal statute prohibiting the unsolicited mailing of contraceptive advertisements violates the First Amendment as applied to truthful, non-deceptive commercial speech. The Court applied the Central Hudson four-part test and found the government failed to demonstrate that the prohibition directly and materially advanced its interest in protecting recipients from offensive mail and shielding minors from exposure to contraceptive information. The fact that recipients can protect themselves by discarding unwanted mail undermines the government's interest.
cases.notes: Bolger v. Youngs Drug Products is significant for applying the Central Hudson test to strike down a federal prohibition on unsolicited contraceptive advertising and for clarifying that material does not lose First Amendment protection merely because it is commercial speech that some recipients find offensive. The case also addressed the definition of commercial speech, holding that mailings combining commercial and informational content can still qualify as commercial speech.

cases.short_name: Zauderer v. Office of Disciplinary Counsel
cases.full_name: Zauderer v. Office of Disciplinary Counsel of the Supreme Court of Ohio
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Ohio's prohibition on attorney advertising using illustrations violates the First Amendment as applied to truthful, non-deceptive print advertisements. However, a state may require attorneys who advertise contingency fee arrangements to disclose that clients may be liable for court costs. Compelled disclosure of purely factual and uncontroversial commercial information is subject to a less demanding standard than restrictions on commercial speech — the government need only show the disclosure is reasonably related to the State's interest in preventing deception of consumers.
cases.notes: Zauderer established the foundational distinction between restrictions on commercial speech (governed by Central Hudson) and compelled commercial disclosures (governed by a more lenient rational basis-like standard requiring only that the disclosure be reasonably related to preventing consumer deception). This Zauderer standard has been applied extensively in product labeling, financial disclosure, and professional licensing contexts. NIFLA v. Becerra (2018) limited Zauderer by holding it applies only to purely factual and uncontroversial disclosures, not ideologically contested ones.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not categorically prohibit lawyers from sending targeted direct-mail solicitations to potential clients known to face specific legal problems. The First Amendment protects truthful, non-deceptive targeted direct mail advertising by attorneys. The mere possibility that some recipients might find such solicitations intrusive or that attorneys might engage in false statements does not justify a categorical ban on a form of communication that is less likely to create the coercive pressure that justifies restrictions on in-person solicitation under Ohralik.
cases.notes: Shapero extended the attorney advertising protection from Bates v. State Bar of Arizona and In Re R.M.J. to targeted direct mail, distinguishing it from in-person solicitation (prohibited in Ohralik) because written communications lack the coercive, hard-to-resist quality of face-to-face encounters. Florida Bar v. Went for It, Inc. (1995) later upheld a 30-day ban on direct mail to accident victims under Central Hudson, creating a limited exception.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city ordinance prohibiting homeowners from displaying signs on their property, with narrow exceptions, violates the First Amendment. Residential signs are an important and distinctive medium of expression, particularly for political and religious messages. The ordinance's near-total prohibition on this form of speech forecloses a venerable means of communication that is both unique and important — the home is the one place where a person can speak to the world without relying on the government's permission to use public forums or the owner's permission to use private property.
cases.notes: City of Ladue v. Gilleo distinguished Taxpayers for Vincent (which upheld a ban on signs on public property) by emphasizing the unique significance of the home as a forum for expression. The Court avoided the content-neutrality question by focusing on the ordinance's total prohibition of a distinctive communication medium. Reed v. Town of Gilbert (2015) subsequently hardened the content-based/content-neutral analysis that applies when sign ordinances make distinctions based on message type.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Florida's ban on in-person solicitation by certified public accountants violates the First Amendment under the Central Hudson test. The state failed to demonstrate that the ban directly and materially advances its interests in protecting consumers from fraud and maintaining CPA independence. Unlike attorney in-person solicitation in Ohralik, CPA solicitation typically occurs in a business context between sophisticated parties, not in circumstances where overreaching is likely.
cases.notes: Edenfield v. Fane is significant for articulating Central Hudson's third prong requirement with precision: the government must demonstrate that its restriction will actually advance the asserted interest in a direct and material way — speculation or conjecture is insufficient. The decision distinguished Ohralik v. Ohio State Bar (upholding attorney in-person solicitation bans) based on the different context of professional-to-business solicitation.

cases.short_name: City of Cincinnati v. Discovery Network
cases.full_name: City of Cincinnati v. Discovery Network, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city ordinance revoking permits for commercial newsracks while allowing newsracks for traditional newspapers violates the First Amendment. The city's distinction between commercial and non-commercial publications is not a valid content-neutral justification under Central Hudson because the differential treatment bears no relationship to the city's interest in safety and aesthetics. A regulation is not content-neutral merely because it targets commercial speech — the government must still demonstrate the restriction directly advances a substantial interest.
cases.notes: City of Cincinnati v. Discovery Network is significant for holding that the commercial/non-commercial distinction cannot serve as a sufficient basis for differential treatment under the First Amendment — content discrimination targeting commercial speech is still content discrimination and must be justified under Central Hudson's framework. The decision reinforced that regulations of commercial speech must directly advance the asserted government interest, not merely correlate with it.

cases.short_name: Florida Bar v. Went for It, Inc.
cases.full_name: Florida Bar v. Went for It, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Florida Bar rule prohibiting lawyers from sending targeted direct-mail solicitations to accident victims and their relatives for 30 days following an accident survives First Amendment scrutiny under the Central Hudson test. The state has a substantial interest in protecting the privacy of accident victims and in maintaining the professionalism of the legal profession. A 30-day waiting period is narrowly tailored to serve these interests without unduly burdening the overall flow of commercial information.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kennedy argued the majority improperly credited the state's speculative evidence about harms from attorney solicitation and that the decision undermined Central Hudson's requirement of a direct and material relationship between the restriction and the asserted interest.

cases.notes: Florida Bar v. Went for It, Inc. is the primary example of a commercial speech restriction that survived Central Hudson scrutiny in the attorney advertising context, creating a limited exception to Shapero's protection of targeted direct mail. The decision is notable for accepting empirical evidence (consumer surveys, anecdotal reports) as sufficient to demonstrate direct advancement of the state's interest, a more lenient approach than Edenfield's demand for concrete proof.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A provision of the Federal Alcohol Administration Act prohibiting beer labels from displaying alcohol content violates the First Amendment under Central Hudson. The government's interest in preventing strength wars among brewers is substantial, but the prohibition fails Central Hudson's fourth prong because it is more extensive than necessary — the government's own regulations affirmatively required disclosure of alcohol content in wines and spirits, demonstrating that less restrictive alternatives existed.
cases.notes: Rubin v. Coors Brewing Co. applied Central Hudson to strike a federal alcohol labeling restriction, finding the government's regulatory scheme internally inconsistent — it prohibited alcohol content disclosures on beer labels while requiring them on wine and spirits. This inconsistency was fatal under Central Hudson's fourth prong because it demonstrated the restriction was more extensive than necessary. The case is frequently cited for the proposition that the government cannot demonstrate narrow tailoring when its own regulatory scheme contradicts the asserted rationale.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A New York statute prohibiting merchants from displaying the cash price of a good alongside a credit card surcharge regulates speech, not conduct. The statute controls how merchants communicate their prices — specifically, it restricts the message that the higher price is a surcharge for credit card use rather than a discount for cash payment. Because the statute regulates a merchant's communication of price information, it is subject to First Amendment scrutiny.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Breyer argued the case presented a simple commercial disclosure regulation that should be evaluated under Zauderer's rational basis standard rather than Central Hudson's intermediate scrutiny.

cases.notes: Expressions Hair Design is significant for holding that price disclosure laws that regulate how prices are communicated — not just what prices may be charged — regulate speech and are subject to First Amendment scrutiny. The Court vacated and remanded for the lower courts to determine the appropriate level of scrutiny under Central Hudson or Zauderer. The case sits at the intersection of commercial speech and compelled disclosure doctrine.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Lanham Act's disparagement clause violates the First Amendment. Trademarks are private speech, not government speech, and the disparagement clause discriminates on the basis of viewpoint. The Court split on the application of the commercial speech doctrine: a plurality found that the clause failed even intermediate scrutiny under Central Hudson, while a concurring plurality argued that viewpoint discrimination triggers strict scrutiny and renders the commercial speech inquiry entirely unnecessary.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kennedy's concurrence took a broader view that the disparagement clause was straightforwardly viewpoint discriminatory and that viewpoint discrimination is unconstitutional regardless of the forum or the nature of the speech.

cases.notes: Matal v. Tam is significant for holding that trademark registration is private speech subject to full First Amendment protection and that viewpoint discrimination in trademark registration is unconstitutional. The decision resolved a circuit split and set up Iancu v. Brunetti (2019), which struck the Lanham Act's prohibition on immoral or scandalous trademarks on the same viewpoint discrimination rationale.

cases.short_name: City of Austin v. Reagan National Advertising
cases.full_name: City of Austin v. Reagan National Advertising of Austin, LLC
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Austin's sign ordinance distinguishing between on-premises and off-premises signs is a content-neutral regulation subject to intermediate scrutiny. Clarifying Reed v. Town of Gilbert, the Court held that a regulation is not automatically content-based simply because one must read the sign to determine if it applies. Reading a sign to determine its location-based context (on/off premises) is content-neutral, whereas reading a sign to restrict its substantive topic or viewpoint is content-based.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Thomas argued that the on/off premises distinction is inherently content-based because determining whether a sign advertises an on-site or off-site business requires reading the sign's message.

cases.notes: City of Austin v. Reagan National Advertising is the most recent significant billboard regulation case, clarifying that Reed v. Town of Gilbert's content-based definition applies only when a law requires examining a message's communicative content — not when it examines location or context. The decision limits Reed's reach and provides guidance for sign ordinances that regulate based on non-communicative factors like location. The case directly follows the Metromedia/Ladue/Taxpayers for Vincent sign ordinance lineage.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: African Americans, whether enslaved or free, were not citizens of the United States and had no standing to sue in federal court. Congress lacked power to prohibit slavery in the territories — the Missouri Compromise was unconstitutional. A slaveholder's property rights in enslaved persons were protected by the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause, which prevented Congress from depriving slaveholders of their property without due process of law.

opinions.author: Curtis
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Curtis's dissent demonstrated that free Black persons had been citizens of several states at the founding and thus were citizens of the United States, directly refuting Taney's historical claims. The dissent is considered one of the greatest in Supreme Court history.

cases.notes: Dred Scott is widely considered the Supreme Court's worst decision. It was effectively overruled by the Thirteenth Amendment (1865), which abolished slavery, and definitively overruled by the Fourteenth Amendment (1868), whose first sentence — 'All persons born or naturalized in the United States are citizens' — directly repudiated Taney's holding on citizenship. The case is foundational to understanding the constitutional history that produced the Fourteenth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A West Virginia statute limiting jury service to white male citizens violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment as applied to a Black defendant convicted by an all-white jury. The Fourteenth Amendment was designed to secure freedom and equal civil rights to recently emancipated slaves, and a state law that excludes Black citizens from jury service because of their race denies Black defendants equal protection.
cases.notes: Strauder v. West Virginia is the first significant Equal Protection Clause case, establishing that the Fourteenth Amendment prohibits racial discrimination in jury selection. The Court read the Amendment's purpose broadly as ensuring that Black citizens could participate fully in civic life. The decision preceded by decades the development of the tiered scrutiny framework but established the foundational principle that race-based state action violates equal protection.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Louisiana's Separate Car Act, which required racial segregation in railroad cars, does not violate the Equal Protection Clause. The Fourteenth Amendment was intended to enforce the absolute equality of the two races before the law, but it was not intended to abolish distinctions based on color or to enforce social equality. Separate but equal accommodations do not deprive Black passengers of equal protection because the underlying assumption of racial inferiority is a fallacy — if one race finds the separation badge of inferiority, it is by the race itself, not by anything in the law.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Harlan's dissent — 'Our Constitution is color-blind' — argued that the Constitution permits no caste distinctions and that the majority's reasoning was inconsistent with the Fourteenth Amendment's purpose. It was adopted as law in Brown v. Board of Education 58 years later.

cases.notes: Plessy v. Ferguson established the separate but equal doctrine that governed racial segregation law for 58 years. It was definitively overruled by Brown v. Board of Education (1954). Justice Harlan's lone dissent is one of the most celebrated in Supreme Court history — his prediction that the majority's decision would prove as pernicious as Dred Scott was vindicated.

cases.full_name: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Racial segregation in public schools violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The separate but equal doctrine of Plessy v. Ferguson has no place in the field of public education. Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal because segregation generates a feeling of inferiority in Black children that may affect their hearts and minds in a way unlikely ever to be undone. The Court relied on psychological evidence of the harm caused by segregation rather than solely on historical analysis of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: Brown v. Board of Education is the most important Equal Protection decision and one of the most consequential Supreme Court decisions in American history. Chief Justice Warren secured unanimity through extensive negotiation among the justices. Brown II (1955) ordered desegregation to proceed with all deliberate speed. The decision overruled Plessy v. Ferguson's separate but equal doctrine and launched the modern civil rights era.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Racial segregation in the public schools of the District of Columbia violates the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment. Although the Fifth Amendment contains no equal protection clause, the concepts of equal protection and due process are not mutually exclusive — discrimination may be so unjustifiable as to be violative of due process. It would be unthinkable that the same Constitution that prohibits state-imposed segregation under the Fourteenth Amendment would impose a lesser duty on the federal government.
cases.notes: Bolling v. Sharpe is the companion case to Brown v. Board of Education, decided the same day. Because the Fourteenth Amendment applies only to states, the federal government's segregation of DC schools required a separate constitutional basis. Warren rooted the prohibition in the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause, reading an implicit equal protection requirement into it — a holding that remains the basis for applying equal protection principles to federal action.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The exclusion of persons of Japanese ancestry from the West Coast during World War II is constitutional. All legal restrictions which curtail the civil rights of a single racial group are immediately suspect and subject to the most rigid scrutiny. However, when under conditions of modern warfare the military urgency is so great that compulsory exclusion is necessary for the properly constituted military authorities to discharge their duty, the courts will not disturb their decision.

opinions.author: Murphy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Murphy argued the exclusion order fell into the ugly abyss of racism and that the military necessity rationale was a legalization of racism that had no reasonable relation to military necessity.

opinions.author: Jackson
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Jackson argued the Court should not validate military orders that violate constitutional rights, warning that a military order validated by the Court becomes a doctrine of the Constitution and lies about like a loaded weapon ready for any authority that brings a plausible need.

cases.notes: Korematsu is the foundational case for the strict scrutiny standard — the first time the Court used that label for racial classifications — but it is also the most discredited application of the standard, upholding Japanese American internment. Trump v. Hawaii (2018) expressly overruled Korematsu, with Chief Justice Roberts stating it was gravely wrong the day it was decided. Despite being overruled, Korematsu remains essential to understanding the development of equal protection strict scrutiny.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Virginia's anti-miscegenation statute, which prohibited and punished interracial marriages, violates both the Equal Protection Clause and the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The Equal Protection Clause requires that racial classifications be subjected to the most rigid scrutiny. Virginia's statute rests solely on distinctions drawn according to race and can survive strict scrutiny only if shown to be necessary to the accomplishment of some permissible state objective. Preserving racial integrity and preventing the corruption of blood are not legitimate state purposes.
cases.notes: Loving v. Virginia struck down anti-miscegenation laws in 16 states and established that the freedom to marry is a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clause as well as an equal protection right. The case is cited in virtually every subsequent equal protection and due process case involving marriage rights, including Obergefell v. Hodges (2015). Warren wrote both equal protection and due process grounds, giving the decision unusual breadth.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A facially neutral law does not violate the Equal Protection Clause solely because it has a racially disproportionate impact. Proof of racially discriminatory intent or purpose is required to show a violation of the Equal Protection Clause. Disproportionate impact may be relevant evidence of discriminatory intent but is not sufficient by itself to establish a constitutional violation. A written personnel test used by the DC police department that disqualified Black applicants at a higher rate than white applicants did not violate the Fifth Amendment's equal protection component absent proof of discriminatory purpose.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued that the discriminatory impact of the test was itself sufficient evidence of discriminatory purpose and that the majority's standard would immunize from constitutional challenge practices that perpetuate racial inequality.

cases.notes: Washington v. Davis is among the most consequential and criticized equal protection decisions. By requiring proof of discriminatory intent rather than allowing disparate impact alone to trigger strict scrutiny, the Court made it substantially more difficult to challenge systemic racial inequality embedded in facially neutral laws. The decision has been extensively criticized for insulating from constitutional scrutiny practices that perpetuate historic racial discrimination.

cases.short_name: Village of Arlington Heights v. MHDC
cases.full_name: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A village's refusal to rezone property for low-income housing did not violate the Equal Protection Clause absent proof of racially discriminatory intent. Discriminatory intent need not be the only purpose behind official action, but it must be a motivating factor. Courts may consider the following factors in determining whether discriminatory purpose motivated an official decision: the discriminatory impact of the official action, the historical background, the sequence of events leading to the challenged decision, departures from normal procedural and substantive standards, and legislative or administrative history.
cases.notes: Arlington Heights elaborated the Washington v. Davis discriminatory intent requirement by providing a multi-factor framework for proving intent from circumstantial evidence when direct evidence is unavailable. The Arlington Heights factors — impact, historical background, sequence of events, procedural departures, legislative history — have become the standard analytical framework for discriminatory intent claims involving facially neutral laws.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Statistical evidence showing that Black defendants convicted of killing white victims are significantly more likely to receive the death penalty than similarly situated defendants does not establish a constitutional violation under the Equal Protection Clause or the Eighth Amendment. To prevail on an equal protection claim, a defendant must prove that the decision-makers in his specific case acted with discriminatory purpose. System-wide statistical disparities, even if valid, do not prove discriminatory intent in any individual case.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued that the Baldus study demonstrated the risk that race infected the capital sentencing process and that the majority's demand for proof of individual discriminatory intent was impossible to satisfy and shielded systemic racial bias from constitutional scrutiny.

cases.notes: McCleskey v. Kemp applied Washington v. Davis to the capital punishment context, holding that the Baldus study — which showed a significant statistical correlation between race and death sentences in Georgia — was insufficient to prove discriminatory intent in McCleskey's individual case. Justice Powell later said McCleskey was the decision he most regretted. The case is widely considered the effective end of statistical equal protection challenges to systemic racial disparities in criminal justice.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: All racial classifications imposed by federal, state, or local governmental actors must be analyzed under strict scrutiny regardless of whether the classification is intended to burden or benefit a racial minority. The Fifth Amendment's equal protection component imposes the same strict scrutiny standard on federal affirmative action programs as the Fourteenth Amendment imposes on state programs. Metro Broadcasting v. FCC (1990) is overruled to the extent it held that benign federal racial classifications require only intermediate scrutiny.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stevens argued that the majority's insistence on treating all racial classifications identically ignored the difference between a caste system and a system of remediation — the Constitution's original prohibition on racial caste does not require treating attempts to dismantle that caste identically to attempts to enforce it.

cases.notes: Adarand established that strict scrutiny applies to all racial classifications by all levels of government regardless of the classification's purpose. The three-part Adarand framework — skepticism, consistency, congruence — requires that all racial classifications be treated with skepticism, that the same standard apply regardless of the race burdened or benefited, and that the standard be congruent across federal and state actors.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The University of Michigan Law School's race-conscious admissions program, which considered race as one factor among many in a holistic review of each application, survives strict scrutiny. Student body diversity is a compelling governmental interest that can justify the use of race in university admissions. The Law School's narrowly tailored program — which considered race as a plus factor but did not use quotas or point systems — satisfies the narrow tailoring requirement. The Court expected that racial preferences in admissions would no longer be necessary within 25 years.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Thomas argued that diversity is not a compelling interest capable of justifying racial discrimination and that the majority's decision was tantamount to an endorsement of racial discrimination in the name of racial discrimination.

cases.notes: Grutter v. Bollinger upheld holistic race-conscious admissions at the University of Michigan Law School, establishing student body diversity as a compelling interest under strict scrutiny. Companion case Gratz v. Bollinger (2003) struck the undergraduate point system. Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard (2023) overruled Grutter, holding that race-conscious admissions programs are unconstitutional because they lack measurable objectives, use race as a negative, and are not limited in time.

cases.short_name: Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard
cases.full_name: Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President and Fellows of Harvard College
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Race-conscious admissions programs at Harvard College and the University of North Carolina violate the Equal Protection Clause. The programs fail strict scrutiny because they lack sufficiently focused and measurable objectives that would allow for judicial review, unavoidably employ race in a negative manner, involve racial stereotyping, and have no logical endpoint. Grutter v. Bollinger is overruled. Universities may still consider how race affected an applicant's life through discrimination, inspiration, or otherwise — but race itself cannot be a factor in admissions decisions.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Sotomayor argued the majority cements a superficial rule of colorblindness as a constitutional principle in an endemically segregated society and effectively overrules decades of precedent permitting narrowly tailored race-conscious admissions programs.

cases.notes: SFFA v. Harvard is the most significant equal protection decision since Grutter, effectively ending race-conscious admissions in higher education. The decision overrules Grutter v. Bollinger (2003) and Gratz v. Bollinger (2003) and marks a fundamental shift in affirmative action doctrine. Roberts's majority opinion preserves a narrow exception for applicants who discuss how race has affected their personal experiences.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An Idaho statute giving mandatory preference to males over females as administrators of decedents' estates violates the Equal Protection Clause. The Equal Protection Clause denies to states the power to legislate that different treatment be accorded to persons placed by a statute in the same class on the basis of criteria wholly unrelated to the objective of that statute. A classification must be reasonable and not arbitrary and must rest upon some ground of difference having a fair and substantial relation to the object of the legislation.
cases.notes: Reed v. Reed is the first case in which the Supreme Court struck down a sex-based classification under the Equal Protection Clause. The Court applied rational basis review but with unusual rigor, finding no substantial relation between the sex preference and the objective of reducing probate court workload. The decision opened the door to heightened scrutiny for sex classifications, though the Court did not formally adopt intermediate scrutiny until Craig v. Boren (1976). Ruth Bader Ginsburg argued the case for the ACLU.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Federal statutes allowing male service members to claim their wives as dependents automatically while requiring female service members to prove their husbands' dependency violate the equal protection component of the Fifth Amendment. A four-Justice plurality would have treated sex as a suspect classification requiring strict scrutiny. The judgment invalidating the statutes commanded a broader majority, but the Court did not produce a majority opinion adopting strict scrutiny for sex classifications.

opinions.author: Stewart
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stewart concurred in the judgment under Reed v. Reed without joining Brennan's plurality opinion that would have elevated sex to a suspect classification.

opinions.author: Powell
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Powell declined to reach the suspect classification question, arguing the Equal Rights Amendment was pending ratification and the Court should not preempt the democratic process on this fundamental constitutional question.

cases.notes: Frontiero is the high-water mark of the effort to make sex a suspect classification requiring strict scrutiny. Brennan's plurality opinion garnered only four votes — one short of a majority. The ERA's pending ratification persuaded Powell not to join, and strict scrutiny for sex has never commanded a majority. The case settled that sex discrimination by the federal government violates the Fifth Amendment's equal protection component.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An Oklahoma statute prohibiting the sale of 3.2% beer to males under 21 and females under 18 violates the Equal Protection Clause. To withstand constitutional challenge under the Equal Protection Clause, classifications by gender must serve important governmental objectives and must be substantially related to achievement of those objectives. Statistical evidence showing that young men are more frequently arrested for drunk driving does not substantially relate the gender classification to the state's traffic safety objective.

opinions.author: Powell
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Powell agreed with the result and the standard but questioned whether the Court had clearly articulated what the intermediate scrutiny standard means in practice.

cases.notes: Craig v. Boren formally established intermediate scrutiny as the constitutional standard for sex-based classifications — important governmental objective, substantially related classification. The case settled the standard that Frontiero's plurality had failed to establish as a majority position. The decision was somewhat unusual procedurally because the original plaintiff (a male under 21) had turned 21 by the time of decision, requiring the Court to address standing through the beer vendor.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Virginia's exclusion of women from the Virginia Military Institute violates the Equal Protection Clause. Parties seeking to uphold government action that classifies individuals on the basis of sex must demonstrate an exceedingly persuasive justification for the classification. The justification must be genuine, not hypothesized or invented post hoc, and it must not rely on overbroad generalizations about the different talents, capacities, or preferences of males and females. Virginia's proposed remedy — a separate Virginia Women's Institute for Leadership — is not an adequate substitute for VMI's unique educational opportunities.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority had effectively applied strict scrutiny to a sex classification while pretending to apply intermediate scrutiny, and that the exceedingly persuasive justification standard had no clear content.

cases.notes: United States v. Virginia strengthened intermediate scrutiny for sex classifications by adding the exceedingly persuasive justification requirement — a standard that approaches but does not formally reach strict scrutiny. Justice Ginsburg's opinion made clear that sex classifications cannot rest on overbroad generalizations or stereotypes about gender roles. The decision is widely understood as having elevated the effective rigor of sex discrimination review without formally changing the doctrinal tier.

cases.full_name: New York City Transit Authority v. Beazer
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A New York City Transit Authority rule categorically excluding persons on methadone maintenance from employment satisfies rational basis review under the Equal Protection Clause. The rule is not constitutionally invalid even though it may be unwise or based on incorrect assumptions about methadone users. The rule bears a rational relationship to legitimate employment goals of safety and efficiency. Equal protection does not require perfect fit between means and ends under rational basis review.
cases.notes: Beazer is a leading illustration of rational basis review's extreme deference — sustaining an employment exclusion that the dissenters argued was based on factual error and overbreadth. The case demonstrates that rational basis does not require empirical accuracy or narrow tailoring, only a conceivable rational relationship between the classification and any legitimate government purpose.

cases.short_name: U.S. Dept. of Agriculture v. Moreno
cases.full_name: United States Department of Agriculture v. Moreno
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A provision of the Food Stamp Act excluding households containing individuals unrelated to any other household member violates the equal protection component of the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause. If the constitutional conception of equal protection means anything, it must at the very least mean that a bare congressional desire to harm a politically unpopular group cannot constitute a legitimate governmental interest. The legislative history reveals that the classification was motivated by a desire to exclude hippie communes — a purpose that is not a legitimate government interest.
cases.notes: Moreno is a foundational rational basis with bite case — establishing that bare animus against an unpopular group is not a legitimate government interest. The case established that even under rational basis review, a classification motivated purely by hostility to a politically disfavored group cannot survive equal protection scrutiny. Moreno, Cleburne, and Romer form the trilogy of rational basis with bite decisions.

cases.short_name: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
cases.full_name: City of Cleburne, Texas v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Mental disability is not a quasi-suspect classification requiring intermediate scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause. However, Cleburne's denial of a special use permit for a group home for intellectually disabled persons violates rational basis review because it was based on irrational prejudice and fear. The city's asserted justifications — concerns about the reaction of neighbors, fears about students from a nearby school, and the location near a flood plain — were not legitimate rational bases but reflected nothing more than an irrational prejudice against the disabled.

opinions.author: Marshall
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Marshall argued the majority was applying something more demanding than ordinary rational basis review — effectively a sliding scale of scrutiny — and that the Court should acknowledge that disability warrants intermediate scrutiny rather than pretending to apply rational basis.

cases.notes: Cleburne is the most important rational basis with bite decision — striking a zoning decision on rational basis grounds while explicitly declining to make disability a quasi-suspect classification. The Court's application of rational basis was notably more searching than typical, leading Marshall to accuse the majority of covert heightened review. The case establishes that animus and irrational prejudice are not legitimate government interests even under the most deferential standard. The judgment was unanimous in result, but the reasoning reflected important differences — Marshall argued the Court was applying something more demanding than ordinary rational basis, while the majority insisted it was not.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Colorado's Amendment 2, which prohibited state and local governments from enacting any law protecting gay, lesbian, or bisexual persons from discrimination, violates the Equal Protection Clause under rational basis review. Amendment 2 classifies gay, lesbian, and bisexual persons not to further a proper legislative end but to make them unequal to everyone else — a bare desire to harm a politically unpopular group does not constitute a legitimate government interest. A law declaring that in general a certain class is unequal seems inexplicable by anything but animus.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority's analysis was logically incoherent — Colorado's law reflected legitimate moral disapproval of homosexual conduct, and the majority had invented an animus rationale to strike a democratically enacted law it found distasteful.

cases.notes: Romer v. Evans applied Moreno's animus principle to sexual orientation, holding that bare desire to harm gay and lesbian persons is not a legitimate government interest. The decision completed the Moreno-Cleburne-Romer rational basis with bite trilogy and became the foundational case for constitutional protection of gay rights, leading directly to Lawrence v. Texas (2003) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015). Kennedy's majority declined to identify sexual orientation as a suspect or quasi-suspect classification.

cases.short_name: Harper v. Virginia Board of Elections
cases.full_name: Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Virginia's poll tax as a condition of voting in state elections violates the Equal Protection Clause. The right to vote is a fundamental political right, preservative of all other rights, and wealth or fee-paying has no relation to voter qualifications. Once the franchise is granted to the electorate, lines may not be drawn that are inconsistent with equal protection.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black argued the Court's invalidation of the poll tax under equal protection was judicial legislation — the original Constitution explicitly permitted property qualifications for voting and the Twenty-Fourth Amendment addressed only federal elections, demonstrating the Framers' intent to leave state voting qualifications to the states.

cases.notes: Harper v. Virginia Board of Elections established voting as a fundamental right whose unequal distribution triggers strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause. The decision struck poll taxes in state elections (the Twenty-Fourth Amendment had already prohibited them in federal elections). Harper is the foundational case for the fundamental rights strand of equal protection doctrine, applied later in Reynolds v. Sims and Shapiro v. Thompson.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Equal Protection Clause requires that the seats in both houses of a bicameral state legislature be apportioned substantially on a population basis. The right to vote is too important in our free, democratic society to be stripped of judicial protection. Legislators represent people, not trees or acres. A citizen, a qualified voter, is no more nor no less so because he lives in the city or on the farm. The dilution of a citizen's vote in one legislative district compared to another is an unconstitutional denial of equal protection.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Harlan argued the Equal Protection Clause was never intended to apply to political apportionment and that the Court's decision was a flagrant violation of the principle that courts should not decide political questions.

cases.notes: Reynolds v. Sims established the one person, one vote principle for state legislative apportionment. The decision required both chambers of state legislatures to be apportioned by population, rejecting the federal analogy that would have allowed one chamber to represent geographic units. Warren later called Reynolds the most important decision of his tenure. The case triggered a massive reapportionment of state legislatures across the country.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: State statutes denying welfare assistance to residents who have not resided within the state for at least one year violate the Equal Protection Clause. The right to travel interstate is a fundamental right protected by the Constitution. A state law that conditions receipt of welfare benefits on prior residence penalizes the exercise of the constitutional right to travel by creating two classes of residents. Such a classification requires and cannot survive strict scrutiny unless shown to promote a compelling governmental interest.

opinions.author: Warren
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Warren argued that the constitutionally protected right to travel should not be derived from equal protection but from the Privileges and Immunities Clause, and that the majority's strict scrutiny analysis was too broad.

cases.notes: Shapiro v. Thompson established that welfare residency requirements that penalize the exercise of the right to travel interstate are subject to strict scrutiny and violate the Equal Protection Clause. The case is the foundation of the fundamental right to travel doctrine and the principle that conditioning government benefits on the relinquishment of a constitutional right triggers heightened scrutiny.

cases.full_name: San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Texas's system of financing public education through local property taxes, which results in wide disparities in per-pupil expenditures across school districts, does not violate the Equal Protection Clause. Education is not a fundamental right under the Constitution. Poverty is not a suspect classification. The Texas system bears a rational relationship to the legitimate state purpose of local control over public schools. The Court defers to legislative judgment on complex social and economic policy questions that do not involve suspect classifications or fundamental rights.

opinions.author: Marshall
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Marshall argued for a sliding scale approach to equal protection scrutiny calibrated to the importance of the interest and the degree of discrimination, and that the Court's refusal to recognize education as a fundamental right ignored its connection to the exercise of explicitly guaranteed constitutional rights like voting and free expression.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued that wealth discrimination in access to education required at least intermediate scrutiny and that the Texas system failed even that standard.

cases.notes: San Antonio ISD v. Rodriguez is the major limit on the fundamental rights strand of equal protection — holding that education is not a fundamental right under the federal Constitution and that wealth is not a suspect classification. The decision foreclosed federal equal protection challenges to school funding disparities and confined the fundamental rights doctrine to rights explicitly or implicitly guaranteed by the Constitution. Marshall's dissent arguing for a sliding scale scrutiny approach has been influential in academic literature but never adopted by the Court.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment requires states to license marriages between two people of the same sex and to recognize same-sex marriages validly performed out of state. The right to marry is a fundamental liberty protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, and the Equal Protection Clause reinforces this conclusion. The four principles identified as underlying the right to marry — individual autonomy, a unique two-person union, the safeguards afforded families and children, and the connection to the nation's social order — apply with equal force to same-sex couples.

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Roberts argued that the Constitution does not resolve the marriage question and that the majority's decision was an act of judicial will rather than legal judgment — removing the issue from democratic deliberation without constitutional warrant.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority's decision represented a threat to American democracy by removing a hotly debated social policy question from democratic resolution and substituting nine lawyers' judgment for that of the people.

cases.notes: Obergefell v. Hodges held that the fundamental right to marry extends to same-sex couples, grounding its holding in both the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment. Kennedy's opinion synthesized the liberty and equality strands of constitutional doctrine. The decision extended the Romer-Lawrence line of gay rights cases and relied heavily on Loving v. Virginia's recognition of marriage as a fundamental right. Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022) cast some uncertainty on Obergefell's continued vitality, though Dobbs expressly disclaimed any effect on Obergefell.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Texas statute making it a crime for two persons of the same sex to engage in certain intimate sexual conduct violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The liberty protected by the Constitution allows homosexual persons the right to make personal choices regarding their private sexual conduct without government intervention. Bowers v. Hardwick is overruled. The petitioners' right to liberty under the Due Process Clause gives them the full right to engage in their conduct without intervention of the government.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: O'Connor concurred on equal protection grounds rather than due process — the Texas statute applied only to same-sex couples, making it a discriminatory law that could not survive even rational basis review under Romer v. Evans. O'Connor would not have overruled Bowers.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority had signed onto the homosexual agenda, had effectively overruled morality-based legislation, and that the majority's logic would invalidate sodomy laws across the country and compel recognition of same-sex marriage — a prediction that proved accurate.

cases.notes: Lawrence v. Texas overruled Bowers v. Hardwick (1986) and held that intimate consensual sexual conduct between adults of the same sex is protected by the liberty guarantee of the Due Process Clause. The decision was grounded in substantive due process rather than equal protection, though O'Connor's concurrence suggested an equal protection path. Lawrence is the bridge case between Romer v. Evans (1996) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015) in the constitutional protection of gay rights. SCHEMA NOTE: Lawrence explicitly overruled Bowers v. Hardwick (1986) — add bowers_v_hardwick_1986 node and OVERRULES edge when Bowers is added.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Hawaii constitutional provision limiting the right to vote for trustees of the Office of Hawaiian Affairs to persons of Hawaiian ancestry violates the Fifteenth Amendment's prohibition on denying or abridging the right to vote on account of race. The ancestry classification in this context is a proxy for race and functions as a racial classification subject to strict constitutional scrutiny. The Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments apply to all persons regardless of ancestry.
cases.notes: Rice v. Cayetano applied the Fifteenth Amendment to strike a voting restriction based on Hawaiian ancestry, holding that ancestry is a proxy for race in this context. The decision complicated Hawaiian sovereignty arguments and the status of the Office of Hawaiian Affairs. The case is significant for the broader principle that ancestry-based classifications that function as racial proxies are subject to the same constitutional scrutiny as explicit racial classifications.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Equal protection challenges to partisan political gerrymandering are justiciable. The political question doctrine does not bar judicial review of claims that a legislative apportionment plan discriminates against an identifiable political group. However, the Indiana legislative reapportionment plan at issue does not constitute an unconstitutional partisan gerrymander — to succeed, plaintiffs must show both intentional discrimination against an identifiable political group and an actual discriminatory effect on that group's ability to influence the political process as a whole.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: O'Connor argued that partisan gerrymandering claims are non-justiciable political questions and that the plurality's standard for unconstitutional gerrymandering was unworkable.

cases.notes: Davis v. Bandemer held partisan gerrymandering claims justiciable but set a standard so demanding that no plaintiff ever succeeded under it. Vieth v. Jubelirer (2004) produced a plurality that would have overruled Bandemer, and Rucho v. Common Cause (2019) definitively held that federal courts lack jurisdiction over partisan gerrymandering claims, effectively overruling Bandemer's justiciability holding.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A four-justice plurality would hold that partisan gerrymandering claims present a non-justiciable political question because there are no judicially discoverable and manageable standards for adjudicating them. Justice Kennedy concurred in the judgment on the ground that no workable standard had been identified but declined to foreclose the possibility that such a standard might be developed in the future. Davis v. Bandemer's holding that partisan gerrymandering is justiciable was not overruled because Kennedy's concurrence declined to join the plurality's categorical holding.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kennedy's decisive concurrence left open the possibility that a workable standard for partisan gerrymandering might be developed, preventing the plurality from overruling Bandemer while signaling deep skepticism about justiciability.

cases.notes: Vieth v. Jubelirer produced a deeply fractured Court on partisan gerrymandering. Because Kennedy declined to join Scalia's plurality, Bandemer was not formally overruled. Rucho v. Common Cause (2019) ultimately held that partisan gerrymandering claims present non-justiciable political questions, ending the search for a workable standard that Kennedy had kept open.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Florida Supreme Court's order mandating manual recounts of undervotes in the 2000 presidential election violated the Equal Protection Clause because it lacked uniform standards for evaluating ballots. When a state grants the right to vote on equal terms, it may not value one person's vote over that of another through arbitrary and disparate treatment in the tabulation process. Because there was insufficient time to establish uniform standards before the federal safe harbor deadline, the recount could not continue. The per curiam opinion expressly limited its holding: 'Our consideration is limited to the present circumstances, for the problem of equal protection in election processes generally presents many complexities.'

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stevens argued the majority's equal protection analysis was unprecedented, that the Court had no basis for stopping the recount, and that the decision threatened public confidence in the judiciary.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Souter agreed there was an equal protection problem but argued the remedy should be to remand for establishment of uniform standards rather than ending the recount entirely.

cases.notes: Bush v. Gore is one of the most controversial Supreme Court decisions in modern history. The per curiam opinion expressly stated it was 'limited to the present circumstances,' which courts have widely read as a signal that the decision should not be treated as precedent beyond its specific facts. The decision effectively ended the Florida recount and decided the 2000 presidential election. Its equal protection holding — that standardless manual recounts violate equal protection — has rarely been applied by lower courts.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not condition the right to appeal a criminal conviction on the defendant's ability to pay for a trial transcript. Destitute defendants must be afforded as adequate appellate review as defendants who can afford to pay for the trial transcript. There can be no equal justice where the kind of trial a man gets depends on the amount of money he has. The Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses require that criminal procedure be fair to all defendants regardless of wealth.
cases.notes: Griffin v. Illinois is the foundational case for the access-to-courts strand of equal protection doctrine, establishing that states cannot condition meaningful access to criminal appellate review on a defendant's ability to pay. The decision drew on both equal protection and due process, often in combination — a fusion that became characteristic of fundamental rights cases. Douglas v. California (1963) extended Griffin to the right to appointed counsel on first appeal.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state procedure under which appointed counsel is denied to an indigent defendant on a first appeal as of right violates the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment. Where the merits of the one and only appeal an indigent has as of right are decided without benefit of counsel, there is inequality before the law in that an indigent, though entitled to appeal, does not have the right to have a lawyer argue on his behalf. The right to counsel on first appeal is constitutionally required.
cases.notes: Douglas v. California extended Griffin v. Illinois to the right to appointed counsel on first appeal as of right, holding that indigent defendants are entitled to counsel even on appeal. The decision is limited to first appeals as of right — Ross v. Moffitt (1974) held there is no constitutional right to appointed counsel for discretionary appeals or certiorari petitions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Maryland's maximum grant regulation, which limits welfare benefits regardless of family size, does not violate the Equal Protection Clause. The regulation bears a rational basis to Maryland's legitimate interest in encouraging employment and in allocating limited resources. In the area of economics and social welfare, a state does not violate the Equal Protection Clause merely because the classifications made by its laws are imperfect. The Constitution does not require the state to choose between attacking every aspect of a problem or not attacking the problem at all.

opinions.author: Marshall
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Marshall argued that welfare benefits are essential to survival and that the majority's rational basis analysis was insufficiently rigorous — the case should have received heightened scrutiny because the classification burdened the poor's ability to meet subsistence needs.

cases.notes: Dandridge v. Williams is the major equal protection precedent for the proposition that welfare benefit classifications receive only rational basis review — establishing that economic and social welfare legislation is subject to minimal scrutiny even when it affects the poor. The decision rejected the argument that poverty or welfare receipt should trigger heightened scrutiny, limiting the fundamental rights strand of equal protection. San Antonio ISD v. Rodriguez (1973) relied on Dandridge in rejecting education as a fundamental right.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A California law limiting new residents for one year to the welfare benefits they would have received in their prior state of residence violates the right to travel protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Privileges or Immunities Clause. The right to travel has three components: the right to enter and leave another state, the right to be treated as a welcome visitor in other states, and the right of a new resident to be treated like other citizens of the new state. The third component — the right of the newly arrived citizen — is protected by the Privileges or Immunities Clause, not merely by equal protection.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Rehnquist argued the majority was wrong to revive the Privileges or Immunities Clause, which had been dormant since the Slaughterhouse Cases (1873), and that the right to travel was adequately protected by equal protection doctrine.

cases.notes: Saenz v. Roe is constitutionally significant for reviving the Privileges or Immunities Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment as an independent source of constitutional rights — the first significant application of that clause since the Slaughterhouse Cases (1873) gutted it. The decision grounded the right to travel in the Privileges or Immunities Clause rather than equal protection, representing a major doctrinal shift in how the right to travel is conceptualized.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Constitution does not confer a fundamental right upon homosexuals to engage in sodomy. The Court declined to announce a fundamental right to engage in homosexual sodomy in the face of longstanding criminal proscription of such conduct. Hardwick's asserted fundamental right — to engage in consensual homosexual sodomy — is facially different from the fundamental rights recognized in Meyer, Pierce, and Griswold. The issue is whether the Constitution confers a fundamental right to engage in consensual homosexual sodomy, and the answer is no.

opinions.author: Blackmun
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Blackmun argued the case was about the fundamental right to be left alone in the most intimate aspects of one's life — not about a 'right to engage in homosexual sodomy' — and that the majority's framing was deliberately degrading.

cases.notes: Bowers v. Hardwick held that the Constitution does not protect consensual homosexual sodomy. The decision was explicitly overruled by Lawrence v. Texas (2003), which held that Bowers was wrong when decided. Bowers is essential for understanding Lawrence — Lawrence's holding is the direct repudiation of Bowers's reasoning and framing. Justice Powell, who provided the fifth vote in Bowers, later said it was his biggest mistake.

cases.short_name: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization
cases.full_name: Dobbs, State Health Officer of the Mississippi Department of Health, et al. v. Jackson Women's Health Organization et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Constitution does not confer a right to abortion; Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey are overruled; and the authority to regulate abortion is returned to the people and their elected representatives. Rational-basis review is the appropriate standard for state abortion regulations.
cases.notes: Majority opinion joined by Thomas, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, and Barrett. Roberts concurred in the judgment but would have taken a narrower approach. Breyer, Sotomayor, and Kagan dissented. The decision explicitly overrules Roe v. Wade (1973) and Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Constitution protects a woman's right to obtain an abortion prior to fetal viability, subject to state regulation that does not substantially burden that right.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by dobbs_v_jackson_womens_health_organization_2022. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.full_name: Planned Parenthood of Southeastern Pennsylvania v. Casey
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The essential holding of Roe v. Wade is reaffirmed: a woman has a right to obtain an abortion prior to fetal viability without excessive government restriction, subject to an undue burden standard.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by dobbs_v_jackson_womens_health_organization_2022. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The exclusion of same-sex couples from marriage does not present a substantial federal question.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by obergefell_v_hodges_2015. One-line summary decision.
