cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute forbidding the use of contraceptives violates the constitutional right of marital privacy, which is protected within the penumbra of specific guarantees of the Bill of Rights.

opinions.author: Goldberg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined by Warren and Brennan; grounded right in Ninth Amendment

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred on due process grounds

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the judgment

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black and Stewart dissented

opinions.author: Stewart
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black and Stewart dissented

cases.notes: Majority opinion joined by Clark, Harlan, Brennan, White, Goldberg, and Warren. Multiple concurrences reflect different constitutional grounds for the right to marital privacy.

cases.full_name: Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Department of Health
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The United States Constitution does not forbid a state from requiring that evidence of an incompetent person's wishes as to the withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment be proved by clear and convincing evidence, and a state may constitutionally apply such a standard as a procedural safeguard to protect the liberty interest in refusing unwanted medical treatment.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Grounded right to refuse treatment in liberty rather than privacy; emphasized importance of advance directives

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued no constitutional right to refuse treatment exists; matter for states to decide

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued Missouri's clear and convincing standard was unconstitutional as applied; right to refuse treatment is fundamental

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued Missouri's interest in preserving life could not override individual liberty interest

cases.notes: The Court assumed for purposes of this case that a competent person has a constitutionally protected liberty interest under the Due Process Clause in refusing lifesaving hydration and nutrition, but held that an incompetent person does not necessarily possess the same right. The Court rejected the argument that close family members have a constitutional right to exercise substituted judgment for an incompetent patient absent substantial proof that their views reflect the patient's wishes.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute that prohibits the teaching of modern foreign languages in schools to children who have not passed the eighth grade violates the liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause because it is arbitrary and without reasonable relation to any legitimate state purpose, and infringes fundamental rights of parents to control their children's education and of teachers to pursue their occupation.

opinions.author: Holmes
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Holmes concurred in the judgment but expressed reservations about the breadth of substantive due process protection for economic liberty.

cases.notes: The opinion discusses the scope of liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause, including the right of parents to control their children's education and the right of individuals to pursue lawful occupations. The Court rejected the state's police power justification based on promoting civic unity and preventing foreign influence.

cases.full_name: Pierce v. Society of Sisters of the Holy Names of Jesus and Mary
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute that compels all children to attend public schools and effectively prohibits private school education unreasonably interferes with the liberty of parents and guardians to direct the upbringing and education of children under their control, and violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The Court affirmed preliminary injunctions restraining enforcement of Oregon's Compulsory Education Act of 1922. The decision protects parental liberty to direct children's education and the right of private schools to operate. The Court noted that states retain power to reasonably regulate schools, require attendance, set teacher qualifications, and mandate instruction in subjects essential to citizenship.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law limiting the hours of labor in bakeries violates the liberty of contract protected by the Fourteenth Amendment because the regulation lacks a fair, reasonable, and direct relation to public health or the health of bakery employees.
cases.notes: The Court struck down a New York labor law limiting bakery workers to 10 hours per day and 60 hours per week. The opinion establishes that while states possess police powers to regulate health, safety, morals, and welfare, those powers have limits when they interfere with liberty of contract without a substantial and direct relation to a legitimate public health purpose. Lochner Era effectively ended with West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish (1937) which adopted rational basis review for economic legislation, though Lochner was never formally overruled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute permitting the state to appeal in criminal cases and retry a defendant does not violate the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when the retrial is conducted free from substantial legal error, because the right to be free from double jeopardy is not a fundamental principle of liberty and justice implicit in the concept of ordered liberty that is binding on the states.

opinions.author: Butler
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's conclusion that state-initiated retrial does not violate fundamental due process protections.

cases.notes: Cardozo articulated the 'ordered liberty' or 'fundamental fairness' test for determining which Bill of Rights protections apply to the states via the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court distinguished between rights that are fundamental to ordered liberty (which bind the states) and those that are merely procedural or not essential to justice (which do not). This case established the selective incorporation doctrine framework, though the specific holding rejected incorporation of the Fifth Amendment's double jeopardy protection. Note: Palko's specific holding that double jeopardy is not incorporated was overruled by Benton v. Maryland (1969). The ordered liberty framework articulated in Palko survived and remains influential.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law that imposes sterilization as punishment for habitual criminals violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when it creates arbitrary classifications between crimes of intrinsically the same quality, such as distinguishing between larceny and embezzlement, with no rational basis in eugenics or criminal inheritability.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the Oklahoma Supreme Court's 5-4 decision upholding the Habitual Criminal Sterilization Act. The opinion emphasizes that strict scrutiny applies to classifications affecting fundamental rights such as procreation and marriage. The Court distinguished Buck v. Bell on the ground that the Virginia statute in that case had a 'saving feature' (enabling confined individuals to be released), whereas Oklahoma's statute created arbitrary distinctions with no eugenic justification.

cases.short_name: Moore v. City of East Cleveland
cases.full_name: Moore v. City of East Cleveland
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city ordinance that restricts occupancy of a dwelling unit to members of a single family, defined narrowly to exclude certain blood relatives living together, violates substantive due process when applied to prevent a grandmother from living with her son and grandsons who are first cousins.

opinions.author: Powell
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Established that family living arrangements are protected under substantive due process; distinguished Village of Belle Terre on the basis that Belle Terre involved unrelated persons while Moore involved blood relatives.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the judgment of reversal.

cases.notes: Plurality decision. Powell's opinion (pp. 498-506) announced the judgment; Stevens concurred in the judgment (pp. 513-521). The decision is fractured and does not command a clear majority on the full doctrinal scope, but the reversal itself was the judgment of the Court.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A parent has a fundamental right under the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause to make decisions concerning the care, custody, and control of their children, and a state statute that permits any person to petition for visitation rights at any time based solely on the child's best interest, without according deference to a fit parent's determination and without requiring a showing of harm, violates that fundamental right.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the judgment on narrower grounds, finding the statute sweeps too broadly in authorizing any person at any time to request visitation, without deciding whether harm is required or the precise scope of parental rights.

cases.notes: Plurality opinion. Justice O'Connor's opinion joined by Chief Justice Rehnquist, Justice Ginsburg, and Justice Breyer. Justice Souter concurred in the judgment on narrower grounds. The holding reflects what the plurality concluded; Souter's concurrence did not join the full reasoning but agreed the statute was unconstitutional as applied. Thomas, Kennedy, and Scalia also wrote separately concurring in the judgment. Stevens, Kennedy, Scalia, and Thomas each had distinct rationales.

cases.full_name: United States v. Carolene Products Co.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has constitutional power under the Commerce Clause to prohibit the shipment in interstate commerce of filled milk (skimmed milk compounded with vegetable oil in imitation of milk or cream), and such prohibition does not violate the Fifth Amendment when supported by a rational basis and legislative findings regarding public health.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in result and all of opinion except the Third section regarding statutory characterization and presumption of constitutionality.

opinions.author: McReynolds
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Would have affirmed the lower court judgment.

opinions.author: Butler
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in result but emphasized that facts alleged are not conclusive of guilt and that defendant may introduce evidence at trial to show the statutory declarations lack substantial foundation.

cases.notes: Justice Cardozo and Justice Reed took no part in consideration or decision. The opinion contains the famous Footnote 4, which suggests narrower scope for presumption of constitutionality when legislation appears to violate specific constitutional prohibitions (particularly First Amendment and equal protection) or restricts political processes or targets discrete and insular minorities.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may regulate the practice of optometry and the fitting of eyeglasses through licensing requirements and restrictions on who may fit lenses, as such economic regulations are subject only to rational basis review and need only be rationally related to a legitimate state interest.
cases.notes: Source text provides only procedural information and does not identify the majority author or vote count. The holding is inferred from the case's well-established doctrinal significance in rational basis review jurisprudence.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Double Jeopardy Clause of the Fifth Amendment is applicable to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment, and a defendant acquitted of a charge in a first trial cannot be retried on that charge following a successful appeal of a conviction on a different count.
cases.notes: The Court overruled Palko v. Connecticut (1937) and held that the Double Jeopardy Clause applies to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court vacated and remanded for consideration of whether the burglary conviction was affected by the double jeopardy violation.

cases.short_name: West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish
cases.full_name: West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law establishing minimum wages for women is constitutional as a reasonable regulation of liberty of contract adopted for the protection of the community against evils menacing health, safety, morals, and welfare, and the state has a special interest in protecting women against exploitative employment contracts.
cases.notes: Overruled Adkins v. Children's Hospital, 261 U.S. 525. Distinguished Morehead v. New York ex rel. Tipaldo, 298 U.S. 587. Affirmed judgment of Supreme Court of Washington (185 Wash. 581; 55 P.2d 1083).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A minimum wage law for women violates the liberty of contract protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by west_coast_hotel_co_v_parrish_1937. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Evidence obtained by federal officers in violation of the Fourth Amendment through an unreasonable search and seizure of a defendant's home and private papers without a warrant must be excluded from trial, and a defendant has the right to petition for the return of such illegally seized materials.
cases.notes: Establishes the exclusionary rule for federal prosecutions. The Court held that the Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures by federal officers and that illegally obtained evidence must be excluded. The Court distinguished this case from Adams v. New York on the ground that Adams involved incidental seizure in execution of a lawful warrant, whereas here the seizure was wholly unlawful and warrantless.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the Fourth Amendment's protection against arbitrary intrusion by police as a fundamental right implicit in the concept of ordered liberty, but the Fourteenth Amendment does not require states to exclude evidence obtained by unreasonable search and seizure as the remedy for Fourth Amendment violations.
cases.notes: The Court held that the Fourth Amendment's core protection against arbitrary police intrusion is fundamental and incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment, but declined to impose the exclusionary rule on the states. The Court emphasized that states may employ alternative remedies such as private actions for damages, internal police discipline, and public opinion.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Evidence obtained by searches and seizures in violation of the Fourth Amendment is inadmissible in state court proceedings, as the exclusionary rule is an essential ingredient of the Fourth Amendment right enforceable against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued against applying the exclusionary rule to the states

cases.notes: Overrules Wolf v. Colorado (1949) on the question of whether the exclusionary rule applies to state prosecutions. The Court held that the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule is constitutionally mandated and must be applied to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to counsel in criminal prosecutions, and states must provide counsel to defendants who cannot afford to retain one.
cases.notes: Landmark decision establishing the constitutional right to appointed counsel for indigent criminal defendants.

cases.full_name: Danny Escobedo v. State of Illinois
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Where the investigation has focused on a particular suspect in police custody, the suspect has requested and been denied an opportunity to consult with his lawyer, and the police have not effectively warned him of his absolute constitutional right to remain silent, the accused has been denied the Assistance of Counsel in violation of the Sixth Amendment as made obligatory upon the States by the Fourteenth Amendment, and no statement elicited by the police during interrogation may be used against him at trial.
cases.notes: The Court held that the right to counsel attaches at the investigatory stage when the focus shifts from a general investigation to an accusatory interrogation of a particular suspect, prior to formal indictment. The opinion emphasizes that the critical stage for legal advice is during police interrogation, not merely at trial.

cases.full_name: Ernesto Miranda v. State of Arizona
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Law enforcement officers must inform suspects of their Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination and Sixth Amendment right to counsel prior to custodial interrogation, and any statements obtained without such warnings are inadmissible in criminal proceedings.

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority's decision imposed unnecessary restrictions on law enforcement and that the Constitution did not require warnings before custodial interrogation.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Criticized the majority's approach as going beyond constitutional requirements and imposing prophylactic rules not mandated by the Fifth and Sixth Amendments.

cases.notes: This case established the requirement for Miranda warnings before custodial interrogation. The decision was 5-4, with Chief Justice Warren writing for the majority. Justices White, Harlan, and Stewart dissented.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment protects people, not places. Electronic surveillance of a person's words in a telephone booth, where he has a reasonable expectation of privacy, constitutes a search and seizure under the Fourth Amendment, and such surveillance without prior judicial authorization based on probable cause is per se unreasonable.
cases.notes: Overrules the trespass doctrine of Olmstead v. United States and Goldman v. United States. Establishes the reasonable expectation of privacy test for Fourth Amendment protection.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Surveillance without physical trespass and without seizure of material objects falls outside the scope of Fourth Amendment protection because the Fourth Amendment limits only searches and seizures of tangible property.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by katz_v_united_states_1967. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Surveillance conducted without physical penetration of a protected area does not constitute a search or seizure under the Fourth Amendment.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by katz_v_united_states_1967. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A police officer may conduct a limited search of the outer clothing of a person for weapons when the officer has reasonable suspicion, based on articulable facts, that the person may be armed and dangerous, even without probable cause for arrest.
cases.notes: Chief Justice Warren delivered the opinion of the Court. The case establishes the constitutional framework for stop-and-frisk searches under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Miranda v. Arizona announced a constitutional rule governing the admissibility of statements made during custodial interrogation in both state and federal courts, and Congress may not supersede this constitutional holding through legislation such as 18 U.S.C. § 3501.
cases.notes: The Court held that Miranda is a constitutional decision that cannot be overruled by statute. The Court declined to overrule Miranda itself, applying stare decisis principles.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The rigid two-pronged test from Aguilar and Spinelli for determining whether an informant's tip establishes probable cause is abandoned in favor of a flexible totality of the circumstances approach, under which a magistrate must make a practical, common-sense decision whether there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place.
cases.notes: The Court declined to address the exclusionary rule modification question under the 'not pressed or passed on below' doctrine. The decision explicitly overrules the rigid application of the Aguilar-Spinelli two-pronged test.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state criminal defendant does not have an absolute right to appointed counsel in all felony cases; the right to counsel depends on the circumstances of the particular case.
cases.notes: Petition for writ of certiorari granted. Court requested counsel to discuss jurisdiction and exhaustion of state remedies. This case was overruled by Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A defendant's own incriminating statements obtained by federal agents after indictment and in the absence of counsel cannot constitutionally be used as evidence against him at trial, even if obtained through surreptitious means without the defendant's knowledge.
cases.notes: The Court held that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies to post-indictment interrogation by government agents, whether direct or surreptitious. The Court distinguished between the government's right to continue investigation and its right to use the defendant's own statements obtained without counsel present.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to effective assistance of counsel. A defendant claiming ineffective assistance must demonstrate that counsel's performance was deficient and that this deficiency prejudiced the defense such that there is a reasonable probability the result would have been different, applying a standard of reasonably effective assistance with strong deference to counsel's strategic choices.
cases.notes: Established the two-prong test for ineffective assistance of counsel claims: deficient performance and prejudice. Applies to both trial and capital sentencing proceedings.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A warrantless search incident to arrest is limited in scope to the person of the arrestee and the area within his immediate control, and may not extend to other areas of the premises.
cases.notes: Established the constitutional scope of searches incident to arrest under the Fourth Amendment. The Court rejected the broader scope permitted under earlier decisions including Harris v. United States and United States v. Rabinowitz.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule does not bar the use in the prosecution's case in chief of evidence obtained by officers acting in reasonable reliance on a search warrant issued by a detached and neutral magistrate but ultimately found to be invalid.
cases.notes: Established the good-faith exception to the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule. The Court reversed the Court of Appeals' judgment and held that evidence obtained pursuant to a facially valid warrant is admissible even if the warrant is later found to be invalid, provided the officer's reliance on the warrant was objectively reasonable.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A warrantless search and seizure of an automobile may be conducted without a warrant if the seizing officer has probable cause to believe the vehicle contains contraband goods subject to forfeiture, because the mobility of vehicles and the impracticability of obtaining a warrant in such circumstances distinguish them from searches of fixed structures like dwellings.

opinions.author: McKenna
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the majority opinion

cases.notes: Chief Justice Taft delivered the opinion of the Court. Justice McKenna concurred before his retirement. The decision establishes the automobile exception to the warrant requirement under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Where the Government uses a device not in general public use to explore details of a private home that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion, the surveillance constitutes a Fourth Amendment search and is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.
cases.notes: Reversed Ninth Circuit decision affirming thermal imaging search. Established that use of sense-enhancing technology to obtain information about home interior not otherwise obtainable without physical intrusion constitutes a search under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Access to historical cell-site records revealing the location and movements of a mobile phone user constitutes a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant supported by probable cause.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the third-party doctrine should apply to cell-site records and that the majority's approach improperly expanded Fourth Amendment protections.

cases.notes: The Court held that digital location information derived from cell-site records is not subject to the third-party doctrine and requires Fourth Amendment protection. This represents a significant expansion of privacy rights in the digital age.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The temporary detention of a motorist upon probable cause to believe that he has violated the traffic laws does not violate the Fourth Amendment's prohibition against unreasonable seizures, even if a reasonable officer would not have stopped the motorist absent some additional law enforcement objective.
cases.notes: The Court rejected the argument that subjective intent or pretext can invalidate police conduct justified by probable cause in traffic stops. The Court also rejected a balancing test that would vary Fourth Amendment protections based on local law enforcement practices or the minor nature of the traffic violation.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Police generally may not, without a warrant, search digital information on a cell phone seized from an individual who has been arrested, because the privacy interests implicated by cell phone searches substantially exceed those addressed by the search incident to arrest exception, and the government interests identified in Chimel are not furthered by warrantless cell phone searches.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in part and in the judgment; did not join the full majority opinion

cases.notes: Consolidated with United States v. Wurie, No. 13-212. Chief Justice Roberts delivered the opinion of the Court, joined by Justices Scalia, Kennedy, Thomas, Ginsburg, Breyer, Sotomayor, and Kagan. Justice Alito filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. The holding applies to both the Riley and Wurie cases.

doctrinal_tests.label: Central Hudson Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The four-part intermediate scrutiny test governing government regulation of commercial speech. All four prongs must be satisfied for a regulation to survive constitutional challenge. The government bears the burden of justification.

prongs.label: Lawful and non-misleading
prongs.description: The speech must concern lawful activity and must not be misleading or fraudulent. If this threshold is not met, the speech receives no First Amendment protection and the analysis ends.
prongs.burden_note: Threshold requirement. If not met, no protection attaches and government may freely regulate.

prongs.label: Substantial government interest
prongs.description: The government must assert a substantial interest in restricting the speech. A merely reasonable or rational interest is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must identify and demonstrate a substantial — not merely legitimate — interest.

prongs.label: Directly advances
prongs.description: The regulation must directly and materially advance the asserted government interest. The government must demonstrate a reasonable fit between the regulation and the interest — a mere speculative connection is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the regulation actually advances the interest, not just that it might.

prongs.label: No more extensive than necessary
prongs.description: The regulation must be no more extensive than necessary to serve the government's interest. Some Justices have argued this requires the 'least restrictive means' available; the Court has generally applied a 'reasonable fit' standard rather than a least-restrictive-means requirement.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show reasonable fit. Circuit split exists on whether 'least restrictive means' is required. See 44 Liquormart (1996) concurrences.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Prong 4 has generated the most doctrinal tension. Justice Thomas has argued commercial speech should receive full First Amendment protection, eliminating the need for Central Hudson entirely. Sorrell (2011) left open whether content- or speaker-based restrictions trigger a higher standard.

doctrinal_tests.label: Brandenburg Incitement Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for determining when advocacy of illegal conduct loses First Amendment protection. Speech may be proscribed as incitement only if it meets both the intent prong (directed to producing imminent lawless action) and the likelihood prong (likely to produce such action). Both prongs must be satisfied. The test is conjunctive — meeting one prong is insufficient.

prongs.label: Directed to imminent lawless action
prongs.description: The speech must be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action. 'Directed to' requires intent — mere recklessness is insufficient. 'Imminent' excludes abstract advocacy of future illegality, however forceful. The speaker must be aiming at producing immediate unlawful conduct.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove both intent and imminence. Abstract advocacy, even of violent overthrow, is protected.
prongs.contest_note: The imminence requirement is contested at the margins. Courts have disagreed on how immediate 'imminent' must be — hours? days? Some circuits have found imminence satisfied where violence was threatened for the near future but not the immediate moment. Hess v. Indiana (1973) operationalized imminence to require that violence be directed at the present, not future, time.

prongs.label: Likely to produce imminent lawless action
prongs.description: The speech must be likely to produce the imminent lawless action. This is an objective probability assessment — is there a realistic likelihood that the speech will actually cause immediate unlawful conduct given the circumstances? Mere tendency to lead to violence, or likelihood of future harm, is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate realistic probability of imminent harm, not mere possibility or tendency.
prongs.contest_note: The likelihood prong intersects with context in ways courts have handled inconsistently. Online speech contexts have generated significant disagreement — when is violent speech online 'likely' to produce imminent violence, given the disconnect between online advocacy and real-world action? Post-January 6 litigation has brought this to the foreground in lower courts.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Brandenburg per curiam was brief and left significant interpretive questions open. The test's intellectual origin lies in Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927) — both of which insisted on imminence as a constitutional requirement — but those were minority positions for 40+ years. SCHEMA ISSUE: source_case_id points to Brandenburg, but the doctrinal content traces to non-majority opinions in earlier cases. The schema cannot currently represent that the 'source' of a test's intellectual substance differs from the case that made it majority law.

doctrinal_tests.label: True Threats Test (Post-Counterman)
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional framework for evaluating whether a statement constitutes an unprotected true threat. Counterman v. Colorado (2023) held that the First Amendment requires proof of a subjective mental state — at minimum recklessness — before a statement can be punished as a true threat. A purely objective reasonable-person standard is constitutionally insufficient.

prongs.label: Objectively threatening statement
prongs.description: The statement must communicate a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence against an identifiable individual or group. This is assessed from the perspective of a reasonable recipient in context.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the statement would be understood by a reasonable person as expressing serious violent intent.

prongs.label: Subjective mens rea — recklessness minimum
prongs.description: The speaker must have been subjectively aware of the threatening nature of the communications. At minimum, the speaker must have consciously disregarded a substantial and unjustifiable risk that their statements would be perceived as threatening. Higher mental states (knowledge, purpose) also suffice. Negligence — should have known — is constitutionally insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove at minimum that the defendant recklessly disregarded the threatening nature of the communication. Objective reasonable-person standard alone is insufficient post-Counterman.
prongs.contest_note: Counterman's majority held that recklessness is the constitutional floor but left open whether higher mental states are required. Justice Barrett (majority) and Justice Thomas (dissent) disagreed sharply on whether the First Amendment requires any subjective mental state for true threats. Circuit courts are actively working out: (1) whether purpose or knowledge is required in some contexts; (2) how to instruct juries on recklessness; (3) whether Counterman applies retroactively to convictions under objective-standard statutes. Active circuit split expected to produce cert petition within 2-3 years.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Counterman is the Court's most significant true threats decision since Virginia v. Black (2003). The recklessness standard resolves a long-standing circuit split but creates new interpretive questions. Pre-Counterman, the majority of circuits applied purely objective standards; post-Counterman those standards are constitutionally deficient. State statutes with objective-only true threats standards are now vulnerable to facial or as-applied challenges.

doctrinal_tests.label: Prior Restraint Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for evaluating government attempts to suppress speech before publication. Established in Near v. Minnesota and refined in New York Times v. United States, the test requires the government to meet an extremely heavy burden. The presumption against prior restraints is nearly absolute; the government must show that publication will directly and immediately cause serious harm that cannot be remedied after the fact.

prongs.label: Presumption of unconstitutionality
prongs.description: Any prior restraint on expression comes with a heavy presumption against its constitutional validity. The government must rebut this presumption affirmatively; the speaker need not demonstrate harm from the restraint.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of overcoming the presumption. Burden is higher than for subsequent punishment of equivalent speech.

prongs.label: Direct, immediate, and irreparable harm
prongs.description: The government must show that publication will cause direct, immediate, and irreparable harm. Speculative harm, remote harm, or harm that could be addressed through subsequent punishment is insufficient. The classic example from Near is publication of troop movements in wartime that would directly endanger soldiers.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate concrete, near-certain harm — not speculation or general concerns about national security, public order, or reputational damage.
prongs.contest_note: The standard for 'direct and immediate' harm is contested in national security contexts. Pentagon Papers (1971) held that the Nixon administration's showing was insufficient, but the six concurrences disagreed sharply on what would constitute a sufficient showing. The district court in Progressive (1979) found the H-bomb article sufficient — the only federal court to ever uphold a prior restraint on national security grounds — but the case was mooted before appellate review. The contested zone is: how concrete and certain must the harm be, and who decides?

prongs.label: No adequate alternative remedy
prongs.description: The harm must be one that cannot be adequately addressed through subsequent punishment or other less restrictive means. If criminal prosecution or civil liability after publication would adequately deter and remedy the harm, a prior restraint is not justified.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show that subsequent punishment is inadequate — that the speech, once published, causes irreversible harm that cannot be meaningfully remedied.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test as articulated is a distillation from Near and Pentagon Papers. No single majority opinion has cleanly enumerated all three prongs — they emerge from the weight of the doctrine across multiple cases. SCHEMA ISSUE C: Pentagon Papers produced nine separate opinions, none commanding a majority. The doctrinal content of what the government must show is assembled from concurrences and dissents, not from a majority holding. The schema's source_case_id points to Near as the doctrinal source, but the test's operational prongs are substantially shaped by Pentagon Papers — a case with no majority opinion.

doctrinal_tests.label: Madsen Content-Neutral Injunction Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for evaluating court injunctions that restrict speech or expressive activity in a content-neutral manner. Established in Madsen v. Women's Health Center (1994), this test is more demanding than ordinary time/place/manner review but less stringent than the near-absolute prior restraint standard. It applies specifically to injunctions issued against specific parties, not generally applicable laws.

prongs.label: Content neutrality
prongs.description: The injunction must be content-neutral — directed at the conduct of specific parties before the court, not at the message or viewpoint being expressed. An injunction targeting anti-abortion protesters specifically because of their message would be content-based and subject to strict scrutiny, not the Madsen standard.
prongs.burden_note: Court must find that the injunction's restrictions stem from the conduct and its effects, not from the content or viewpoint of the speech.
prongs.contest_note: Scalia's dissent in Madsen argued that injunctions targeting a specific group engaged in a specific protest are inherently content-based — you cannot separate the conduct from the message in protest contexts. This tension has not been definitively resolved and resurfaces in cases involving targeted protest injunctions.

prongs.label: Burdens no more speech than necessary
prongs.description: The injunction must burden no more speech than necessary to serve the significant governmental interests at stake. This is a proportionality inquiry: the restriction must be tailored to the harm, not broader than needed. Courts must examine each provision of the injunction separately against this standard.
prongs.burden_note: Government (or plaintiff seeking injunction) must show each restriction is necessary. Overbroad provisions are severable and may be invalidated without voiding the entire injunction.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Madsen is important not just for the test but for the institutional holding that courts may issue injunctions restricting speech when needed to protect the rights of others — but that those injunctions are subject to constitutional scrutiny. The Madsen test has been applied and refined in Schenck v. Pro-Choice Network (1997) and Hill v. Colorado (2000), both of which are outside this slice's scope.

doctrinal_tests.label: Actual Malice Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for determining whether a public official or public figure plaintiff may recover damages in a defamation action against a press or media defendant. Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant published the statement with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard for whether it was true or false. The standard is subjective and defendant-focused: it asks what the defendant actually believed, not what a reasonable publisher would have believed.

prongs.label: Public official or public figure plaintiff
prongs.description: The plaintiff must be a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure. Private figures are governed by the Gertz standard, not actual malice. The categorization of who qualifies as a public figure is itself a contested doctrinal question with its own factors.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant may raise plaintiff's status as a threshold issue. Court determines plaintiff's status as a matter of law.
prongs.contest_note: The boundary between limited-purpose public figures and private figures is heavily litigated and inconsistently applied across circuits. A person becomes a limited-purpose public figure by voluntarily injecting themselves into a particular public controversy — but what counts as voluntary, and which controversies qualify, varies. SCHEMA ISSUE A: The public figure taxonomy (public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, private figure) is a classification framework that functions as a precondition to the test. It does not fit cleanly as a prong because it is itself a multi-factor inquiry with its own sub-criteria. The schema has no way to represent a structured pre-test classification as part of a DoctrinalTest.

prongs.label: Knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard
prongs.description: Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that: (a) defendant knew the statement was false at the time of publication, OR (b) defendant acted with reckless disregard for whether the statement was true or false. Reckless disregard requires proof that the defendant subjectively entertained serious doubts about the truth of the statement. Negligence — even gross negligence — is constitutionally insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Clear and convincing evidence standard — higher than preponderance but lower than beyond reasonable doubt. Plaintiff must prove defendant's subjective state of mind, which is typically established through circumstantial evidence of the defendant's newsgathering process.
prongs.contest_note: The subjective recklessness standard — defendant must have entertained serious doubts — was established in St. Amant v. Thompson (1968). The standard for what constitutes 'reckless disregard' in the context of hot-button political reporting, social media republication, and AI-generated content is an active area of development. Some courts have struggled with applying a subjective standard to institutional defendants (corporations, news organizations) where no individual decision-maker had the requisite state of mind.

prongs.label: Statement of fact, not opinion
prongs.description: The statement must be capable of being proven false — i.e., it must be a statement of fact, not pure opinion or rhetorical hyperbole. A statement that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts cannot satisfy the falsity element and is protected regardless of the speaker's intent.
prongs.burden_note: Whether a statement is fact or opinion is a question of law for the court. Plaintiff bears the burden of establishing the statement is capable of being proven false.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The actual malice standard has been extended beyond classic press defamation to: IIED claims by public figures (Hustler v. Falwell, 1988), false light invasion of privacy, and some tortious interference claims. The extension pattern is modeled through MODIFIES edges from the relevant cases.

doctrinal_tests.label: Public Figure Classification Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The framework for determining whether a defamation plaintiff is a public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, or private figure — which in turn determines which constitutional standard governs the claim. Established across NYT v. Sullivan (public officials), Curtis Publishing v. Butts (public figures), and Gertz v. Robert Welch (private figures and the public figure taxonomy).

prongs.label: Public official
prongs.description: Government employees who have or appear to have substantial responsibility for or control over the conduct of governmental affairs. The category is not limited to elected officials — it includes appointed officials in positions with significant public trust. The line between public officials and low-level government employees is fact-specific.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must demonstrate plaintiff held a position of significant governmental responsibility. Courts look to the nature of the position, not just its title.

prongs.label: All-purpose public figure
prongs.description: Individuals who have achieved pervasive fame or notoriety in the community — celebrities, major public personalities. These individuals are public figures for all purposes and contexts. They must prove actual malice for any defamatory statement, regardless of whether it concerns their public role.
prongs.burden_note: Courts apply this category narrowly. True all-purpose public figures are rare — most cases involve limited-purpose public figures.

prongs.label: Limited-purpose public figure
prongs.description: Individuals who voluntarily inject themselves into a particular public controversy and thereby become public figures for the limited range of issues related to that controversy. They must prove actual malice only for statements relating to the controversy that made them public figures.
prongs.burden_note: Most heavily litigated category. Courts assess: (1) whether a public controversy existed, (2) whether plaintiff voluntarily thrust themselves into it, (3) whether the alleged defamation relates to plaintiff's role in that controversy.
prongs.contest_note: The voluntary injection requirement and the scope of the relevant controversy are inconsistently applied. Some circuits require active voluntary participation; others find that being named in a public controversy, even involuntarily, can make someone a limited-purpose public figure. What constitutes a 'public controversy' distinct from merely a matter of public interest is also unsettled.

prongs.label: Private figure
prongs.description: Anyone who does not qualify as a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure. Private figure plaintiffs need only prove fault (negligence or higher, set by state law) and are limited to actual damages unless they prove actual malice.
prongs.burden_note: For matters of public concern: plaintiff proves fault at whatever standard state law imposes (minimum: negligence). For matters not of public concern: state law has broader latitude.

doctrinal_tests.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A (second instance): This test functions as a threshold classification that determines which other test applies. It is a meta-test — a test about which test governs — rather than a test applied to the merits. The schema has no way to represent a DoctrinalTest that is a precondition to another DoctrinalTest rather than an independent test applied to the underlying facts. The GOVERNED_BY edge from freedom_of_press to this test is accurate, but the conditional relationship between this test and actual_malice_test is not expressible in the current edge vocabulary.

doctrinal_tests.label: Sherbert Compelling Interest Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The pre-Smith constitutional standard for Free Exercise challenges. The government must satisfy strict scrutiny to enforce a law that substantially burdens religious practice: a compelling governmental interest pursued through the least restrictive means available. Established in Sherbert v. Verner (1963), extended in Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972). Superseded by Employment Division v. Smith (1990) for neutral, generally applicable laws, but remains operative for non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws and has been restored by statute (RFRA) for federal laws.

prongs.label: Substantial burden on religious practice
prongs.description: The law or government action must substantially burden the plaintiff's sincerely held religious belief or practice. A de minimis burden does not trigger the test. Courts do not evaluate the reasonableness or centrality of the belief — only its sincerity and the burden's substantiality.
prongs.burden_note: Plaintiff establishes prima facie case by showing substantial burden on sincere religious practice.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes a 'substantial burden' is contested. Some circuits require that the government directly compel action contrary to religious belief; others find substantial burden where government conditions a significant benefit on conduct that violates religious belief (the Sherbert-Hobson's-choice pattern). The RFRA substantial burden standard has also generated significant circuit disagreement.

prongs.label: Compelling governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate a compelling interest in enforcing the law against the religious objector. A general interest in uniform enforcement is insufficient — the government must show a particularized compelling interest in denying the specific exemption sought.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating compelling interest. The interest must be compelling as applied to the claimant, not just compelling in the abstract.

prongs.label: Least restrictive means
prongs.description: The government must pursue the compelling interest through the least restrictive means available. If a less burdensome alternative would achieve the same compelling interest, the government must use it.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating no less restrictive alternative is available.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Status: modified rather than overruled. Smith did not overrule Sherbert across the board — it limited Sherbert to non-neutral, non-generally-applicable laws. Sherbert remains operative in its original domain (unemployment compensation cases and analogous individualized exemption systems) and was restored by RFRA for federal laws. The Sherbert test is also the model for RFRA's statutory compelling interest test. SCHEMA ISSUE B: RFRA (42 U.S.C. § 2000bb) restored the Sherbert test as a statutory matter after Smith abandoned it constitutionally. The graph models the constitutional Sherbert test but cannot represent that a statute has restored an abandoned constitutional standard. The constitutional test and the statutory test now coexist and are applied in different contexts. Phase 2 statute layer problem.

doctrinal_tests.label: Smith Neutral General Law Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The post-1990 constitutional standard for Free Exercise challenges to neutral, generally applicable laws. Under Employment Division v. Smith, the Free Amendment does not relieve an individual of the obligation to comply with a valid and neutral law of general applicability on the ground that the law proscribes conduct that his religion prescribes. A neutral, generally applicable law that incidentally burdens religious practice does not require compelling interest justification — it need only satisfy rational basis review. The threshold question is whether the law is in fact neutral and generally applicable.

prongs.label: Law is neutral
prongs.description: The law's object must not be to target, restrict, or burden religious practice. A law is not neutral if it singles out religious conduct for special burdens, if it exempts comparable secular conduct while burdening religious conduct, or if its purpose (as revealed by text, history, or enforcement) is to disadvantage a particular religion or religion generally.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears initial burden of demonstrating the law is not neutral. If challenger establishes non-neutrality, burden shifts to government to satisfy strict scrutiny.
prongs.contest_note: The neutrality inquiry is contested at the margins. How much anti-religious legislative history is required to make a facially neutral law non-neutral? When does selective enforcement evidence non-neutrality? These questions are resolved inconsistently across circuits. Masterpiece Cakeshop v. Colorado Civil Rights Commission (2018) found non-neutrality based on commissioners' hostile statements — but the scope of that holding is disputed.

prongs.label: Law is generally applicable
prongs.description: The law must apply across the board without carving out exceptions for comparable secular conduct. A law is not generally applicable if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allows secular reasons to excuse compliance but not religious ones, or if it exempts some comparable secular conduct while burdening religious conduct. Fulton (2021) significantly tightened this prong: even a categorical exception that permits discretionary secular exemptions makes the law not generally applicable.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the law is not generally applicable by showing comparable secular conduct is exempted. The comparison class — what counts as 'comparable' — is a contested question.
prongs.contest_note: The general applicability inquiry is the most actively litigated aspect of post-Smith doctrine. What counts as a 'comparable' secular exemption? How many secular exceptions are needed before a law loses general applicability? Fulton held that a single discretionary exemption for secular reasons suffices to make a law not generally applicable — but the scope of Fulton's rule is being actively worked out in lower courts. Multiple circuits have disagreed on application.

prongs.label: Rational basis if neutral and generally applicable
prongs.description: If the law is both neutral and generally applicable, it need only satisfy rational basis review to survive Free Exercise challenge — the government need not demonstrate a compelling interest or least restrictive means. The religious objector has no constitutional right to an exemption from a valid neutral general law.
prongs.burden_note: If the law is neutral and generally applicable, the government need only show a rational basis. Burden effectively shifts to challenger to show the law lacks even a rational basis, which is nearly impossible to establish.
prongs.contest_note: The rational basis tier for neutral general laws is contested at the Supreme Court level — not in application but in validity. At least four sitting justices (Alito, Thomas, Gorsuch, and Barrett in Fulton) have questioned whether Smith correctly held that neutral general laws only require rational basis. If Smith is overruled, this prong disappears entirely and Sherbert's compelling interest standard is restored. SCHEMA ISSUE C: This is the first instance in the graph of a prong that is contested not because of application disagreements but because the test itself may be overruled. The contested flag was designed for circuit splits on prong meaning — not for intra-Court challenges to the test's existence.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Smith is the most contested Free Exercise precedent. Scalia's majority opinion was joined by only four other justices; O'Connor, joined by Brennan, Marshall, and Blackmun, concurred in the judgment but rejected the majority's reasoning and argued Sherbert should have been applied. The 5-4 split on reasoning (though not on result) foreshadowed the precedent's contested status. Justice Alito has written extensively against Smith in Fulton concurrences and other writings. The Barrett-Kavanaugh-Thomas concurrence in Fulton explicitly invited reconsideration.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lemon Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The three-part Establishment Clause test established in Lemon v. Kurtzman (1971) and explicitly overruled in Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022). For 51 years, a government action survived Establishment Clause scrutiny only if it: (1) had a secular legislative purpose, (2) had a primary effect that neither advanced nor inhibited religion, and (3) did not foster excessive entanglement with religion. The test was widely criticized by justices across the ideological spectrum for producing inconsistent results, but it survived until Kennedy because no majority could agree on a replacement.

prongs.label: Secular purpose
prongs.description: The government action must have a secular legislative purpose. A purely religious purpose is unconstitutional. Courts look to stated purposes but may look beyond them if the stated purpose is a sham.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show the government action lacks any secular purpose. Courts gave government wide latitude here — almost any plausible secular purpose satisfied this prong.
prongs.contest_note: The secular purpose prong was widely criticized as toothless — courts rarely found a government action to lack any secular purpose. Justice Scalia called the prong 'a ghoul in a horror movie that repeatedly kill[s] and refuse[s] to die' in his concurrence in Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches Union Free School District (1993) — he concurred in the judgment while criticizing the Lemon test. The prong's weakness contributed to the Lemon test's eventual overruling.

prongs.label: Primary effect
prongs.description: The government action's principal or primary effect must neither advance nor inhibit religion. The Endorsement Test (Justice O'Connor) reformulated this prong: the government may not send a message to nonadherents that they are outsiders or a message to adherents that they are insiders.
prongs.burden_note: This prong generated significant litigation. Courts examined whether the government action would be perceived by a reasonable observer as endorsing religion.
prongs.contest_note: The Endorsement Test reformulation (Lynch v. Donnelly, 1984, O'Connor concurrence; County of Allegheny v. ACLU, 1989) was adopted by some justices but not all. The test asked what a reasonable observer would understand — but courts disagreed on who the reasonable observer was and what they knew. The Endorsement Test and the Lemon primary effect prong coexisted uneasily for decades.

prongs.label: No excessive entanglement
prongs.description: The government action must not foster excessive entanglement between government and religion. Entanglement includes both institutional entanglement (ongoing government supervision of religious organizations) and political entanglement (government action that generates divisive political battles along religious lines).
prongs.burden_note: This prong was used primarily to strike down programs requiring ongoing government monitoring of religious institutions receiving government funds.
prongs.contest_note: The excessive entanglement prong was eventually absorbed into the primary effect inquiry in Agostini v. Felton (1997), reducing Lemon from a three-part to an effectively two-part test in practice. The fact that courts were silently modifying the test's structure without formally overruling it contributed to its eventual demise.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Lemon test is the first fully overruled DoctrinalTest in this graph. valid_until: 2022-06-27 (date of Kennedy v. Bremerton decision). status: overruled. The test governed Establishment Clause analysis for 51 years despite persistent criticism. Justice Scalia's Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches (1993) concurrence mocked the test as a 'ghoul in a horror movie.' Multiple justices called for its replacement in multiple cases. Kennedy v. Bremerton finally provided a majority willing to overrule it. SCHEMA ISSUE A: This is the first instance of a fully overruled DoctrinalTest with valid_until populated. The temporal model handles this correctly, but the visualization and query layer need to ensure overruled tests are displayed differently from active ones — particularly that queries for 'what test currently governs this area' exclude overruled tests.

doctrinal_tests.label: Coercion Test
doctrinal_tests.description: An Establishment Clause analytical framework focusing on whether government action coerces religious participation. Articulated in Justice Kennedy's Lee v. Weisman majority (1992): the Establishment Clause bars government from coercing anyone to support or participate in religion or its exercise. The coercion test asks whether government action exerts direct or indirect coercive pressure toward religious participation. It was developed as an alternative to Lemon in the school prayer context and remains operative post-Kennedy as a standalone analytical framework.

prongs.label: Government direction or control of religious activity
prongs.description: The government must have directed, controlled, or sponsored the religious activity at issue. Purely private religious expression that happens to occur on government property or at government events does not satisfy this prong.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show government involvement in directing or sponsoring the religious activity — not merely permitting it.
prongs.contest_note: The line between government direction and government permission is contested. Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) held that a public school football coach's post-game prayers were his own private religious expression, not government-directed — even though he was a government employee acting in his official capacity on school grounds. The dissent argued this mischaracterized the coercion inherent in the coach's supervisory relationship with players.

prongs.label: Coercion of religious participation
prongs.description: The government action must coerce participation in religious activity — either directly (legal compulsion) or indirectly (social pressure, implicit pressure from authority figures, peer pressure in school contexts). Lee v. Weisman held that even subtle indirect pressure can constitute unconstitutional coercion in the school context, where students are legally required to attend and are subject to authority.
prongs.burden_note: Courts have recognized that coercion need not be direct legal compulsion — indirect pressure, particularly in school settings where students are captive and subject to authority, can satisfy this prong.
prongs.contest_note: How much indirect pressure constitutes coercion is actively contested. The school prayer cases apply a sensitive coercion standard; cases involving adult audiences apply a more demanding standard. Kennedy v. Bremerton's majority found no coercion from a coach's personal prayers — the dissent argued this ignored the realistic coercive pressure on players. The post-Kennedy scope of the coercion standard in non-school contexts is unsettled.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The coercion test was Justice Kennedy's preferred alternative to Lemon, articulated in Lee v. Weisman. It survived Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) as a distinct analytical framework — the Kennedy majority did not overrule the coercion test, only Lemon. The coercion test is particularly important in school prayer contexts where the captive audience and authority relationships make indirect pressure more salient.

doctrinal_tests.label: Expressive Association Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional framework for evaluating whether a group has a First Amendment right to exclude members whose presence would burden the group's expressive message. Established in Roberts v. United States Jaycees (1984) and applied in Boy Scouts of America v. Dale (2000). The test asks whether the group engages in expressive activity, whether forced inclusion would significantly affect the group's ability to express its views, and whether the government's interest in preventing discrimination justifies the associational burden.

prongs.label: Group engages in expressive association
prongs.description: The group must engage in some form of expression — advocating a viewpoint, promoting a message, engaging in activities protected by the First Amendment. A group that exists purely for social purposes without an expressive dimension receives less protection than one with a clear expressive purpose.
prongs.burden_note: Group bears initial burden of establishing it engages in expressive association. Courts look to the group's stated purpose, activities, and messaging.
prongs.contest_note: What counts as 'expressive' association is contested at the margins. Dale expanded the category significantly — the Boy Scouts' opposition to homosexuality was found to be part of its expressive mission even though the Scouts had never publicly articulated that position. Critics argued this definition of 'expressive' was so broad as to give any organization with any viewpoint a license to discriminate.

prongs.label: Forced inclusion significantly burdens expressive message
prongs.description: Compelling the group to accept the member must significantly affect the group's ability to advocate its viewpoint. The government cannot force a group to include a member whose presence would impair the group's expression of its message in a significant way.
prongs.burden_note: Group must show that including the member would significantly burden its message — not merely that it prefers not to include them. The more clearly the member's presence conflicts with the group's expressive mission, the stronger the protection.
prongs.contest_note: The 'significantly burdens' standard is applied deferentially — courts generally accept the group's characterization of what burdens its message. This deference was criticized in Dale: the Court accepted the Boy Scouts' post-hoc articulation of its anti-homosexuality message without requiring that the position be actually expressed or consistently held.

prongs.label: Government interest does not justify the burden
prongs.description: The government's interest in compelling inclusion — typically anti-discrimination law — must be weighed against the associational burden. The more purely expressive the group's purpose, the less likely the government interest will override it. A large, quasi-commercial organization receives less protection than a small, ideologically cohesive advocacy group.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify the anti-discrimination interest against the associational burden. The Roberts Court drew a distinction between large commercial organizations (Jaycees — required to admit women) and intimate expressive associations (more protected).

doctrinal_tests.notes: source_case_id points to Roberts (1984) which established the test and has the ESTABLISHES edge. Dale (2000) significantly expanded and modified the test through a MODIFIES(expands) edge — Dale is the operative statement of how the test works in practice, but Roberts is the canonical source. The expressive association test was initially articulated in Roberts (1984) but its current form reflects Dale (2000), which significantly expanded the scope of expressive association protection and the deference courts give to groups' claims about their expressive purposes. source_case_id points to Dale as the operative statement of the test.

doctrinal_tests.label: Compelled Disclosure Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for evaluating government requirements that compel disclosure of associational membership or donor identity. Established in NAACP v. Alabama (1958) and significantly tightened in Americans for Prosperity Foundation v. Bonta (2021), which held that disclosure requirements are subject to exacting scrutiny: they must be narrowly tailored to a substantial government interest.

prongs.label: Compelled disclosure of protected associational information
prongs.description: The government must be compelling disclosure of membership lists, donor identities, or organizational affiliations that are protected by the First Amendment. Not all disclosure requirements trigger this test — routine business records and public organizational information do not implicate associational privacy.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the disclosure requirement reaches protected associational information, not merely public or non-sensitive organizational data.

prongs.label: Chilling effect on protected association
prongs.description: The disclosure requirement must create a reasonable probability of harm to members — harassment, retaliation, or deterrence of protected associational activity. The chilling effect need not be proven with certainty; a reasonable probability of harm suffices.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate a reasonable probability that disclosure would chill protected First Amendment activity. Courts look to evidence of past harassment or the nature of the organization's activities.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes a sufficient showing of chilling effect has varied across circuits. Americans for Prosperity Foundation v. Bonta (2021) held that the state's blanket requirement that charities disclose major donors to the Attorney General was not narrowly tailored even without proof of specific harassment — the Court presumed the chilling effect from the nature of the requirement.

prongs.label: Substantial government interest, narrowly tailored
prongs.description: Post-Bonta, the government must demonstrate that its disclosure requirement is narrowly tailored to a substantial interest. A blanket disclosure requirement that is not limited to cases where disclosure is actually necessary to serve the government's interest fails this prong. The government cannot satisfy this prong by asserting a general regulatory interest in knowing who funds organizations.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating narrow tailoring. Bonta rejected California's blanket donor disclosure requirement as insufficiently tailored even to a legitimate anti-fraud interest.
prongs.contest_note: Bonta significantly tightened the compelled disclosure test by requiring narrow tailoring rather than merely a substantial relationship between disclosure and a state interest. The precise scope of Bonta's narrow tailoring requirement is being worked out in lower courts — particularly whether it effectively establishes strict scrutiny for donor disclosure or a somewhat less demanding standard.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The compelled disclosure test connects to the anonymous speech doctrine in core_political_speech. Both protect the ability to engage in expressive activity without government-compelled identification. Buckley v. Valeo (1976) established a somewhat different (more permissive) standard for campaign finance disclosure that exists in tension with the NAACP/Bonta line — that tension is a Phase 2 issue requiring the campaign finance doctrine to be modeled.

doctrinal_tests.label: Tinker Substantial Disruption Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The governing test for student speech in public schools. School officials may restrict student expression only when they can reasonably forecast that the speech will substantially disrupt or materially interfere with school activities or the rights of other students. The test requires specific, articulable facts supporting the forecast — not speculation or undifferentiated fear. Established in Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969).

prongs.label: Student expression on school grounds
prongs.description: The expression must occur in the school context — on school grounds, at school events, or in school-sponsored activities. The scope of 'school context' for off-campus digital speech is an actively contested question not yet resolved by the Supreme Court.
prongs.burden_note: Student establishes prima facie case by demonstrating expression occurred in school context.
prongs.contest_note: The application of Tinker to off-campus social media speech is heavily contested in lower courts. Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021) addressed a cheerleader's off-campus Snapchat but the Court declined to set a clear rule, holding only that the school had overstepped in that particular case.

prongs.label: Substantial disruption or material interference
prongs.description: School officials must demonstrate a reasonable forecast — based on specific, articulable facts — that the student expression will substantially disrupt school operations or materially interfere with the rights of other students. Past incidents, credible threats, or specific evidence of planned disruption may support the forecast. Speculation, displeasure with the message, or desire to avoid controversy is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating substantial disruption or material interference. The showing must be based on specific facts, not generalized concern.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes 'substantial disruption' is contested at the margins. Courts disagree on whether a school can suppress speech in anticipation of third-party disruption (the heckler's veto problem), and on how much weight to give to evidence that the speech topic is controversial in the school community.

doctrinal_tests.label: Miller Obscenity Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The three-part test for identifying obscenity established in Miller v. California (1973). Material is obscene and unprotected if it satisfies all three prongs. The test replaced the Memoirs v. Massachusetts 'utterly without redeeming social value' standard with a more workable framework that allows community standards to define the first two prongs while applying a national standard to the third.

prongs.label: Prurient interest appeal
prongs.description: Whether the average person, applying contemporary community standards, would find that the work, taken as a whole, appeals to the prurient interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish community standards and the work's appeal to prurient interest.

prongs.label: Patently offensive depiction
prongs.description: Whether the work depicts or describes, in a patently offensive way, sexual conduct specifically defined by the applicable state law.
prongs.burden_note: State law must specifically define the sexual conduct at issue — vague statutes are unconstitutional.

prongs.label: Lacks serious value (SLAPS test)
prongs.description: Whether the work, taken as a whole, lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value. This prong is judged by a national reasonable person standard, not community standards.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish lack of serious value. Pope v. Illinois (1987) clarified that a national reasonable person standard — not community standards — applies to this prong.

doctrinal_tests.label: O'Brien Expressive Conduct Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The four-part test for evaluating government regulation of expressive conduct established in United States v. O'Brien (1968). Applies when government regulates conduct that has an expressive component for reasons unrelated to its communicative impact. If the regulation targets the communicative aspect, strict scrutiny applies instead.

prongs.label: Within constitutional power
prongs.description: The regulation must be within the constitutional power of the government.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish constitutional authority for the regulation.

prongs.label: Substantial government interest
prongs.description: The regulation must further an important or substantial government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must identify a substantial interest beyond suppression of expression.

prongs.label: Unrelated to suppression of expression
prongs.description: The government interest must be unrelated to the suppression of free expression. This is the critical prong — if the government's purpose is to suppress the message, the O'Brien test does not apply and strict scrutiny governs.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate its interest is in the non-communicative aspect of the conduct.
prongs.contest_note: Courts sometimes disagree about whether a regulation is truly unrelated to expression or whether it targets the communicative impact of the conduct. The O'Brien/strict scrutiny line depends on this determination.

prongs.label: No greater than essential
prongs.description: The incidental restriction on First Amendment freedoms must be no greater than is essential to the furtherance of the government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the restriction is tailored to its non-expressive interest.

doctrinal_tests.label: Buckley Campaign Finance Framework
doctrinal_tests.description: The framework established in Buckley v. Valeo (1976) for evaluating campaign finance restrictions. Distinguishes between expenditure limits and contribution limits, applying different levels of scrutiny to each. Expenditure limits on political speech receive strict scrutiny; contribution limits receive a more deferential standard requiring a sufficiently important interest and closely drawn means.

prongs.label: Expenditure vs. contribution classification
prongs.description: Determine whether the restriction targets expenditures (spending to disseminate speech directly) or contributions (giving money to a candidate or committee). Expenditure limits receive strict scrutiny; contribution limits receive more deferential review.
prongs.burden_note: Classification determines applicable standard of review.
prongs.contest_note: The expenditure/contribution distinction has been contested since Buckley. Critics argue it is unprincipled — both involve spending money to influence elections. Citizens United declined to reconsider the distinction but extended expenditure protection to corporations.

prongs.label: Anti-corruption interest
