prongs.description: For both expenditures and contributions, only the government interest in preventing quid pro quo corruption or its appearance is sufficient. Interests in equalizing political influence or preventing the appearance of undue influence are insufficient under Buckley.
prongs.burden_note: Government must identify evidence of actual or apparent quid pro quo corruption, not merely the appearance of influence.
prongs.contest_note: The scope of the anti-corruption interest is heavily contested. McConnell v. FEC (2003) took a broader view; Citizens United narrowed it back to quid pro quo corruption.

doctrinal_tests.label: Strict Scrutiny (Equal Protection)
doctrinal_tests.description: The most demanding standard of judicial review under the Equal Protection Clause. Applied to laws that classify on the basis of race, national origin, or alienage, or that burden the exercise of a fundamental right. A law subject to strict scrutiny is presumptively unconstitutional and will be upheld only if the government demonstrates that it is necessary to achieve a compelling governmental interest and is narrowly tailored to that end. Strict scrutiny is famously described as 'strict in theory and fatal in fact,' though in practice some laws survive.

prongs.label: Compelling governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must identify a compelling interest served by the classification. Accepted compelling interests include national security (debated), remedying specific documented past discrimination, and — until SFFA (2023) — student body diversity in higher education. Mere legitimate or important interests are insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of demonstrating compelling interest

prongs.label: Narrow tailoring
prongs.description: The classification must be narrowly tailored to achieve the compelling interest — it must be necessary, not merely helpful. Narrow tailoring requires consideration of race-neutral alternatives, avoidance of unnecessary burden on non-benefited groups, and (for affirmative action) temporal limitation. The government may not use racial quotas or mechanical point systems.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of demonstrating narrow tailoring
prongs.contest_note: The degree of fit required by narrow tailoring — whether it requires the least restrictive means or merely a close fit — has been contested across cases.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Strict scrutiny under equal protection was first articulated in Korematsu v. United States (1944), which applied the standard while upholding Japanese internment. In practice strict scrutiny is nearly always fatal — the major exceptions are Korematsu itself (subsequently repudiated in Trump v. Hawaii, 2018) and Grutter v. Bollinger (2003), which SFFA v. Harvard (2023) later overruled.

doctrinal_tests.label: Intermediate Scrutiny (Equal Protection)
doctrinal_tests.description: The middle tier of judicial review under the Equal Protection Clause. Applied to laws that classify on the basis of sex or illegitimacy. The government must demonstrate that the classification is substantially related to an important governmental interest. Intermediate scrutiny is more demanding than rational basis but less demanding than strict scrutiny. United States v. Virginia (1996) effectively heightened the standard for sex classifications by requiring an exceedingly persuasive justification.

prongs.label: Important governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate that its classification serves an important governmental interest — more than merely legitimate but less than compelling. Accepted important interests include preventing drunk driving (Craig v. Boren), compensating for past economic discrimination (Califano v. Webster), and maintaining the effectiveness of military forces (Rostker v. Goldberg).
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of demonstrating important interest

prongs.label: Substantial relation / Exceedingly persuasive justification
prongs.description: The classification must be substantially related to achieving the important interest. After United States v. Virginia (1996), sex classifications also require an exceedingly persuasive justification — a standard that effectively approaches strict scrutiny. The justification must be genuine, not hypothesized post hoc.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden; post-VMI must show exceedingly persuasive justification for sex classifications
prongs.contest_note: Whether post-VMI intermediate scrutiny for sex is functionally equivalent to strict scrutiny is contested. The Court has not formally elevated sex to a suspect classification.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Craig v. Boren (1976) formally established intermediate scrutiny as the standard for sex-based classifications. The standard was developed to avoid the 4-justice plurality in Frontiero v. Richardson (1973) that would have elevated sex to a suspect classification requiring strict scrutiny.

doctrinal_tests.label: Rational Basis Test (Equal Protection)
doctrinal_tests.description: The default, most deferential standard of judicial review under the Equal Protection Clause. Applied to all laws that do not involve suspect or quasi-suspect classifications or fundamental rights. A law satisfies rational basis if it is rationally related to any legitimate governmental interest — even one not articulated by the legislature. Courts will hypothesize conceivable rational bases and sustain the law unless no rational basis is conceivable. The challenger bears the burden of negating every possible rational basis.

prongs.label: Legitimate governmental interest
prongs.description: Any legitimate governmental interest suffices — the government need not articulate it, and courts will hypothesize one. Virtually any plausible government purpose qualifies: economic regulation, administrative convenience, promotion of health or safety. Only bare desire to harm a politically unpopular group is illegitimate.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears burden of negating all conceivable legitimate interests

prongs.label: Rational relation
prongs.description: The law must be rationally related to the legitimate interest — not perfectly tailored, just not arbitrary. The fit may be loose, overinclusive, or underinclusive. Courts defer to legislative judgments about the relationship between means and ends. In rare cases of animus-based classifications, the Court applies rational basis with more scrutiny, asking whether any legitimate interest actually explains the classification.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears burden; highly deferential to legislature
prongs.contest_note: Rational basis with bite — applied in Moreno, Cleburne, and Romer — is more demanding than standard rational basis but has not been formally acknowledged as a distinct tier. Its doctrinal status remains contested.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Rational basis emerged from the post-Lochner settlement — the Court's abandonment of substantive economic due process review after 1937. Carolene Products (1938) established rational basis as the default standard while reserving heightened review for fundamental rights and discrete and insular minorities.

doctrinal_tests.label: Clear and Convincing Evidence Standard for Withdrawal of Life-Sustaining Treatment
doctrinal_tests.description: A procedural standard requiring that evidence of an incompetent person's wishes regarding withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment be proved by clear and convincing evidence, balancing the individual's liberty interest against state interests in preserving life and protecting against surrogate abuse.

prongs.label: Individual Liberty Interest
prongs.description: The competent person's constitutionally protected liberty interest in refusing unwanted medical treatment, including life-sustaining hydration and nutrition.
prongs.burden_note: The challenger seeking to withdraw treatment must prove the incompetent person's wishes by clear and convincing evidence.

prongs.label: State Interests
prongs.description: The state's interests include: (a) general interest in protection and preservation of human life; (b) safeguarding the personal element of an individual's choice between life and death; (c) guarding against potential abuses by surrogates; (d) ensuring accurate factfinding through adversarial process; and (e) declining to make judgments about quality of life while asserting an unqualified interest in preservation of human life.
prongs.burden_note: The state bears the burden of articulating and defending its interests; the clear and convincing evidence standard distributes the risk of erroneous decision on those seeking to terminate treatment.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies specifically to decisions regarding withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment for incompetent persons. The Court emphasized that an erroneous decision to withdraw treatment cannot be corrected, whereas an erroneous decision not to withdraw maintains the status quo and may be corrected by future events. The test does not require states to accept substituted judgment by family members absent substantial proof that their views reflect the patient's wishes.

doctrinal_tests.label: Meyer Substantive Due Process Rationality Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A state law that interferes with liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause must have a reasonable relation to a legitimate purpose within the state's competency to effect. The legislature's determination of what constitutes proper exercise of police power is not final but is subject to judicial supervision. Arbitrary legislation lacking reasonable relation to a legitimate state purpose violates substantive due process.

prongs.label: Legitimate State Purpose
prongs.description: The state must identify a purpose within its competency to effect.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish a legitimate purpose.

prongs.label: Reasonable Relation
prongs.description: The legislative means must have a reasonable relation to the identified legitimate purpose. The means cannot be arbitrary or wholly unrelated to the purpose.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate reasonable relation between means and purpose.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Meyer applied this test to strike down Nebraska's foreign language teaching prohibition, finding that while the state had a legitimate interest in promoting civic unity and English language proficiency, the means (complete prohibition of modern language instruction before eighth grade) was arbitrary and lacked reasonable relation to that purpose, particularly in peacetime. The Court emphasized that desirable ends cannot be promoted by prohibited means.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lochner Police Power Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether a state regulation is a valid exercise of police power or an unconstitutional interference with liberty of contract. The regulation must have a fair, reasonable, and appropriate relation to a legitimate public purpose (safety, health, morals, or welfare), and the connection between the means and the end must be direct and substantial, not remote or shadowy.

prongs.label: Legitimate Public Purpose
prongs.description: The regulation must relate to a legitimate state interest in safety, health, morals, or general welfare of the public.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the regulation addresses a legitimate public purpose.

prongs.label: Fair, Reasonable, and Appropriate Means
prongs.description: The regulation must be a fair, reasonable, and appropriate exercise of police power in relation to the stated purpose. The connection between the regulation and the public purpose must be direct and substantial, not remote, shadowy, or arbitrary.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the regulation is reasonably related to the legitimate purpose and not arbitrary or meddlesome.

prongs.label: No Arbitrary Interference with Liberty of Contract
prongs.description: The regulation must not constitute an arbitrary or unreasonable interference with the liberty of contract protected by the Fourteenth Amendment. The regulation cannot be justified merely by assertion that it relates to public health; there must be a material and substantial danger to public health or employee health.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the regulation is not arbitrary and has a material connection to the stated public purpose.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test requires courts to examine whether a regulation is truly a health law or merely a pretext for regulating labor. The Court must look beyond the proclaimed purpose to the natural and legal effect of the statute. The test applies heightened scrutiny to regulations that interfere with liberty of contract, requiring more than a remote or speculative connection to public health.

doctrinal_tests.label: Palko Ordered Liberty Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether a right enumerated in the Bill of Rights is so fundamental to ordered liberty and due process that it applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. The test asks whether the right is rooted in the traditions and conscience of the people and whether it is essential to a scheme of ordered liberty such that its denial would violate fundamental principles of liberty and justice.

prongs.label: Rooted in Traditions and Conscience
prongs.description: The right must be rooted in the traditions and conscience of the American people and recognized as fundamental throughout the nation's history.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate that the right is deeply embedded in American legal and political traditions.

prongs.label: Essential to Ordered Liberty
prongs.description: The right must be of the very essence of a scheme of ordered liberty, such that neither liberty nor justice would exist if it were sacrificed. It must be a principle of justice so fundamental that a fair and enlightened system of justice would be impossible without it.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show that the right is indispensable to the concept of ordered liberty and due process.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test represents the Court's selective incorporation framework. Rights that fail the test (such as double jeopardy protection, grand jury indictment, jury trial, and immunity from self-incrimination) are not incorporated. Rights that pass (such as freedom of speech, press, religion, assembly, and right to counsel in capital cases) are incorporated and bind the states. The test emphasizes that the mere presence of a right in the Bill of Rights does not automatically make it applicable to the states.

doctrinal_tests.label: Parental Fundamental Right Protection Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for evaluating whether state interference with parental decisions concerning child care, custody, and control violates the Due Process Clause. The test examines whether the state has accorded appropriate deference to a fit parent's determination, whether there is a presumption favoring parental judgment, and whether the state action is narrowly tailored to address legitimate state interests.

prongs.label: Fit Parent Status
prongs.description: Whether the parent is fit. A presumption exists that fit parents act in their children's best interests.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish unfitness or harm/potential harm to justify interference.

prongs.label: Deference to Parental Judgment
prongs.description: Whether the state accords appropriate deference and weight to the fit parent's determination of the child's best interests.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show it gave proper deference to parental judgment; failure to do so violates the fundamental right.

prongs.label: Narrowness of State Action
prongs.description: Whether the state action is narrowly tailored and does not sweep too broadly in permitting interference with parental decisions.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the statute or application is not overbroad and is limited to legitimate protective purposes.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies heightened scrutiny to state interference with parental decisions. The Court emphasized that absent a showing of harm or potential harm to the child, or absent a finding that the parent is unfit, the state should not inject itself into the private realm of the family. The test does not require a formal harm showing as a threshold matter in all cases, but the plurality declined to decide that precise question. The plurality explicitly declined to identify the precise standard of review, stating only that the statute failed as applied. The test reflects balancing rather than a formal scrutiny tier.

doctrinal_tests.label: Escobedo Pretrial Right to Counsel Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A multi-factor test determining whether the right to counsel attaches at the pretrial interrogation stage, requiring consideration of whether the investigation has focused on a particular suspect, the suspect is in custody, interrogation is being conducted, the suspect has requested counsel, and the suspect has not been warned of the right to remain silent.

prongs.label: Investigation Focused on Particular Suspect
prongs.description: The investigation must have ceased to be a general inquiry into an unsolved crime and must have begun to focus on a particular suspect.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the investigation remains general; if focused on suspect, right to counsel attaches.

prongs.label: Suspect in Police Custody
prongs.description: The suspect must have been taken into police custody or otherwise restrained of liberty.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show suspect was not in custody to avoid counsel requirement.

prongs.label: Interrogation Process Conducted
prongs.description: The police must be carrying out a process of interrogations that lends itself to eliciting incriminating statements.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show no interrogation occurred to avoid counsel requirement.

prongs.label: Suspect Requested and Was Denied Counsel
prongs.description: The suspect must have requested an opportunity to consult with his lawyer and been denied that opportunity.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show suspect did not request counsel or was granted access to counsel.

prongs.label: No Effective Warning of Right to Remain Silent
prongs.description: The police must not have effectively warned the suspect of his absolute constitutional right to remain silent.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show suspect was warned of right to remain silent to avoid exclusion of statement.

doctrinal_tests.notes: When all five prongs are satisfied, the accused has been denied the Assistance of Counsel in violation of the Sixth Amendment, and any statement elicited during interrogation is inadmissible. The test applies a functional rather than formal approach, rejecting reliance on whether formal indictment has occurred.

doctrinal_tests.label: Miranda Warnings Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test requiring law enforcement to provide specific warnings to suspects in custody before interrogation, and to obtain a valid waiver of rights before questioning.

prongs.label: Custody Requirement
prongs.description: The suspect must be in custody or otherwise deprived of freedom of action in any significant way.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that the suspect was in custody.

prongs.label: Interrogation Requirement
prongs.description: There must be interrogation or its functional equivalent (express questioning or words or actions likely to elicit an incriminating response).
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that interrogation occurred.

prongs.label: Warning of Right Against Self-Incrimination
prongs.description: The suspect must be warned that they have the right to remain silent and that anything they say can be used against them in court.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide this warning before custodial interrogation.

prongs.label: Warning of Right to Counsel
prongs.description: The suspect must be warned that they have the right to an attorney and that one will be provided if they cannot afford one.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide this warning before custodial interrogation.

prongs.label: Valid Waiver
prongs.description: The suspect must knowingly, intelligently, and voluntarily waive their rights before any interrogation proceeds.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish a valid waiver of rights.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test establishes the procedural requirements for lawful custodial interrogation. Failure to comply results in suppression of any statements obtained.

doctrinal_tests.label: Terry Stop and Frisk Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for evaluating the constitutionality of a stop and frisk: (1) the officer must have reasonable suspicion based on articulable facts that criminal activity is afoot; and (2) the frisk must be limited to a pat-down of outer clothing for weapons, conducted for officer safety.

prongs.label: Reasonable Suspicion
prongs.description: The officer must have reasonable suspicion based on articulable facts that criminal activity is afoot or that the person may be armed and dangerous.
prongs.burden_note: Government must articulate specific facts and rational inferences supporting the suspicion.

prongs.label: Limited Frisk for Weapons
prongs.description: The frisk must be limited to a pat-down of the outer clothing to determine whether the person is armed, and may not extend to a full search for evidence of crime unless a weapon is felt.
prongs.burden_note: Officer must limit the search to outer garments and to the purpose of detecting weapons.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies when an officer conducts an investigatory stop without probable cause for arrest. The frisk is justified by officer safety concerns and must be strictly limited in scope.

doctrinal_tests.label: Totality of the Circumstances Test for Probable Cause
doctrinal_tests.description: A flexible standard for determining whether an informant's tip, combined with corroborating facts, establishes probable cause for a search warrant. The issuing magistrate must make a practical, common-sense decision whether there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place, considering all circumstances set forth in the affidavit.

prongs.label: Fair Probability Standard
prongs.description: The magistrate must determine whether there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in the particular place to be searched.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish fair probability based on totality of circumstances.

prongs.label: Substantial Basis for Magistrate's Conclusion
prongs.description: A reviewing court must ensure that the magistrate had a substantial basis for concluding that probable cause existed.
prongs.burden_note: Reviewing court must verify the magistrate's reasoning was grounded in the facts presented.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test replaces the rigid two-pronged Aguilar-Spinelli test. Corroboration of informant details by independent police work is of significant value under this standard. The elements of veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge are understood as closely intertwined issues that inform the common-sense probable cause determination.

doctrinal_tests.label: Strickland Ineffective Assistance of Counsel Test
doctrinal_tests.description: Two-part test for evaluating claims of ineffective assistance of counsel under the Sixth Amendment. Defendant must show deficient performance and prejudice resulting from that deficiency.

prongs.label: Deficient Performance
prongs.description: Counsel's performance was deficient. The proper standard is reasonably effective assistance, considering all circumstances. Counsel's representation must be evaluated from counsel's perspective at the time, with strong presumption that conduct falls within the wide range of reasonable professional assistance. Judicial scrutiny must be highly deferential and eliminate hindsight bias.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show counsel's performance fell below an objective standard of reasonableness.

prongs.label: Prejudice
prongs.description: The deficient performance prejudiced the defense. Defendant must show there is a reasonable probability that, but for counsel's unprofessional errors, the result of the proceeding would have been different. A reasonable probability is one sufficient to undermine confidence in the outcome. Court must consider the totality of evidence before the judge or jury.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must demonstrate reasonable probability of different outcome absent counsel's errors.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Court need not determine deficient performance before examining prejudice; if easier to dispose on lack of prejudice, that course should be followed. Applies in federal collateral proceedings, direct appeal, motions for new trial, and federal habeas challenges to state convictions.

doctrinal_tests.label: Good-Faith Reliance on Warrant Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test determining whether evidence obtained pursuant to a search warrant is admissible despite the warrant's invalidity, based on whether the officer's reliance on the warrant was objectively reasonable.

prongs.label: Detached and Neutral Magistrate
prongs.description: The warrant must have been issued by a detached and neutral magistrate, not one who merely serves as a rubber stamp for police.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the magistrate performed a neutral and detached function.

prongs.label: Objectively Reasonable Reliance
prongs.description: The officer's reliance on the magistrate's probable-cause determination and on the technical sufficiency of the warrant must be objectively reasonable.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the officer could not reasonably question the warrant's validity.

prongs.label: No Knowing or Reckless Falsity
prongs.description: The affidavit supporting the warrant must not contain information that the affiant knew was false or would have known was false except for reckless disregard of the truth.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show knowing or reckless falsity in the affidavit.

prongs.label: Substantial Basis for Probable Cause
prongs.description: The affidavit must provide the magistrate with a substantial basis for determining the existence of probable cause; the warrant cannot be based on an affidavit so lacking in indicia of probable cause as to render official belief in its existence entirely unreasonable.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the affidavit provided a substantial basis for probable cause.

prongs.label: Facial Validity of Warrant
prongs.description: The warrant must not be so facially deficient—such as in failing to particularize the place to be searched or the things to be seized—that executing officers cannot reasonably presume it to be valid.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the warrant was not facially deficient.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies when evidence is obtained pursuant to a search warrant that is ultimately found to be invalid. Suppression remains an appropriate remedy if the magistrate was misled by knowing or reckless falsity, wholly abandoned the detached and neutral role, or if the warrant is so facially deficient that reasonable reliance is impossible.

doctrinal_tests.label: Carroll Automobile Search Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A warrantless search of an automobile is valid if: (1) the seizing officer has probable cause to believe the vehicle contains contraband or goods subject to forfeiture; and (2) the search is conducted in circumstances where securing a warrant is impracticable due to the vehicle's mobility. The test balances the government's need to seize contraband in transit against the individual's Fourth Amendment protections.

prongs.label: Probable Cause
prongs.description: The seizing officer must have probable cause—facts and circumstances sufficient to warrant a man of prudence and caution in believing—that the automobile contains contraband or goods subject to forfeiture and destruction.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish probable cause based on facts known to the officer or reasonably trustworthy information received.

prongs.label: Impracticability of Warrant
prongs.description: The search must occur in circumstances where it is not practicable to secure a warrant because the vehicle can be quickly moved out of the locality or jurisdiction in which the warrant must be sought, distinguishing automobiles from fixed structures like dwellings.
prongs.burden_note: The government need not show it attempted to obtain a warrant; the inherent mobility of vehicles satisfies this prong.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established in Carroll v. United States. The test reflects the Court's holding that the Fourth Amendment recognizes a necessary distinction between searches of dwellings (requiring warrant) and searches of vehicles in transportation (permissible without warrant upon probable cause). The Court emphasized that the seizure of contraband in transit is the primary purpose, with arrest of the transporter being incidental.

doctrinal_tests.label: Sense-Enhancing Technology Search Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A search occurs under the Fourth Amendment when: (1) the Government uses a device not in general public use; (2) to explore details of a private home; (3) that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion into a constitutionally protected area. Such a search is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.

prongs.label: Device Not in General Public Use
prongs.description: The technology or device used must not be in general public use.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that the device is in general public use to avoid triggering search analysis.

prongs.label: Exploration of Home Details
prongs.description: The device must be used to explore details of a private home.
prongs.burden_note: Applies specifically to the home, which receives heightened Fourth Amendment protection.

prongs.label: Information Previously Unknowable Without Physical Intrusion
prongs.description: The information obtained could not previously have been obtained without physical intrusion into a constitutionally protected area.
prongs.burden_note: Government cannot justify warrantless use by arguing the information came from the home's external surface or did not reveal 'intimate details.'

doctrinal_tests.notes: When all three prongs are satisfied, the surveillance is a Fourth Amendment search and is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant. The test rejects mechanical interpretations based on whether information originates from external surfaces or whether it reveals 'intimate details.' In the sanctity of the home, all details are treated as intimate.

doctrinal_tests.label: Cell Phone Search Warrant Requirement Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test determining whether a warrantless search of digital information on a cell phone seized incident to arrest is permissible. The test requires that police generally obtain a warrant before searching digital information on a cell phone, with limited exceptions for exigent circumstances and physical examination to ensure the phone will not be used as a weapon.

prongs.label: Warrant Requirement
prongs.description: Police must generally obtain a warrant before searching digital information on a cell phone seized from an arrestee.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify warrantless search through a recognized exception.

prongs.label: Chimel Interests Not Furthered
prongs.description: Digital data on a cell phone does not present the officer safety or evidence destruction risks identified in Chimel, because data cannot be used as a weapon and concerns about remote wiping are better addressed through case-specific exigent circumstances analysis.
prongs.burden_note: Government cannot rely on Chimel rationales to justify warrantless cell phone searches.

prongs.label: Substantial Privacy Interests
prongs.description: Cell phones implicate substantially greater individual privacy interests than physical searches because they store vast amounts of personal information in one place, including data dating back years, and may contain information stored on remote servers.
prongs.burden_note: Government must overcome heightened privacy interests to justify warrantless search.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies the balancing approach articulated in Wyoming v. Houghton, weighing privacy intrusion against government interests. The test does not preclude warrantless searches in exigent circumstances or physical examination of the phone itself to ensure officer safety. The Court rejected proposed fallback rules that would have allowed searches based on relevance to the crime of arrest, officer safety, or analogy to pre-digital counterparts.

edges.notes: The 14th Amendment Due Process Clause incorporates the 1st Amendment speech protections against state governments. Valid_from reflects Gitlow (1925), first case to hold this.
The 1st Amendment applies to state governments via the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause.

edges.citation: Virginia State Board of Pharmacy v. Virginia Citizens Consumer Council, 425 U.S. 748 (1976)
edges.notes: Commercial speech became a recognized doctrinal sub-area only after Virginia Pharmacy Board extended First Amendment protection to it.

SCHEMA NOTE: Valentine established a rule by denying protection — not by articulating a positive doctrine. This edge exposes a gap: ESTABLISHES implies positive creation. Consider adding holding_type: [affirmative | by_negation] to Case nodes, or a separate ESTABLISHES_BY_NEGATION edge type.

The Court did not explicitly say it was overruling Valentine, but the holding is directly incompatible. SCHEMA NOTE: Need a way to represent implicit vs. explicit overruling. Consider adding overrule_type: [explicit | implicit] to OVERRULES edges. The Court stated that the notion of unprotected commercial speech had 'passed from the scene,' using language stronger than typical implicit overrulings.

SCHEMA NOTE: Posadas both applies AND modifies Central Hudson — the 'greater includes lesser' reasoning expanded the test's deference to government. A single node can have both APPLIES and MODIFIES edges to the same target. Is that right, or should MODIFIES subsume APPLIES? Consider making MODIFIES a sub-type that implies application.

SCHEMA NOTE: DISTINGUISHES is technically correct but undersells what happened — 44 Liquormart effectively repudiated Posadas's 'greater includes lesser' reasoning without formally overruling it. Consider adding a LIMITS edge type, or a direction attribute on MODIFIES: [narrows | expands | complicates | repudiates]. This is the Sorrell problem anticipated in our design discussion.
Tightened application of prongs 3 and 4. Rejected broad deference to legislative judgment. Direction: narrows (government-friendly application).

Direction: complicates. Sorrell raised the possibility that content- or speaker-based commercial speech restrictions might trigger scrutiny higher than Central Hudson intermediate scrutiny — potentially strict scrutiny. The Court declined to resolve this, leaving the framework unsettled. SCHEMA NOTE: This is the clearest case for a direction attribute on MODIFIES. 'Complicates' is not the same as 'narrows' or 'expands'.
Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice.

SCHEMA ISSUE A: Brandenburg established this test as majority law, but the test's intellectual substance was articulated in dissents and concurrences in earlier cases — Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919), Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). source_case_id on DoctrinalTest, and ESTABLISHES edges generally, have no mechanism to represent this distinction between 'case that made this majority law' and 'non-majority opinions that originated the doctrine's content.'
Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice.

Direction: clarifies. Hess operationalized the imminence prong — 'later' is not imminent. This is a clarification, not a narrowing or expansion: it resolves ambiguity in prong 1 without changing the scope of the test.

Direction: expands. Claiborne extended the Brandenburg/incitement framework to civil liability contexts. The doctrine previously operated in criminal prosecution cases; Claiborne established it applies with equal force against civil tort claims, expanding the doctrine's scope of application.
MODIFIES is not present for the test node itself — Claiborne applied the Brandenburg test without changing its prongs. Applied the test in a civil context for the first time.

Direction: expands. Snyder extended the public concern doctrine from the public employee speech context (where Connick v. Myers originated it) to private plaintiff intentional infliction of emotional distress claims. The doctrine node's valid_from is 1983 (Connick), but Connick is outside this slice's scope. SCHEMA ISSUE B: This MODIFIES edge has Snyder as source, but the target doctrine originated in Connick (1983), which has no case node in this slice. The ESTABLISHES edge for this doctrine is missing — it would need to come from a Connick node that doesn't exist here. Cross-slice node dependency not yet formally supported.

Direction: clarifies. Counterman resolved the circuit split on the mens rea standard for true threats, settling that recklessness is the constitutional floor. The true threats category itself was established earlier (Virginia v. Black, 2003; R.A.V., 1992) — Counterman clarified the mental state element rather than creating the category. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The true_threats_doctrine node has valid_from: 2023 because that's when the current formulation became operative. But the category predates Counterman by decades. The ESTABLISHES edge for the category (from Virginia v. Black or R.A.V.) is missing — those cases are out of scope. Same cross-slice dependency problem as Issue B.
The post-Counterman true threats test is new — prior circuit standards varied and are now constitutionally deficient. Counterman is genuinely the source_case_id for this test formulation.
Added in v0.2 fix — parent_id existed on node but CHILD_OF edge was missing from edge list.
Added in v0.2 fix — parent_id existed on node but CHILD_OF edge was missing from edge list.

Repudiates: Virginia Pharmacy effectively repudiated the commercial_speech_no_protection doctrine by establishing that commercial speech receives First Amendment protection — directly negating the prior no-protection rule. Renamed from OVERRULES (invalid Case->Doctrine) to MODIFIES(repudiates).

Historical governance only. The no-protection rule governed commercial speech from Valentine v. Chrestensen (1942) until Virginia State Bd. of Pharmacy (1976) established First Amendment coverage. valid_until marks the overruling date.

Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice. Prior restraint doctrine is grounded in the Press Clause as well as the Speech Clause of the First Amendment. Near emphasized the press freedom dimension; Pentagon Papers applied both.

SCHEMA ISSUE E: This GOVERNED_BY edge is conditionally applicable — the Madsen test governs only when the government instrument is a content-neutral injunction against specific parties. The prior_restraint_test governs classic prior restraints (licensing schemes, administrative censorship). The schema cannot express this conditionality on edge type. A future edge attribute — 'condition' or 'instrument_type' — would be needed.

Prior restraint doctrine and core political speech doctrine are closely related: prior restraint is the procedural mechanism by which core political speech is most severely threatened. Near v. Minnesota involved a political newspaper. Pentagon Papers involved political speech (government conduct of the Vietnam War). The RELATED_TO edge here is the first use of this edge type in the graph — per schema Issue #12, the editorial criteria for RELATED_TO need to be defined. This edge is justified by: (1) shared constitutional text (both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1), (2) overlapping case law (political speech cases often invoke prior restraint analysis), and (3) the doctrines operate as complementary protections — prior restraint is the 'how it's suppressed' dimension of the same protection that core political speech doctrine addresses from the 'what is protected' dimension.

Pentagon Papers clarified — through six concurrences — what the government must show to satisfy the prior restraint test's direct-harm prong. The per curiam itself said only that the government had not met its burden; the operational content of what would suffice comes from the concurrences. Direction: clarifies (operationalizes the burden without changing the test's structure).

Progressive applied the prior restraint test and found the government's showing sufficient — the only federal court ever to do so in a national security prior restraint case. valid_until reflects mootness date. The holding is district court only, never reviewed on appeal, and has no precedential force. SCHEMA ISSUE B: The APPLIES edge is structurally identical to APPLIES edges from SCOTUS cases, but this case's holding is non-precedential, unreviewd, and mooted. The schema has no way to weight or qualify edge authority by court level or procedural posture.

Clarifies: Alexander clarified the boundary between prior restraint and subsequent punishment, holding that RICO forfeiture of assets from past obscene speech is subsequent punishment not prior restraint. This clarifies the doctrine's scope without narrowing or expanding it. Changed from DISTINGUISHES (invalid Case->Doctrine) to MODIFIES(clarifies).

Madsen clarified that content-neutral injunctions against specific parties are not classic prior restraints and are governed by a distinct intermediate standard. This clarifies the doctrine's scope without narrowing or expanding it: it marks a boundary between what counts as a classic prior restraint and what is a different (though related) form of speech restriction.

The Press Clause of the First Amendment ('or of the press') is the textual grounding. Courts have generally not treated the Press Clause as conferring rights categorically different from those of ordinary speakers, but it provides the constitutional foundation for the distinctive press freedom doctrines modeled here.

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1, specifically the Press Clause. (2) Overlapping case law — Near v. Minnesota (1931) and NYT v. United States (1971) appear in both doctrinal lineages; Near is the foundational prior restraint case and the foundational press freedom case. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — prior restraint addresses the mechanism of pre-publication suppression; freedom of the press addresses post-publication liability and press-specific constitutional protections. Different analytical mechanisms protecting the same press freedom interest.

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Sullivan's public concern analysis imports reasoning from the core political speech tradition; Snyder v. Phelps (core_political_speech slice) applies public concern doctrine in a press-adjacent context. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech doctrine governs what speech is protected; freedom of the press doctrine governs how press defendants are treated in defamation and related torts. Overlapping but not hierarchical — press freedom is not a sub-category of core political speech.

SCHEMA ISSUE A: This GOVERNED_BY edge is accurate but incomplete. The public_figure_classification_test is a threshold classification that determines which other test applies (actual_malice_test vs. negligence). The schema has no way to represent a DoctrinalTest that is a precondition to another DoctrinalTest. The conditional relationship between this test and actual_malice_test requires a new edge type or edge attribute.

Sullivan applied First Amendment protections against a state defamation judgment via the 14th Amendment. This is the incorporation mechanism — 1st Amendment applied against state through 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Sullivan distinguished the prior restraint context from the post-publication liability context. Near established press freedom from prior restraint; Sullivan established press freedom from post-publication defamation liability. Brennan's Sullivan opinion cited Near as part of the historical foundation for robust press protection.

Expands: Butts extended the constitutional protection — and the actual malice standard — from public officials to public figures. The plurality applied a different standard (highly unreasonable conduct) but Warren's concurrence, which was adopted in Gertz, applied full actual malice. The expansion is ultimately confirmed by Gertz.

Warren's Butts concurrence argued for applying full actual malice to public figures. Gertz's majority adopted this position, superseding Harlan's plurality standard from Butts. This is a clean INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES instance: a concurrence in an earlier case became the majority rule in a later case.

Clarifies: Gertz established that actual malice applies to public figures (confirming Warren's Butts concurrence, superseding Harlan's plurality standard) and established the public figure taxonomy that determines who must prove actual malice. It also clarified that actual malice governs presumed and punitive damages even for private figure plaintiffs.

Extends: Hustler extended the actual malice standard to IIED claims by public figures — a different cause of action (tort, not defamation) in a new legal context. The doctrine's prongs are unchanged; its applicability is expanded to a new category of claims. This is a clean use of the MODIFIES(extends) direction value added in v0.4.

Snyder v. Phelps extended Hustler's reasoning to private plaintiff IIED claims in a public concern context, distinguishing Hustler (public figure plaintiff) while reaching a similar result through the public concern doctrine. The doctrinal connection between these two cases is important for understanding the full scope of IIED protection for press-adjacent speech.

Clarifies: Milkovich added the opinion/fact distinction as prong 3 of the actual malice test by establishing that a statement must be capable of being proven false to be actionable. This clarifies the scope of the test without narrowing or expanding it.
Stub governance edge. reporters_privilege_doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Phase 2 statute layer. pending_establishment on the doctrine node documents the Branzburg dependency.
Reporter's privilege claims, to the extent they exist constitutionally, derive from the Press Clause of the First Amendment.
The Free Exercise Clause is textually distinct from the Speech and Press Clauses — 'or prohibiting the free exercise thereof' — and provides the constitutional grounding for this area.

condition.context: Applies to non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws, and to individualized exemption systems
condition.note: Sherbert remains operative where Smith does not apply — non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws, unemployment compensation cases, and is restored by RFRA for federal laws

condition.context: Applies only to neutral, generally applicable laws
condition.note: Smith test governs neutral general laws only. Non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws trigger strict scrutiny regardless of Smith.

edges.notes: RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) involved a licensing scheme for religious solicitation that is simultaneously a prior restraint case and a Free Exercise case. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — prior restraint governs the mechanism of pre-publication suppression; free exercise governs government targeting of religious practice. Different analytical frameworks protecting related First Amendment interests.
Cantwell incorporated the Free Exercise Clause against states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Expands: Sherbert significantly expanded the protection of religiously motivated conduct by requiring the government to satisfy strict scrutiny before enforcing laws that substantially burden religious practice — even neutral generally applicable laws.

Expands: Yoder extended the Sherbert compelling interest framework to compulsory education laws and established that the government's interest in compulsory schooling was not compelling enough to override deeply held religious objections backed by a demonstrated religious way of life. The case set the high-water mark for Free Exercise protection.

Narrows: Smith significantly narrowed Sherbert by holding that the compelling interest test applies only to non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws — not to neutral general laws that incidentally burden religious practice. This is not a full overruling of Sherbert (which remains operative in unemployment compensation cases and analogous contexts) but a major narrowing of its scope.

Narrows: Smith significantly narrowed Free Exercise protection for religiously motivated conduct by holding that the Free Exercise Clause does not require religious exemptions from neutral, generally applicable laws.

Harlan's Sherbert dissent anticipated an important strand of Smith's reasoning: concern that constitutionally compelled religion-specific exemptions from neutral laws raise structural and administrability problems.

Clarifies: Lukumi gave definitive content to what Smith meant by neutral and generally applicable — standards that Smith articulated but did not fully define. This is a classic clarification: it resolves ambiguity in the test without narrowing or expanding its scope.

Clarifies: Fulton significantly expanded the general applicability inquiry by holding that a categorical exception in a contract that allows for discretionary secular exemptions makes the entire policy not generally applicable — even if no such exemption had ever been granted. This is a clarification that in practice significantly expands when strict scrutiny applies, but without formally changing the Smith framework.

Expands the general-applicability branch of the doctrine by treating discretionary secular exemptions as sufficient to defeat general applicability, even absent evidence that such exemptions were actually granted. The neutrality branch is not directly implicated by Fulton's holding.
The Establishment Clause — 'Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion' — is the textual source. Incorporated against states via 14th Amendment in Everson (1947).

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both clauses appear in the same sentence of the First Amendment and are GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) implicated both; Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) decided both Free Exercise and Establishment Clause claims; RFRA cases raise both. (3) Complementary but structurally tensioned — the two clauses protect the same interest (religious liberty) but through opposing commands: Establishment Clause prohibits government promotion of religion; Free Exercise Clause prohibits government suppression of religion. Laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause challenge; laws that are neutral toward religion risk Free Exercise challenge. This structural tension is unique to the Religion Clauses — no other RELATED_TO pair has this opposing-commands relationship. SCHEMA ISSUE D: The Religion Clauses' structural tension is different from the complementary relationship between prior restraint and core political speech. Consider adding a relationship_type attribute to RELATED_TO edges: complementary | structurally_tensioned | historically_linked. relationship_type: structurally_tensioned — the two clauses protect the same interest through opposing commands, creating genuine constitutional tension rather than mere doctrinal overlap.
Historical governance only. The Lemon test governed Establishment Clause analysis from 1971 until it was explicitly overruled by Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022). valid_until: 2022-06-27.

condition.context: Particularly applicable in school and captive-audience contexts
condition.note: The coercion test survived Kennedy v. Bremerton and remains operative, especially in school prayer contexts where captive audience and authority relationships make coercion analysis most relevant.

edges.notes: The post-Kennedy governing standard. Operative from June 27, 2022 forward.
Everson incorporated the Establishment Clause against the states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Clarifies: Lemon established the first structured analytical framework for Establishment Clause analysis, giving the doctrine a concrete operational test. This clarified how courts apply the doctrine without narrowing or expanding its substantive scope.
Lee applied Lemon alongside the new coercion test. valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling.

Scalia's Lee dissent (1992) argued for a history-and-tradition approach to Establishment Clause analysis rather than the coercion test or Lemon. Gorsuch's Kennedy majority adopted this approach 30 years later. This is the second-longest INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES gap in the graph (30 years, compared to Holmes-Abrams to Brandenburg at 50 years).

Complicates: Van Orden introduced significant uncertainty about the Lemon test's continued vitality. The plurality openly moved toward a history-and-tradition approach; Breyer's controlling concurrence applied ad hoc balancing. Combined with McCreary's opposite result on the same day, Van Orden significantly complicated the doctrinal picture.

Van Orden and McCreary were companion cases decided the same day reaching opposite results on factually different Ten Commandments displays. The contrast illustrates that Establishment Clause analysis of religious displays is highly context-specific. Companion-case contrast; direction is conventional rather than temporal.
valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling by Kennedy.

Scalia's McCreary dissent (2005) argued the Establishment Clause was not intended to require government neutrality between religion and irreligion — the founding-era understanding permitted acknowledgment of a monotheistic God. This historical argument was adopted by the Kennedy v. Bremerton majority 17 years later.

edges.citation: Kennedy v. Bremerton School District, 597 U.S. 507, 534 (2022)
edges.notes: Kennedy treated Lemon and the endorsement test as no longer governing and stated that the Court had abandoned them. This functions as an explicit repudiation of Lemon's governing force, even if the opinion did not use the simplest formulaic 'Lemon is overruled' phrasing.

Repudiates: Kennedy explicitly abandoned the Lemon test. Changed from OVERRULES (invalid Case->DoctrinalTest) to MODIFIES(repudiates). The Case->Case OVERRULES edge (kennedy_overrules_lemon) handles the case-level overruling; this edge handles the test-level repudiation. W001 cascade acknowledged: the lemon_test is ESTABLISHED by lemon_v_kurtzman which is overruled — the test is correctly marked status:overruled with valid_until:2022-06-27.

Complicates: Kennedy replaced a settled (if criticized) analytical framework with a deliberately open-ended historical inquiry. The doctrine is now more complex and less predictable than it was under Lemon — the test's content is actively being worked out in lower courts. This is the clearest instance of the 'complicates' direction in the graph: a case that replaced certainty with uncertainty.

Kennedy created tension with prior coercion cases, especially in school settings, but did not expressly reformulate Lee's coercion standard. The decision held on its own facts that the district's response violated Free Exercise and Free Speech and that the Establishment Clause did not require suppression of the coach's prayer.
Grounds the expressive association branch. Intimate association branch is separately grounded in amendment_14_section_1 — see intimate_association_doctrine GROUNDED_IN edge.

Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge for intimate association, which is grounded in the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause, not the First Amendment. This is the first use of a Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge in the graph — the schema permits GROUNDED_IN from Area or Doctrine to ConstitutionalProvision.

RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Hurley v. Irish-American Gay Group (1995) is simultaneously a compelled speech case (core political speech) and a compelled association case; Dale's expressive association analysis imports compelled speech reasoning. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech protects expression; freedom of association protects the right to join with others in expressive activity. Compelled association doctrine often merges with compelled speech doctrine. SCHEMA ISSUE B: The overlap between compelled association and compelled speech (Hurley, Dale) means some cases are simultaneously in both doctrinal lines. The schema models this correctly through edges but the conceptual boundary between the two areas is genuinely porous.
NAACP applied the First Amendment through the 14th Amendment against state action — foundational incorporation application.

Expands: Hurley extended the expressive association framework to parade organizers — establishing that an event itself (not just a membership organization) can be an expressive association whose organizers have the right to control the event's message. This expanded the category of entities that can invoke expressive association protection beyond membership organizations.

Expands: Dale significantly expanded expressive association protection by (1) applying it to a large national organization; (2) giving substantial deference to the organization's characterization of its expressive purpose; and (3) treating an unarticulated anti-homosexuality stance as part of the organization's expressive mission. This expanded both the category of organizations that qualify and the deference courts give to their claims.
The Petition Clause — 'or the right of the people...to petition the Government for a redress of grievances' — is the textual source.

RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Button (1963) recognized litigation as political expression, connecting petition and speech doctrine; Brandenburg's protections for political advocacy extend to petitioning activity. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech protects expression of political views; freedom of petition protects the right to direct that expression at government. Petitioning is a specific form of political expression directed at government action.

RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — NAACP v. Button (1963) is simultaneously a petition case and an association case — the NAACP's litigation activity is both petitioning government and expressive association; NAACP v. Alabama (1958, association slice) and Button (petition slice) are companion cases from the same civil rights context. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — freedom of association protects the right to join together; freedom of petition protects the right to direct collective action at government. Group petitioning is the intersection.
First Amendment implicit association grounding for intimate association doctrine.

Clarifies: Dale clarified that organizational assertions of expressive burden receive near-total judicial deference — courts will not second-guess an organization's characterization of its own expressive mission. This resolved a question left open by Roberts about how much deference courts owe to organizational self-characterization.

Dale distinguished Roberts by holding that the Boy Scouts — unlike the Jaycees — is an expressive organization whose selectivity on the contested characteristic (sexual orientation) is integral to its expressive mission. The Jaycees' exclusion of women was unrelated to its expressive activities; the Boy Scouts' exclusion of gay scoutmasters was allegedly central to its expressive mission of teaching Scout values.

Narrows: Bonta added a narrow tailoring requirement to the compelled disclosure test, tightening the standard beyond the prior substantial relationship test. This narrows the government's ability to compel disclosure — government must now show not just a substantial interest but that its disclosure requirement is narrowly tailored to serve that interest.
Expands: Bonta expanded the protection for associational privacy by adding the narrow tailoring requirement — making it harder for government to justify compelled disclosure requirements.
Stub governance edge — intimate_association_doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Due Process slice.

Clarifies: California Motor Transport extended the Noerr doctrine to courts and agencies (not just legislatures), gave it full First Amendment grounding, and introduced the sham exception. This is a clarification that both expanded the doctrine's reach and defined its outer limit.

Clarifies: Guarnieri clarified that the Petition Clause does not provide greater protection than the Speech Clause for public employee claims — both require that the petition or speech address a matter of public concern. This resolved a circuit split on whether public employee petition claims had an independent standard.

The longest INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES gap in the graph — 50 years. Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) argued that the First Amendment requires imminence and likelihood of harm before speech may be punished, and that the marketplace of ideas should be trusted to reject dangerous expression. Brandenburg adopted this reasoning wholesale in 1969, overruling the bad tendency test that the Abrams majority had applied.

Holmes's Gitlow dissent (1925) reiterated and deepened the Abrams argument — requiring imminence, likelihood of harm, and deference to the marketplace of ideas. Brandenburg (1969) adopted this position 44 years later. Two Holmes dissents, two INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edges, both converging on Brandenburg.
Gitlow was the first case to incorporate a Bill of Rights provision against the states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

condition.context: Applies in public school settings only
condition.note: Student speech doctrine is a specialized sub-doctrine of core political speech operative only in the public school context

condition.context: Applies in public school settings only
condition.note: Tinker test governs student speech in public schools

edges.notes: Narrows: Bethel carved out the first exception to Tinker's substantial-disruption test, holding that lewd, vulgar, or plainly offensive speech may be restricted in the school context without a disruption showing.
Narrows: Hazelwood carved out the second exception to Tinker — school-sponsored speech may be regulated for legitimate pedagogical concerns without showing substantial disruption.
Narrows: Morse carved out the third exception to Tinker — speech promoting illegal drug use may be restricted without a disruption showing.

Clarifies the boundary between permissible accommodation and impermissible preference in the aid/accommodation doctrine — a tax exemption that confers a pure financial benefit on religion without a corresponding burden crosses into impermissible preference.

Clarifies that exempting religious organizations from generally applicable civil regulations is permissible accommodation, not establishment — the exemption relieves a genuine regulatory burden on religious autonomy.

Texas Monthly distinguished Amos on the ground that a tax exemption conferring pure financial benefit differs from a regulatory exemption relieving a genuine burden on religious exercise. This distinction defines the accommodation/preference line.
Narrows: R.A.V. added viewpoint-neutrality requirement even within the fighting words category — government cannot selectively target fighting words based on topic or viewpoint.

Virginia v. Black distinguished R.A.V. by holding that a ban on cross burning with intent to intimidate is NOT impermissibly content-discriminatory. It falls within the R.A.V. exception permitting regulation of a subset of unprotected speech when the basis for the regulation consists entirely of the very reason the entire class of speech is unprotected (fear of violence).

Mitchell distinguished R.A.V. by holding that sentence enhancements for bias-motivated conduct target criminal acts rather than expression — the First Amendment does not prohibit evidentiary use of speech to establish criminal motive.

Narrows: Cohen limited the fighting words doctrine by requiring that challenged speech be directed at a specific individual in a face-to-face confrontation — generalized offensive expression in a public space is not fighting words.

Expands: Ferber created a new categorical exception for child pornography that operates independently of the Miller test — child pornography is unprotected even if it would not satisfy the Miller obscenity standard.
Metromedia applied Central Hudson to outdoor advertising restrictions, holding that sign ordinances cannot prefer commercial speech over noncommercial speech.
Expands: Richmond Newspapers expanded press rights by establishing a First Amendment right of access to criminal trials — a positive press right, not just a shield from compelled disclosure.

Clarifies: Tornillo established that the First Amendment prohibits government from compelling newspapers to publish content they would not otherwise publish, securing absolute editorial autonomy for the print press. This is distinct from defamation doctrine — it governs compelled speech, not liability for published speech.

Narrows: Dun & Bradstreet limited Sullivan/Gertz by holding that their First Amendment protections apply only to speech on matters of public concern — private speech receives no special constitutional protection in defamation.

Narrows: Zelman narrowed the Establishment Clause's restrictions on government aid by holding that neutral voucher programs that flow to religious schools through private choice do not constitute an establishment even if the aid directly funds religious education.

Clarifies via O'Connor concurrence: Lynch v. Donnelly produced the endorsement test (O'Connor, J., concurring) — government violates the Establishment Clause when it sends a message that religion is favored or that religious adherence is relevant to political standing.
Schenck's 'clear and present danger' test was the first articulation of the incitement framework. It governed until formally replaced by Brandenburg's imminence requirement.

Dennis applied a gravity-discounted danger test during the Cold War era — effectively approving punishment for political speech based on the seriousness of the threatened harm rather than its imminence. This approach was repudiated by Brandenburg.

Brandeis's Whitney concurrence articulated the imminence requirement and the marketplace of ideas rationale that became Brandenburg's governing test 42 years later. Along with Holmes's Abrams and Gitlow dissents, Whitney's Brandeis concurrence is one of the foundational minority opinions that became the law of the land.

Sherbert v. Verner reached a seemingly inconsistent result with Braunfeld — both involved neutral laws burdening religious exercise, but Sherbert required compelling interest justification while Braunfeld did not. Employment Division v. Smith resolved the tension by returning to the Braunfeld approach.

Clarifies: Feiner identified the outer limit of hostile audience protection — when imminent uncontrollable violence exists, police may order speaker to desist — but courts have read Feiner narrowly to avoid the heckler's veto.

Roth was the foundational case first categorically excluding obscenity from First Amendment protection and establishing the community standards/prurient interest test. Miller v. California (1973) replaced the Roth test with the SLAPS standard.

Repudiates: Miller explicitly rejected and replaced the Roth/Memoirs 'utterly without redeeming social value' standard with the three-part SLAPS test, providing a more workable definition of obscenity.

Ashcroft limited Ferber by holding that virtual child pornography not involving actual children falls outside the Ferber categorical exception — Ferber's rationale (harm to real children in production) does not apply to virtual images.

Clarifies: Mosley articulated the foundational content-neutrality principle — government has no power to restrict expression because of its message, ideas, subject matter, or content — and established that selective exemptions from content-neutral restrictions based on message violate this principle.
Clarifies: Perry formalized the three-category public forum taxonomy (traditional, designated, nonpublic) that now structures all public forum analysis.

Tornillo distinguished Red Lion by refusing to apply broadcast-style content obligations to newspapers — the scarcity rationale does not apply to print media, which is entitled to full editorial autonomy.

Clarifies: Turner established that cable television is intermediate between broadcast (Red Lion) and print (Tornillo) — cable operators receive more First Amendment protection than broadcasters. Content-neutral structural regulations (like must-carry rules) are subject to intermediate scrutiny under O'Brien, not the deferential broadcast standard. Content-based regulations on cable receive strict scrutiny.
Pap's A.M. resolved the Barnes plurality confusion by establishing that a majority adopts the secondary effects rationale for nude dancing ordinances.
Zelman extended Mueller's neutrality/private choice framework from indirect tax deductions to direct tuition vouchers flowing to religious schools.

Narrows: Gooding established that fighting words statutes must be narrowly drawn to track Chaplinsky's precise definition — broader statutes sweeping in merely offensive speech are unconstitutionally overbroad.

Expands: Lovell expanded prior restraint doctrine beyond judicial injunctions (as in Near) to encompass administrative licensing schemes, holding that granting an official unbridled discretion to permit or deny distribution of literature is a facially unconstitutional prior restraint.

Applies the categorical exclusion of libel (pre-Sullivan) to group defamation. Beauharnais relied on the historical exclusion of libelous speech from First Amendment protection, not the fighting words doctrine. R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul (1992) effectively undermined Beauharnais by prohibiting viewpoint discrimination even within unprotected categories.

R.A.V. effectively undermined Beauharnais by holding that content discrimination within unprotected categories (like fighting words) is presumptively unconstitutional — the group libel approach of Beauharnais cannot survive R.A.V.'s viewpoint-neutrality requirement.

Debs applied the Schenck clear and present danger test to uphold conviction of the Socialist Party's presidential candidate for anti-war speech — broader application than Schenck's leaflet distribution.
Frohwerk applied the Schenck clear and present danger test to uphold conviction of a newspaper editor who published anti-war articles.

Narrows: Alameda Books tightened the secondary effects doctrine by requiring actual evidentiary support linking the regulation to the targeted effects — cities cannot simply assert secondary effects without providing evidence.
Adderley laid the intellectual groundwork for the nonpublic forum category, which Perry subsequently formalized and systematized into the modern three-category public forum framework.

opinion_ref.author: Black
opinion_ref.note: Adderley majority established the nonpublic forum concept that Perry systematized

opinion_ref.author: Rehnquist
opinion_ref.note: Van Orden plurality moved away from Lemon toward history-and-tradition analysis later embraced in Kennedy

edges.notes: Van Orden's plurality moved away from Lemon and toward a history-and-tradition mode of analysis later embraced in Kennedy v. Bremerton. Along with Scalia's Lee and McCreary dissents, Van Orden is a key way station in the intellectual arc from Lemon to Kennedy.

Virginia v. Black clarified the true threats doctrine by holding that cross burning with intent to intimidate is a constitutionally proscribable true threat — articulating the 'serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence' formulation that became central to subsequent true threats analysis including Counterman.

Clarifies: Yates required that incitement prosecution prove advocacy of illegal action rather than abstract advocacy of dangerous ideas — effectively gutting Dennis without formally overruling it. The abstract doctrine/action advocacy distinction became central to Brandenburg's reformulation twelve years later.

Yates distinguished Dennis without overruling it by requiring proof of advocacy of illegal action rather than abstract doctrine — effectively rendering the Smith Act unenforceable against theoretical advocacy.

Clarifies: Reed hardened and broadened the content-based/content-neutral distinction by holding that any facial subject-matter distinction triggers strict scrutiny regardless of the government's motive — the most significant expansion of content-neutrality doctrine since Mosley (1972).
Reed extended Mosley's content-neutrality principle by making facial subject-matter distinctions categorically content-based, subject to strict scrutiny regardless of discriminatory intent.

Davis applied Buckley's expenditure protection to strike down the Millionaire's Amendment — a scheme that raised opponents' contribution limits when a self-funded candidate spent beyond a threshold. Any mechanism penalizing a candidate's decision to spend personal funds violates the First Amendment.
Expands: Davis extended Buckley's protection of candidate expenditures to cover asymmetric regulatory schemes that penalize spending decisions, not just direct expenditure caps.

Expands: Arizona Free Enterprise extended Davis's reasoning from asymmetric contribution limits to triggered public matching fund systems, completing the doctrinal foreclosure of legislative attempts to level the playing field through reactive funding schemes.

Clarifies: Stanley established that the obscenity doctrine's categorical exclusion does not extend to private possession in the home — the state's interest in regulating obscenity stops at the threshold of private possession for personal use.

Paris Adult Theatre explicitly held that Stanley's private-possession right does not extend to commercial exhibition — states may regulate obscene material in adult theaters even for consenting adults, because the privacy rationale of Stanley does not apply to commercial settings.

opinion_ref.author: Harlan
opinion_ref.note: Yates majority laid the abstract/action distinction that Brandenburg formalized

edges.notes: Yates laid the intellectual groundwork for Brandenburg by requiring proof of advocacy of illegal action rather than abstract doctrine. Brandenburg absorbed this distinction and formalized it into the modern imminence/likelihood test.
Upheld content-neutral ban on signs on public property under intermediate scrutiny, establishing aesthetic interests as a substantial government interest.
Ladue distinguished Taxpayers for Vincent by emphasizing the unique significance of the home as a forum — a total ban on residential signs forecloses a communication medium unavailable anywhere else.

Clarifies: Bolger established the three-factor test (the 'Bolger factors') for determining whether speech is commercial: (1) whether it is an advertisement, (2) whether it refers to a specific product, and (3) whether the speaker has an economic motivation.

Clarifies: Zauderer established a separate, more lenient standard for compelled commercial disclosures — requiring only reasonable relation to preventing deception — distinct from Central Hudson's intermediate scrutiny for restrictions on commercial speech. Clarifies: Zauderer created a bifurcated commercial speech standard — compelled disclosures only require reasonable relation to preventing deception (not Central Hudson's intermediate scrutiny), while restrictions on commercial speech still require Central Hudson analysis.

Narrows: Florida Bar created a limited exception to the commercial speech protection for targeted attorney direct mail established in Shapero, upholding a 30-day waiting period after accidents under Central Hudson.

Matal held that trademarks are private speech entitled to full First Amendment protection — viewpoint discrimination in trademark registration is unconstitutional regardless of the commercial context.

Narrows: City of Austin clarified and narrowed Reed v. Town of Gilbert's content-based definition — a regulation is not automatically content-based simply because one must read a sign to apply it. Only regulations requiring examination of communicative content (topic, viewpoint) are content-based.

Dred Scott (1857) predates the Fourteenth Amendment (1868) by eleven years. This edge represents the constitutional void that the Amendment was designed to fill — the Amendment's citizenship clause directly repudiated Taney's holding that Black persons could not be citizens.

Strauder was the first significant equal protection case, establishing that the Fourteenth Amendment prohibits racial discrimination in jury selection and reading the Amendment's purpose as securing full civic equality for Black citizens.

Narrows: Plessy dramatically narrowed the equal protection guarantee established in Strauder by holding that separate but equal facilities satisfied the Fourteenth Amendment — effectively gutting the Amendment's promise of civic equality for 58 years.

opinion_ref.author: Harlan
opinion_ref.note: Harlan's color-blind Constitution dissent was adopted as law in Brown 58 years later

edges.notes: Justice Harlan's Plessy dissent — arguing that the Constitution is color-blind and permits no caste distinctions — was vindicated and effectively adopted in Brown v. Board of Education 58 years later.

Brown overruled Plessy in the field of public education — holding that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal. The separate but equal doctrine was subsequently dismantled in other contexts. Coded as effective rather than explicit because Brown did not use the word overrule.

Expands: Brown dramatically expanded the equal protection guarantee by holding that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal and abolishing the separate but equal doctrine in public education.

Expands: Bolling extended equal protection principles to the federal government via the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause — making the federal government subject to the same non-discrimination requirements as states under the Fourteenth Amendment.
