Bolling grounded federal equal protection in the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause rather than the Fourteenth Amendment, which applies only to states.

Korematsu was the first case to apply the strict scrutiny label to racial classifications — articulating that legal restrictions curtailing the rights of a single racial group are immediately suspect and subject to the most rigid scrutiny.

Washington v. Davis established the foundational rule that facially neutral laws require proof of discriminatory intent — not merely disparate impact — to trigger strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause.

Clarifies: Arlington Heights elaborated the Washington v. Davis discriminatory intent requirement with a multi-factor framework for proving intent from circumstantial evidence — impact, historical background, sequence of events, procedural departures, and legislative history.

McCleskey applied Washington v. Davis to hold that statistical evidence of systemic racial disparities in capital sentencing is insufficient to prove discriminatory intent in an individual defendant's case.

Clarifies: Adarand settled that strict scrutiny applies to all racial classifications regardless of purpose or benefited group — establishing the consistency and congruence principles that govern affirmative action analysis.

Grutter applied strict scrutiny to uphold race-conscious holistic admissions, establishing student body diversity as a compelling interest — the major exception to the near-fatal application of strict scrutiny to racial classifications.

opinion_ref.author: Thomas
opinion_ref.note: Thomas's Grutter dissent arguing diversity is not a compelling interest was adopted as law in SFFA 20 years later

edges.notes: Thomas's Grutter dissent — arguing that diversity is not a compelling interest and that all racial preferences are unconstitutional — was vindicated and effectively adopted by the Roberts Court majority in SFFA v. Harvard 20 years later.

Narrows: SFFA dramatically narrowed the affirmative action doctrine by eliminating student body diversity as a compelling interest and effectively ending race-conscious admissions in higher education.

Clarifies: Reed v. Reed was the breakthrough case invalidating a sex-based classification, opening the path toward heightened scrutiny for sex, but the quasi-suspect classification doctrine in its modern form was established in Craig v. Boren (1976).

Clarifies: Frontiero's plurality would have made sex a suspect classification requiring strict scrutiny — the high-water mark of that effort — but fell one vote short of a majority, leaving the standard unsettled until Craig v. Boren.

Craig v. Boren formally established intermediate scrutiny as the constitutional standard for sex-based classifications — settling the standard that Frontiero's plurality had failed to establish as a majority holding.
Clarifies: Craig formally established sex as a quasi-suspect classification subject to intermediate scrutiny, resolving the uncertainty left by Frontiero's fractured plurality.

Complicates: VMI added the exceedingly persuasive justification requirement to intermediate scrutiny for sex classifications — a standard that approaches but does not formally reach strict scrutiny, making the tier harder to define precisely.
Beazer illustrates rational basis review at its most deferential — sustaining a categorical employment exclusion based on conceivable safety and efficiency rationales despite evidence of overbreadth.

Clarifies: Moreno established that bare congressional desire to harm a politically unpopular group cannot constitute a legitimate government interest — introducing the animus principle into rational basis review that Cleburne and Romer would later develop.

Clarifies: Cleburne applied rational basis with unusual rigor — effectively rational basis with bite — striking a zoning denial based on irrational prejudice against the disabled while explicitly declining to make disability a quasi-suspect classification.

Clarifies: Romer applied Moreno's animus principle to sexual orientation, completing the Moreno-Cleburne-Romer rational basis with bite trilogy and establishing that bare desire to harm gay and lesbian persons is not a legitimate government interest.
Romer is the foundational constitutional protection for sexual orientation under equal protection, leading directly to Lawrence v. Texas (2003) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015).

Harper established voting as a fundamental right whose unequal distribution triggers strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause — the foundational case for the fundamental rights strand of equal protection.
Connection captured through fundamental_rights_ep_doctrine rather than direct strict scrutiny test application.
Reynolds applied the fundamental right to vote to require population-based apportionment of both chambers of state legislatures — establishing one person, one vote as a constitutional requirement.
Connection captured through fundamental_rights_ep_doctrine rather than direct strict scrutiny test application.
Expands: Shapiro extended the fundamental rights EP doctrine to the right to travel interstate, establishing that welfare residency requirements penalizing travel trigger strict scrutiny.

Narrows: Rodriguez established the major limit on the fundamental rights EP doctrine — holding that education is not a fundamental right and wealth is not a suspect classification, foreclosing federal equal protection challenges to school funding disparities.

opinion_ref.author: O'Connor
opinion_ref.note: O'Connor's Romer equal protection reasoning was applied in Lawrence's equal protection path

edges.notes: Romer's animus-based equal protection analysis intellectually preceded Lawrence — O'Connor's Lawrence concurrence applied Romer's equal protection reasoning directly to the Texas sodomy statute.

Lawrence was decided on due process grounds but O'Connor's concurrence identified an equal protection path — the Texas statute's application only to same-sex couples raised equal protection concerns under Romer's animus rationale.

Expands: Obergefell extended the fundamental right to marry to same-sex couples under both the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses — the culmination of the Romer-Lawrence-Obergefell constitutional arc.

Obergefell fused due process and equal protection — it did not present as a clean strict scrutiny equal protection case. The RELATED_TO edge captures the connection without overstating doctrinal formalism.

opinion_ref.author: Warren
opinion_ref.note: Loving's recognition of marriage as a fundamental right was directly applied in Obergefell (majority opinion — plurality used as schema approximation for INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES)

edges.notes: Loving v. Virginia's recognition of marriage as a fundamental right and its rejection of race-based marriage restrictions as unconstitutional were directly applied in Obergefell — Kennedy's majority opinion cited Loving as the foundational precedent for the right to marry regardless of identity.

opinion_ref.author: Kennedy
opinion_ref.note: Romer's equal protection protection for gay persons preceded Obergefell's extension of marriage rights (majority opinion — plurality used as schema approximation for INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES)

edges.notes: Romer established that gay and lesbian persons are entitled to constitutional equal protection — the foundational premise that Obergefell extended to the right to marry.

opinion_ref.author: Harlan
opinion_ref.note: Harlan's color-blind Constitution dissent was invoked by Roberts in SFFA to justify colorblind equal protection

edges.notes: Justice Harlan's Plessy dissent — arguing the Constitution is color-blind and permits no caste distinctions — was explicitly invoked by Chief Justice Roberts in SFFA v. Harvard to justify the colorblind equal protection principle that bars race-conscious admissions.
Companion cases decided the same day holding state and federal school segregation unconstitutional under the Fourteenth and Fifth Amendments respectively.

Rice applied strict scrutiny to strike an ancestry-based voting restriction, holding that ancestry classifications that function as racial proxies are subject to the same constitutional scrutiny as explicit racial classifications.
Bandemer held partisan gerrymandering claims justiciable under equal protection — later overruled on justiciability by Rucho v. Common Cause (2019).

Complicates: Vieth's plurality would have overruled Bandemer as non-justiciable, but Kennedy's concurrence refused — leaving partisan gerrymandering's justiciability under equal protection nominally intact but practically undermined until Rucho v. Common Cause (2019).
Bush v. Gore applied the fundamental right to vote to require uniform standards in manual recounts — but expressly limited the holding to its unique facts, making its precedential value minimal.

Expands: Griffin established the access-to-courts strand of fundamental rights equal protection, holding that states cannot condition meaningful appellate review on ability to pay for a trial transcript.
Expands: Douglas extended Griffin's access-to-courts doctrine from the right to a trial transcript to the right to appointed counsel on first appeal as of right.
Dandridge established that welfare benefit classifications receive only rational basis review, rejecting poverty as a suspect classification or welfare as a fundamental right.
Rodriguez applied Dandridge's rational basis framework to reject education as a fundamental right and wealth as a suspect classification.
Clarifies: Saenz grounded the right to travel in the Privileges or Immunities Clause rather than equal protection, clarifying the constitutional basis for the travel right established in Shapiro.

Saenz distinguished Shapiro by grounding the right of new residents to equal welfare benefits in the Privileges or Immunities Clause rather than equal protection — a significant doctrinal shift in the constitutional basis for the travel right.

Saenz grounded the right of newly arrived citizens in the Privileges or Immunities Clause of Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment — a significant revival of that clause after its dormancy since the Slaughterhouse Cases (1873). SCHEMA NOTE: Add saenz_interprets_privileges_immunities edge when amendment_14_privileges_immunities clause node is added.
Bowers applied rational basis to uphold Georgia's sodomy law as applied to homosexual conduct, finding the longstanding criminalization of such conduct a sufficient rational basis.

Lawrence explicitly overruled Bowers v. Hardwick, stating it was wrong when decided and that its reasoning should be and now is rejected. The decision was grounded in substantive due process liberty rather than equal protection.

opinion_ref.author: Blackmun
opinion_ref.note: Blackmun's Bowers dissent arguing the case was about the fundamental right to be left alone was adopted in Lawrence

edges.notes: Blackmun's Bowers dissent — reframing the issue as being about the fundamental right to be left alone rather than a 'right to engage in homosexual sodomy' — was effectively adopted by Kennedy's Lawrence majority, which similarly reframed the question around liberty rather than the specific conduct.
The Due Process Clause is one of five clauses of the Fifth Amendment.
Dobbs explicitly overrules Roe v. Wade, holding that the Constitution does not confer a right to abortion.
Dobbs explicitly overrules Planned Parenthood v. Casey, which had reaffirmed Roe's essential holding.
Dobbs applies the substantive due process doctrine to determine whether abortion is a protected liberty interest, concluding that it is not deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition.
Dobbs applies stare decisis principles to justify overruling Roe and Casey, identifying five factors that weigh in favor of overruling.
Dobbs holds that rational-basis review is the appropriate standard for evaluating state abortion regulations, as abortion is not a fundamental constitutional right.
Obergefell explicitly overrules Baker v. Nelson, holding that the exclusion of same-sex couples from marriage does present a substantial federal question.
Obergefell establishes that the fundamental right to marry extends to same-sex couples under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Obergefell establishes that state laws denying same-sex couples the right to marry violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Substantive due process is grounded in the Due Process Clause of Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Griswold established the constitutional doctrine of marital privacy as a fundamental right protected by the Bill of Rights.

Griswold invokes the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause, particularly through Harlan's concurrence which grounds the right to marital privacy in substantive due process rather than the Bill of Rights penumbra.

Lawrence clarifies and expands the substantive due process liberty doctrine by explicitly recognizing that liberty protects intimate conduct between consenting adults and extends beyond spatial bounds to include autonomy of self.
Lawrence establishes the doctrine that private, consensual sexual conduct between adults is protected from criminal prohibition under the Due Process Clause.
Cruzan established the doctrine that a competent person has a liberty interest under the Due Process Clause in refusing unwanted medical treatment.

Cruzan established the clear and convincing evidence standard as a constitutionally permissible procedural safeguard for decisions regarding withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment for incompetent persons.
Meyer applied the substantive due process liberty doctrine to protect parental rights and occupational freedom from arbitrary state interference.
Meyer established the substantive due process rationality test requiring that laws interfering with liberty have a reasonable relation to a legitimate state purpose.
Meyer established that parental rights to direct children's education, including the choice of subjects and instructors, are fundamental liberties protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.

Pierce applied and reinforced Meyer's doctrine of parental rights in education, holding that Oregon's compulsory public school attendance law unconstitutionally interfered with parents' fundamental right to direct their children's education.
Lochner v. New York is the foundational case establishing the doctrine of liberty of contract as a fundamental right protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause.
Lochner v. New York establishes the test for determining whether a state regulation is a valid exercise of police power or an unconstitutional interference with liberty of contract.
Lochner applies the doctrine of police power while establishing that this power has constitutional limits when it interferes with liberty of contract.
Palko established the ordered liberty doctrine as the framework for determining which Bill of Rights protections apply to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Palko established the selective incorporation doctrine, rejecting total incorporation and adopting a case-by-case approach based on fundamental fairness and ordered liberty.
Palko established the ordered liberty test as the operative standard for determining incorporation of Bill of Rights protections into the Fourteenth Amendment.
Skinner establishes procreation and marriage as fundamental rights requiring constitutional protection under the Fourteenth Amendment.

Loving v. Virginia established that racial classifications in state statutes, particularly criminal statutes, are subject to the most rigid scrutiny and can only be upheld if necessary to accomplish a permissible state objective independent of invidious racial discrimination.

Loving v. Virginia established the freedom to marry as a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, holding that this right cannot be restricted by invidious racial discriminations.
Moore established that family living arrangements involving blood relatives are protected liberty interests under substantive due process.
Skinner applied strict scrutiny to Oklahoma's sterilization act, holding that legislation affecting fundamental rights like procreation requires the most rigid scrutiny.
Troxel v. Granville established the parental fundamental right doctrine as a substantive due process protection under the Fourteenth Amendment.
Troxel v. Granville established the parental right protection test for evaluating state interference with parental decisions concerning child care, custody, and control.

Carolene Products applied rational basis review to uphold the Filled Milk Act, establishing that regulatory legislation affecting ordinary commercial transactions is presumed constitutional if it rests upon some rational basis.
Williamson applied rational basis review to uphold Oklahoma's optometry regulations, becoming the canonical example of deferential rational basis review for economic legislation.
Washington v. Davis established that discriminatory purpose, not merely disproportionate impact, is required to establish an equal protection violation under the Fifth Amendment.

Benton explicitly overruled Palko v. Connecticut, holding that the Double Jeopardy Clause applies to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment, contrary to Palko's holding that only a 'watered-down' version applied.
Benton established the doctrine that the Double Jeopardy Clause of the Fifth Amendment applies to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment.

Carolene Products established the rational basis test for equal protection review of economic and social legislation, including the famous Footnote 4 suggesting heightened scrutiny for legislation affecting discrete and insular minorities.

West Coast Hotel narrowed the Lochner-era liberty of contract doctrine, holding that liberty of contract is subject to reasonable state regulation in the interest of community health, safety, morals, and welfare. This effectively ended the Lochner era.
Explicitly overruled by syllabus point 6.
Criminal procedure rights apply to the states through selective incorporation via the Fourteenth Amendment.
Weeks v. United States established the exclusionary rule for federal prosecutions, holding that evidence obtained in violation of the Fourth Amendment must be excluded from trial.
The Court applied Fourth Amendment protections to the warrantless search and seizure of the defendant's home and private papers by a federal marshal.
Wolf applies the selective incorporation framework from Palko, holding that the Fourth Amendment's core protection is incorporated but the exclusionary rule is not required.
Wolf establishes that the Fourth Amendment's core protection against arbitrary police intrusion is a fundamental right incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment.
Wolf establishes that states have flexibility in choosing remedies for Fourth Amendment violations, including alternatives to the exclusionary rule such as damages actions and police discipline.
Mapp explicitly overrules Wolf's holding that the exclusionary rule is not an essential ingredient of the Fourth Amendment right enforceable against the states.
Mapp established that the exclusionary rule is a constitutionally mandated doctrine applicable to state prosecutions.
Mapp applied and extended the Fourth Amendment right to privacy to state prosecutions through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Gideon established that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies to state criminal prosecutions through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Escobedo v. Illinois established the multi-factor test for determining when the right to counsel attaches at the pretrial interrogation stage.
Escobedo v. Illinois established that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel extends to critical pretrial stages, including police interrogation.
Escobedo v. Illinois established the functional distinction between investigatory and accusatory stages, rejecting a purely formal test based on indictment.
Miranda v. Arizona established the Miranda Doctrine as a constitutional requirement for custodial interrogation.
Miranda v. Arizona established the Miranda Warnings Test as the operative standard for determining the admissibility of statements obtained during custodial interrogation.
Katz explicitly rejects the trespass doctrine of Olmstead, holding that Fourth Amendment protection does not depend on physical intrusion into a protected area.
Katz explicitly rejects Goldman's holding that surveillance without physical penetration falls outside Fourth Amendment protection.
Katz establishes the reasonable expectation of privacy doctrine as the framework for determining Fourth Amendment protection, replacing the property-based trespass doctrine.
Terry v. Ohio established the constitutional framework for stop-and-frisk searches under the Fourth Amendment.
Terry v. Ohio established the two-part test for evaluating the constitutionality of stops and frisks.
Dickerson affirms and applies the Miranda doctrine, holding that it is a constitutional rule that governs admissibility in both state and federal courts.
Illinois v. Gates established the totality of the circumstances test as the standard for evaluating probable cause from informant tips.
Illinois v. Gates established the modern doctrine governing probable cause determinations based on informant tips, replacing the rigid Aguilar-Spinelli framework.

Betts applied the right to counsel doctrine in a limited, circumstance-dependent form, holding that the right does not require appointment of counsel in all state felony cases. Overruled by Gideon v. Wainwright.
Massiah established the doctrine that the Sixth Amendment protects a defendant's right to counsel after indictment against government-elicited incriminating statements.
Strickland established the foundational two-prong test for ineffective assistance of counsel claims.
Strickland articulated the substantive doctrine that the Sixth Amendment guarantees effective assistance of counsel as a fundamental right.
Chimel established the scope limitation for searches incident to arrest.
United States v. Leon established the good-faith exception to the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule.
United States v. Leon established the good-faith reliance on warrant test for determining admissibility of evidence obtained pursuant to an invalid warrant.

United States v. Leon narrowed the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule by creating an exception for evidence obtained by officers acting in reasonable reliance on a facially valid warrant issued by a detached and neutral magistrate.
Carroll v. United States established the automobile exception to the Fourth Amendment warrant requirement.
Carroll v. United States established the two-prong test for warrantless automobile searches: probable cause and impracticability of obtaining a warrant.

Carroll v. United States applied and synthesized the probable cause standard from prior cases including Stacey v. Emery and Commonwealth v. Carey, defining it as facts and circumstances warranting a man of prudence and caution in believing an offense has been committed.
Kyllo established the sense-enhancing technology test as a framework for determining when use of technology constitutes a Fourth Amendment search.
Kyllo established the doctrine that the Fourth Amendment protects the home against warrantless searches using sense-enhancing technology not in general public use.
Carpenter established that digital location information from cell-site records receives Fourth Amendment protection despite the third-party doctrine.

Whren established the principle that probable cause to believe a traffic violation has occurred is sufficient to justify a traffic stop under the Fourth Amendment, regardless of the officer's subjective intent.
Riley v. California establishes the doctrine that warrantless searches of digital information on cell phones are generally unreasonable under the Fourth Amendment.
Riley v. California establishes the cell phone search warrant requirement test as the governing standard for warrantless searches of digital information on cell phones seized incident to arrest.
Riley narrows the search incident to arrest exception by holding it does not extend to digital information on cell phones seized from arrestees.

Riley applies and reinforces the Fourth Amendment warrant requirement, holding that warrants are generally required before searching digital information on cell phones and emphasizing that the warrant requirement is an important component of Fourth Amendment jurisprudence.

schema_issues_found.description: ESTABLISHES edge type implies positive doctrine creation. Valentine established a rule by denying protection, not by articulating a framework. Need holding_type attribute on Case nodes (affirmative | by_negation) or a separate ESTABLISHES_BY_NEGATION edge type.
Virginia Pharmacy did not explicitly overrule Valentine but is directly incompatible with it. OVERRULES edge needs an overrule_type attribute: explicit | implicit | effective.

MODIFIES is doing too much work. Posadas expanded Central Hudson deference, 44 Liquormart narrowed it, Sorrell complicated it without resolving it. Need a direction attribute: narrows | expands | complicates | repudiates | clarifies. Without this, MODIFIES edges carry no directionality and the research tool cannot tell users whether doctrine tightened or loosened.

A case can have both APPLIES and MODIFIES edges to the same target. Ambiguous whether MODIFIES should subsume APPLIES or coexist with it. Recommend: MODIFIES implies application. Remove redundant APPLIES edge when MODIFIES is present.

Prong 4 has been interpreted differently over time — some justices push for least restrictive means, the majority applies reasonable fit. The prong text itself is contested. Static prong sub-schema cannot represent this evolution. Consider adding a valid_from / valid_until window to individual prongs, or a contested: true flag with a notes field.

INCORPORATES is not in the original edge type list — added here ad hoc for the 14th Amendment incorporation relationship. Needs to be added to the formal schema as a ConstitutionalProvision-to-ConstitutionalProvision edge type.

Dissents and concurrences as doctrinal sources. The Brandenburg test was established as majority law in 1969, but its intellectual substance originated in Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). The schema has no mechanism to represent a non-majority opinion as a doctrinal source. ESTABLISHES edges run from Case nodes, and source_case_id on DoctrinalTest points to the case that made the test majority law — but this conflates 'legal establishment' with 'intellectual origin.' Proposed fix: add an optional intellectual_source field to DoctrinalTest (array of {case_id, opinion_type: majority|concurrence|dissent, author, note}). Alternatively, add an INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edge type between Case nodes with an opinion_type attribute.

schema_issues_found.location: majority_author: null on Brandenburg and Hess (Case nodes)
schema_issues_found.description: Per curiam opinions have no majority author, but the schema's majority_author field is a plain string with no formal null handling documented. More importantly, the schema has no way to record concurring opinions as doctrinal sources or to note that Black/Douglas's Brandenburg concurrences argued for a broader rule. The majority_author field needs: (1) explicit null semantics for per curiam; (2) an optional concurrences array with author and significance fields, parallel to the dissent pattern. This is related to Issue #7 but distinct — it affects the Case node schema, not just the edge layer.

Cross-slice node dependency gap. Several doctrines modeled in this slice originated in cases outside the slice's scope (public_concern_doctrine originates in Connick v. Myers, 1983; true_threats_doctrine as a category originates in R.A.V. v. St. Paul, 1992 and Virginia v. Black, 2003). The ESTABLISHES edges for these doctrines cannot be modeled without case nodes for Connick, R.A.V., and Virginia v. Black. The schema has no formal mechanism for a node to declare a dependency on a case node in another slice, or to represent a doctrine as 'partially established' pending a future slice addition. Proposed fix: add an established_by_pending field to Doctrine and DoctrinalTest nodes — an array of {case_id, slice_id, note} — documenting out-of-scope establishment cases. Alternatively, require stub Case nodes (id and citation only, status: stub) for any case referenced by an ESTABLISHES edge.

Single valid_from cannot represent layered doctrinal development. The true threats doctrine has two distinct temporal layers: (1) the category itself (unprotected since at least R.A.V., 1992); (2) the subjective mens rea requirement (Counterman, 2023). The node's valid_from can only capture one of these. This is a generalization of Commercial Speech Issue #5 (prong evolution) — here the problem is at the doctrine node level, not the prong level. A doctrine can have elements that became operative at different times. Proposed fix: add an elements_history array to Doctrine nodes, parallel to the prong valid_from/valid_until pattern in DoctrinalTest, allowing individual elements to carry their own validity windows.

APPLIES vs. MODIFIES tension on context-extension cases. Claiborne Hardware applied the Brandenburg test in a civil context for the first time. The schema rules say APPLIES is for cases that apply a doctrine 'without modifying it' — but extending a doctrine's applicability to a new legal context (civil vs. criminal) is a meaningful doctrinal development that APPLIES doesn't capture, while MODIFIES (which requires a direction attribute) seems too strong. 'Expands' on the incitement_doctrine MODIFIES edge captures the context extension, but the test node gets only APPLIES. Consider whether context-extension deserves its own direction value: 'extends' (applies doctrine in a new legal context without changing its prongs).

No RELATED_TO edges exist yet across either slice. The schema defines RELATED_TO as a cross-edge for non-hierarchical doctrinal relationships between Area nodes. Commercial speech and core political speech are sibling Area nodes under free_speech, but their relationship is more than just shared parentage — the public concern doctrine (core political speech) and the commercial speech doctrine share the same axis of analysis (what kind of speech is this?), and Sorrell (commercial speech) imports reasoning from the content-based restriction doctrine (core political speech). A RELATED_TO edge between commercial_speech and core_political_speech would be meaningful — but the schema gives no guidance on when RELATED_TO is appropriate vs. redundant vs. misleading. Needs editorial criteria.

No node type for legal instruments. Prior restraint doctrine applies differently depending on whether the government instrument is a licensing scheme, a judicial injunction, or an administrative censorship order. The schema has no way to represent instrument type as a structured concept — it can only appear in free-text notes. This matters for query accuracy: a walk looking for 'cases where prior restraint test was applied' will return Near (licensing scheme), Pentagon Papers (injunction sought by DOJ), and Madsen (content-neutral injunction) without distinguishing them, even though they trigger different constitutional standards. Proposed fix: add an InstrumentType enum to the schema — licensing_scheme, judicial_injunction, administrative_order, statute, regulation — as an optional attribute on edges between Case nodes and Doctrine/Test nodes.

Inadequate procedural posture representation. The schema's Case node status enum (good_law, overruled, limited, disputed) has no value for mooted, dismissed, cert_denied, or never_reviewed. US v. Progressive is mooted and was never reviewed on appeal — its district court holding has no precedential force — but the schema cannot represent this distinction. A mooted unreviewd district court holding and an affirmed SCOTUS decision are structurally identical in the current schema. Proposed fixes: (1) add status values: mooted, dismissed, cert_denied, per_curiam_affirmed; (2) add a precedential_weight field: binding, persuasive, none; (3) add an appellate_history array to Case nodes. The court field also needs expansion: currently the schema implies scotus as primary, but the graph needs district, circuit (by number), and state court levels.

Fractured concurrences as doctrinal source — extreme case. Pentagon Papers produced nine separate opinions with no majority. The per curiam said only that the government failed to meet its burden; the operational content of what would constitute a sufficient showing is assembled from six non-majority concurrences. This is a more extreme instance of Issue #7 (Holmes Abrams dissent / Brandeis Whitney concurrence as doctrinal sources). In Brandenburg, at least there was a majority opinion that stated the test. In Pentagon Papers there is no majority opinion at all — the test is a synthesis of concurrences. The schema's ESTABLISHES edge from near_v_minnesota to the test is accurate for the test's origin; the MODIFIES(clarifies) edge from Pentagon Papers captures something, but doesn't represent that Pentagon Papers is the primary operational source of the test's second prong. Proposed fix: extends Issue #7 fix — the intellectual_source or INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES mechanism must handle not just dissents/concurrences but cases where the per curiam itself provides no substantive doctrine.

Conditional governance — GOVERNED_BY cannot express instrument-type conditions. The prior restraint Area is governed by two different tests depending on the government instrument: the classic prior restraint test (Near/Pentagon Papers) applies to licensing schemes and government-sought injunctions; the Madsen test applies to content-neutral court injunctions against specific parties. The current schema cannot express this conditionality — both GOVERNED_BY edges exist but the graph cannot tell a query 'use Madsen when the instrument is a content-neutral injunction, use prior_restraint_test otherwise.' Proposed fix: add an optional condition attribute to GOVERNED_BY edges — a structured object with instrument_type, context, and note fields. This is related to Issue #14 (no instrument type node) and would be resolved together.

First use of RELATED_TO edge type — editorial criteria needed. This is the first RELATED_TO edge in the graph. The edge is justified (prior restraint and core political speech protect the same speech through complementary doctrinal mechanisms) but the schema provides no editorial criteria for when RELATED_TO is appropriate. Without criteria, contributors will use it inconsistently — either over-connecting everything related or under-using it. This is Issue #12 from the core political speech slice. The prior restraint / core political speech relationship provides a concrete example to anchor the criteria: RELATED_TO is appropriate when two Area nodes (1) share constitutional grounding, (2) have overlapping case law, and (3) operate as complementary rather than hierarchical doctrinal frameworks. Proposed spec addition: formalize these three criteria as the RELATED_TO editorial standard.

Meta-test problem: a DoctrinalTest that is a threshold precondition to another DoctrinalTest. The public_figure_classification_test is not applied to the merits of a defamation claim — it is applied first to determine which other test governs the claim. The schema has no way to represent a conditional relationship between DoctrinalTests: 'if public_figure_classification_test returns public_official or public_figure, then actual_malice_test applies; if it returns private_figure, then press_defamation_private_figure_doctrine applies.' This is a new pattern. Proposed fix: add a PRECONDITION_TO edge type between DoctrinalTest nodes, or add a conditional_test field to DoctrinalTest that specifies what governs when the test returns each classification. This is related to but distinct from the GOVERNED_BY condition attribute (which handles instrument-type conditionality, not test-sequencing conditionality).

Constitutional constraint on common law cause of action — new structural pattern. All prior slices modeled government restrictions on speech (statutes, court orders, administrative licensing). Sullivan constitutionalized a state common law cause of action (defamation) by imposing a constitutional standard on what private plaintiffs must prove. This is structurally different: the First Amendment is constraining a private lawsuit, not a government restriction. The schema's GOVERNS_BY and ESTABLISHES edges model doctrine that governs areas of law — but they don't capture the distinction between doctrine that restricts government action and doctrine that constrains private litigation. A research tool query for 'what governs this area' returns both types without distinguishing them, which is misleading for practice. Proposed fix: add a constraint_type field to Doctrine nodes: government_restriction | litigation_constraint | structural_rule.

Federal constitutional floor with state law superstructure. The private figure defamation doctrine sets a constitutional minimum (no strict liability, fault required) but explicitly leaves states free to set higher standards. The schema models federal constitutional law but this doctrine is incomplete without reference to the state law it constrains and the state variation it permits. The doctrine node correctly describes the federal floor but the graph has no way to represent the relationship between the federal floor and the state law superstructure. This is a Phase 2 boundary condition. Proposed fix: add a federalism_structure attribute to Doctrine nodes: federal_floor (federal minimum, state law operates above), federal_ceiling (federal maximum, state law operates below), federal_preemption (federal law displaces state law), federal_exclusive (state has no role). This is a relatively common constitutional law pattern and will recur in equal protection, due process, and other 14th Amendment doctrine.

Partially superseded plurality opinion — status enum inadequate. Butts is in an unusual status: Harlan's plurality holding (highly unreasonable conduct standard) was superseded by Gertz, but the case is not overruled because Warren's concurrence position (full actual malice for public figures) was adopted by Gertz. The case is simultaneously: (1) limited as to Harlan's plurality standard, and (2) the source of the rule adopted by Gertz via Warren's concurrence. The status: limited captures the first but not the second. There is no status value for 'partially superseded with surviving doctrinal contribution via concurrence.' Proposed fix: add a doctrinal_survival note field to Case nodes, and consider whether status needs a partially_superseded value distinct from limited.

Multi-factor standard without clean prongs — Doctrine vs. DoctrinalTest boundary. The opinion-fact distinction involves a contextual multi-factor analysis (is the statement objectively verifiable? does context signal opinion? is the statement capable of being proven true or false?) that courts apply as a totality-of-circumstances test rather than a sequential prong-by-prong analysis. It fits neither the Doctrine model (too structured) nor the DoctrinalTest model (lacks clean numbered prongs and sequential burden structure). Currently modeled as Doctrine. The promotion rule criteria suggest it might need its own treatment: it recurs across multiple areas (defamation, IIED, false light), has its own lineage, and is independently queried. Proposed fix: add a test_form field to DoctrinalTest: sequential_prongs (standard multi-prong test like Central Hudson or Brandenburg) | classification_framework (like public figure taxonomy) | totality_of_circumstances (like opinion-fact analysis) | balancing (like Mathews). This would allow DoctrinalTest to represent a wider range of structured legal analysis.

schema_issues_found.location: free_exercise_doctrine (Doctrine node) / employment_division_v_smith_1990 (Case node) / smith_test (DoctrinalTest node)
schema_issues_found.description: Intra-Court challenge to a precedent — dispute_source needed. The Smith rule is formally good law but at least four sitting justices have explicitly called for its reconsideration (Barrett, Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch in Fulton concurrences). The status: disputed flag is correct but conflates four distinct types of doctrinal instability: (1) circuit split on application, (2) academic consensus against the rule, (3) intra-Court challenge by sitting justices, and (4) legislative response (RFRA). These are meaningfully different for a research tool — a circuit split means lower courts are divided; an intra-Court challenge means the Supreme Court may revisit the rule at any time. Proposed fix: add dispute_source enum to status fields: circuit_split | academic | intra_court_challenge | legislative_response | post_overruling_transition. Multiple values should be permitted.

Threshold classification framework — second instance of Issue #19 meta-test problem. The belief/conduct distinction is a precondition to Free Exercise analysis, not a test applied to the merits. It determines whether the conduct is even subject to constitutional protection analysis. Like the public figure taxonomy (Issue #19), it is a gateway classification, not a merits test. The GOVERNED_BY edge is accurate but cannot represent that belief_conduct_distinction is a precondition to both sherbert_test and smith_test. The PRECONDITION_TO edge type proposed in Issue #19 would handle this. This second instance confirms the pattern is recurring and the fix is needed before Equal Protection (where tiered scrutiny is the same kind of threshold classification).

Constitutional test restored by statute — schema cannot represent this. The Sherbert compelling interest test was significantly curtailed by Smith (1990) as a constitutional matter, but Congress responded with RFRA (1993), which restored the Sherbert test as a statutory standard for federal laws. City of Boerne v. Flores (1997) struck down RFRA as applied to state laws. The graph models the constitutional Sherbert test. It cannot represent that: (1) the constitutional test was narrowed, (2) a statute restored the narrowed test for federal laws, and (3) that statute was partially invalidated, leaving the test operative only for federal laws. This is a three-layer constitutional-statutory interaction that requires the Phase 2 statute layer to model completely. Proposed interim fix: add a statutory_restoration note field to DoctrinalTest nodes documenting when a statute has restored a limited constitutional test.

Totality-of-circumstances analysis without clean prongs — second instance of Issue #23. The determination of whether a law is neutral and generally applicable draws on multiple factors (text, history, enforcement patterns, structure of exceptions) that courts apply holistically rather than sequentially. This is the same structural problem as the opinion-fact distinction (Issue #23). The neutrality_general_applicability_doctrine is modeled as a Doctrine rather than a DoctrinalTest because its factors lack the sequential burden structure of a formal test. The test_form field proposed in Issue #23 (totality_of_circumstances) would apply here. This second instance confirms the pattern is sufficiently common to warrant the field addition in v0.5.

Conditional GOVERNED_BY edges with overlapping validity windows — temporal ambiguity. Both the Sherbert test and the Smith test GOVERN the free_exercise Area simultaneously, with overlapping valid_from dates and no valid_until on either. This is accurate — both tests remain operative but in different circumstances — but creates graph ambiguity: a query for 'what test currently governs free exercise?' returns both tests without indicating that Smith governs neutral general laws and Sherbert governs everything else. The condition attribute on GOVERNED_BY edges (added in v0.4 for instrument-type conditionality) is used here to document the conditionality, but the condition attribute is optional and not queryable as a structured field. Proposed fix: promote condition.context to a structured enum rather than a free string, so queries can filter GOVERNED_BY edges by governing condition.

First overruled DoctrinalTest with valid_until — temporal query behavior. The Lemon test is the first DoctrinalTest in the graph with status: overruled and valid_until populated. The temporal model handles this correctly at the data level, but it surfaces a query design requirement: any query for 'what test currently governs this area' must filter by valid_until: null on GOVERNED_BY edges and status: active on DoctrinalTest nodes, or it will return the Lemon test as a governing test. The validator and pipeline must enforce this. In Neo4j, the canonical QT-1 query (what test governs this area) must include WHERE t.status <> 'overruled' AND e.valid_until IS NULL. This is the first instance demonstrating that temporal filtering is required at the query layer, not just the data layer.

Interpretive methodology modeled as Doctrine — inadequate node type. The historical practices and understandings standard is not a doctrine in the conventional sense — it is an interpretive methodology that tells courts how to analyze Establishment Clause questions rather than what the answer is. It lacks the structural features of a Doctrine (substantive rule that lawyers apply) and lacks the structured prongs of a DoctrinalTest. The schema has no node type for interpretive methodology. Currently modeled as Doctrine as the best available fit, but the test_form field proposed in Issue #23 (adding totality_of_circumstances, classification_framework values) should also add interpretive_methodology as a DoctrinalTest test_form value, or the schema should consider an InterpretiveFramework node type for a future version.

schema_issues_found.location: establishment_clause (Area node) / establishment_clause_doctrine (Doctrine node) / historical_practices_doctrine (Doctrine node)
schema_issues_found.description: Post-overruling transition — status: disputed insufficient. The Establishment Clause doctrine is in a state that the current schema cannot precisely represent: the old governing test (Lemon) is explicitly overruled; the new governing standard (historical practices) is stated but its operational content is actively being developed in lower courts; multiple circuits are applying the new standard with inconsistent results. This is categorically different from status: disputed (contested doctrine with defined content) or status: active (settled doctrine). It is a third state: the governing framework changed, the replacement exists, but the replacement has no settled application yet. The dispute_source enum proposed in Issue #24 (post_overruling_transition) would capture this. The schema needs a way to represent that a doctrine is in active post-overruling development, including: (1) which cases are overruled, (2) what the replacement is, (3) when the transition began, (4) what aspects are still being settled.

RELATED_TO relationship_type attribute needed. The Religion Clauses' relationship is qualitatively different from prior restraint/core political speech. Prior restraint and core political speech are complementary — they protect the same interest through different mechanisms. The Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses are structurally tensioned — they protect the same interest through opposing commands, and maximizing one necessarily limits the other. Laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause challenge; neutral laws risk Free Exercise challenge. This structural tension generates the RFRA problem and the accommodation doctrine. The RELATED_TO edge type needs an optional relationship_type attribute: complementary | structurally_tensioned | historically_linked | parallel_development. This would allow the research tool to explain not just that two areas are related but how.

Companion cases decided simultaneously — DISTINGUISHES edge semantics imperfect. Van Orden and McCreary County were decided on the same day by the same Court with opposite results. The DISTINGUISHES edge type was designed for later cases limiting earlier cases to their facts — a precedent/precedent-limiter temporal relationship. Using DISTINGUISHES for two simultaneously-decided companion cases is technically accurate (the cases do limit each other to their facts) but semantically odd: neither case distinguishes the other in the conventional sense of a later court limiting an earlier holding. Proposed fix: add a companion_case attribute to Case nodes (array of companion case IDs) for cases decided simultaneously on the same question. Alternatively, add COMPANION_TO as a new edge type for simultaneously-decided companion cases.

Dual constitutional grounding — Area GROUNDED_IN cannot distinguish sub-doctrine grounding. The freedom_of_association Area has a GROUNDED_IN edge to amendment_1 (covering expressive association). But intimate_association_doctrine within the same Area is grounded in amendment_14_section_1 (substantive due process), not amendment_1. The current model handles this by adding a Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge from intimate_association_doctrine to amendment_14_section_1 — the first use of this edge pattern in the graph. This is a valid v0.4 schema use (GROUNDED_IN is permitted from Doctrine to ConstitutionalProvision) but it surfaces an ambiguity: a consumer of the graph traversing from amendment_1 through GROUNDED_IN to freedom_of_association would not reach intimate_association_doctrine, and vice versa for amendment_14_section_1. The dual-grounding problem requires documentation in the spec: when a sub-doctrine has different constitutional grounding than its parent Area, use a Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge and note the distinction.

Porous boundary between compelled association and compelled speech — cases in two doctrinal lines simultaneously. Hurley is simultaneously a compelled speech case (core political speech doctrine — government cannot compel a speaker to carry a message they reject) and a compelled association case (freedom of association — parade organizers cannot be forced to include a group with a conflicting message). The case sits in both doctrinal lines. The schema models this correctly through edges to doctrine nodes in both areas, but there is no way to represent that a case is 'primarily' in one doctrinal line while 'secondarily' in another. The RELATED_TO edge between freedom_of_association and core_political_speech captures the relationship at the Area level, but the case-level overlap is not explicitly represented. Proposed fix: add a primary_doctrine field to Case nodes identifying the primary doctrinal area the case is associated with, and allow the edges to reflect secondary doctrinal associations.

Exacting scrutiny as a distinct standard — scrutiny level enum inadequate. The exacting scrutiny standard for disclosure requirements is distinct from both intermediate scrutiny and strict scrutiny — it requires narrow tailoring (like strict scrutiny) but applies to a less demanding category of governmental burden than content-based speech restrictions. The current scrutiny_level enum (strict | intermediate | rational_basis | balancing | per_se) does not have a value for exacting scrutiny. Currently mapped to strict as the closest fit, but this is imprecise — it will cause the research tool to treat exacting scrutiny cases the same as strict scrutiny cases, which is doctrinally incorrect. Proposed fix: add exacting to the scrutiny_level enum. This would also apply to other disclosure and association contexts.

Constitutional doctrine with significant statutory dimension — Phase 2 boundary. The Noerr-Pennington doctrine is rooted in the First Amendment Petition Clause, but its primary application is as an immunity from federal antitrust liability under the Sherman Act. The constitutional grounding is thin — courts have sometimes rested the doctrine on statutory interpretation of the Sherman Act rather than on the First Amendment directly. The schema models it as a constitutional petition doctrine because it has a constitutional dimension, but the fuller picture requires the statutory layer (Phase 2) to represent the Sherman Act relationship. This is the first instance of a doctrine that sits primarily at the intersection of constitutional law and a federal statute, where the constitutional grounding may be secondary to the statutory interpretation question.

Thin doctrinal area — Area node represents a real category but the doctrine is constitutionally underdeveloped. The Petition Clause has generated far less Supreme Court doctrine than the Speech, Press, Assembly, or Religion Clauses. Most petition activity is analyzed under speech doctrine, with the Petition Clause cited as additional support rather than the primary ground. The Area node is appropriate for taxonomic completeness but the graph's representation of petition doctrine is necessarily less rich than other 1A areas. This surfaces a schema question: should the graph represent the relative doctrinal development of different areas? Currently all Area nodes appear structurally equivalent regardless of how developed their doctrine is. A doctrinal_development_level field (nascent | developing | mature | contested) on Area nodes would allow the research tool to signal that some areas have richer doctrine than others.

Dual constitutional grounding through different legal theories — theory attribute needed on GROUNDED_IN edges. The intimate association doctrine is grounded in both amendment_1 (First Amendment implicit association) and amendment_14_section_1 (substantive due process liberty) through two different legal theories. Two GROUNDED_IN edges correctly capture the dual grounding, but the schema has no way to represent which legal theory each grounding reflects. A researcher looking at the graph cannot tell whether the amendment_14 GROUNDED_IN edge reflects incorporation (14th Amendment applying 1st Amendment to states) or substantive due process (14th Amendment's own liberty clause). Proposed fix: add an optional theory attribute to GROUNDED_IN edges: incorporation | substantive_due_process | structural_inference | textual | historical. This would make doctrinal theories navigable rather than implicit in the notes field.

Third instance of threshold classification meta-test problem (Issues #19, #25). The expressive association doctrine requires a threshold determination — does this organization engage in protected expressive activity? — before the merits analysis applies. This is the same structural pattern as the public figure taxonomy (Issue #19) and the belief/conduct distinction (Issue #25): a classification framework that preconditions which analysis follows. Three confirmed instances across three different doctrinal areas (press freedom, free exercise, association) establishes this as a systemic schema gap. The PRECONDITION_TO edge type proposed in Issue #19 is now confirmed as a genuine need. Adding it to the v0.5 spec is high priority before modeling Equal Protection, where the strict/intermediate/rational basis tiers are the most consequential instance of this pattern.

Anonymous speech sub-doctrine not yet modeled — floating connection. The compelled disclosure doctrine (NAACP v. Alabama, Americans for Prosperity) is substantively connected to the anonymous speech doctrine (Talley v. California, 1960; McIntyre v. Ohio Elections Commission, 1995) in the core political speech area. Both protect the ability to engage in expressive activity without government-compelled identification. The RELATED_TO edge from freedom_of_association to core_political_speech captures this relationship at the Area level, but the specific doctrinal connection (compelled_disclosure_doctrine ↔ anonymous_speech_doctrine) cannot be modeled because anonymous_speech is not yet a separate doctrine node in the core_political_speech slice. The core_political_speech slice should be expanded to include the anonymous speech doctrine as a sub-doctrine, at which point a RELATED_TO edge between compelled_disclosure_doctrine and the anonymous_speech_doctrine node would make the connection explicit.

Cross-field doctrinal placement — first doctrine with a primary application outside constitutional law. The Noerr-Pennington doctrine originated as a Sherman Act / antitrust doctrine and is primarily litigated in antitrust cases. Its constitutional grounding in the First Amendment Petition Clause was only made explicit in California Motor Transport (1972). The schema places it in freedom_of_petition (correct for constitutional law purposes) but it is equally a doctrine of antitrust law. When Phase 2 models antitrust doctrine, Noerr-Pennington will need to appear there as well. Proposed fix: add secondary_area_ids (string[]) field to Doctrine and DoctrinalTest nodes, parallel to secondary_parents on Area nodes. This would allow a doctrine to have a primary constitutional law home (area_id) and secondary homes in other fields (secondary_area_ids: ['antitrust']). This is the doctrinal equivalent of the DAG structure at the Area level — doctrines, like areas, can genuinely belong in multiple contexts.
